Classify these NIH grants by SCIENTIFIC CONTENT (not administrative mechanism).

These are F/K/T awards that were previously auto-classified as "training" based on activity code.
Now classify them by what the research actually IS:

Categories:
- basic_research: Understanding mechanisms, pathways, disease processes (knowledge output)
- biotools: Developing research tools, assays, methods, platforms (tool output)
- therapeutics: Developing drugs, treatments, therapies (treatment output)
- diagnostics: Developing clinical tests, biomarker panels (diagnostic test output)
- medical_device: Developing physical devices for patients (device output)
- digital_health: Patient-facing software, apps, clinical decision support
- other: Health services, behavioral interventions, epidemiology

application_id: 10891465
activity_code: DP1
title: A functional evolutionary genetic approach to combat viral infection
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Humans are under increasing threat from viral infection, particularly from epidemic and
pandemic viruses that spill over from other animals (e.g. Ebola virus, SARS coronavirus,
influenza A virus). This proposal will use evolutionary genetics to determine whether the
animals that harbor such viruses also possess species-specific antiviral proteins that could be
leveraged for therapeutic development.

application_id: 10911377
activity_code: R00
title: Measuring treatment response and residual disease in leukemia with personalized, sensitive, and quantitative genomic methods
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Better methods are urgently needed to measure therapeutic response and detect early relapse, especially in acute myeloid leukemia (AML), where 50 percent of patients that have a complete response to initial treatment go on to relapse within 2 years. Current methods for measuring minimal residual disease in AML are not sensitive enough, do not apply to all patients, and do not fully capture the genetic heterogeneity of the disease. In this project, we propose that utilizing personalized genetic mutations associated with a patient’s leukemia will result in more sensitive, quantitative, and general measurements of minimal residual disease, allowing for better evaluation of treatment efficacy and earlier detection of relapse, ultimately improving patient outcome in AML.

application_id: 10749930
activity_code: R34
title: Stepped Transition in Education Program for Emerging Adults with Autism: Pilot Effectiveness Trial
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA IN TUSCALOOSA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This study will inform our understanding of how to support emerging adults with autism spectrum disorder (ASD). We will use a theoretically informed and research-based transition support program developed for people with ASD and test how effective it is when delivered by community agencies. Results will provide information on the effectiveness of the program as well as on mechanisms of change and contextual factors that promote or impede implementation.

application_id: 10686569
activity_code: DP2
title: Studying the Regulatory Dynamics with Single-cell Multiomics
org_name: NEW YORK GENOME CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
The spatiotemporal dynamics of the cell’s complex gene regulatory machinery laid the foundations for diverse
biological processes including diseases and aging. Understanding the regulatory dynamics during the cell’s
state transition is essential for developing interventions while being hindered by the limitation that only static
snapshots of the cell’s regulatory states can be captured with current methods. Here we present a set of new
single-cell multiomics tools to measure the linked variables in shaping regulatory states of cells, and to
understand the functional impacts of regulatory dynamics during the aging of mammalian brain tissues.

application_id: 11062755
activity_code: DP2
title: How do neurons recognize self from non-self?
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Crucial to the wiring of neural circuits is the expression of sufficient Protocadherin (Pcdh) protein isoform
diversity such that essentially every neuron in the brain is uniquely barcoded at its surface and therefore
capable to self-recognize and self-avoid. This proposal aims to develop novel genomic, genetic and
biochemical approaches to test a new model of Pcdh RNA splicing mechanism. The findings from this work
will provide general principles of gene expression regulation of Pcdh genes and more broadly in vivo.

application_id: 10826264
activity_code: R36
title: Violence Exposure and Perpetration in Black and Minoritized Youth
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Violence towards others and suicide are typically studied as phenomena present in discrete groups; however, self/other-directed harm co-occurs to yield a higher risk of premature death34 and represents an understudied health crisis occurring in a uniquely vulnerable and often violence-exposed group.27,28,35-37 Despite decades of research we are still unable to reliably (1) identify this group on a large scale and (2) conceptualize the function of these symptoms to guide effective and scalable treatment planning. This study directly addresses these gaps by using a multidisciplinary, mixed-methods approach to help describe possible functions of self/other-directed harm.

application_id: 10950590
activity_code: DP2
title: Host-Dependent Mechanisms that Guide the Longitudinal Dynamic of Sites of SIV Integration
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative The integration of SIV into the host genome poses a significant barrier to achieving a successful HIV cure. This project seeks to elucidate the host-dependent mechanisms that govern preferences in HIV/SIV integration and the pivotal role SIV integration plays in disrupting host cellular functions; the latter ultimately leads to immune senescence, premature aging of the immune system, and, in some cases, tumorigenesis. By addressing these crucial aspects, my project aims to offer mechanistic insights that can lead to potential therapeutic targets for preventing integration or reversing host-immune dysfunctions triggered by SIV integration.

activity_code: DP2
title: Smart Steps: A context-aware adherence intervention to improve PrEP adherence among men who have sex with men (MSM) with substance use disorder
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Adherence to once-daily tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine is highly efficacious as pre-exposure
prophylaxis (PrEP) against HIV acquisition in men who have sex with men (MSM) with substance use
disorders, but adherence continues to be difficult to achieve. Digital pill systems (DPS) can provide real-time,
on-demand adherence data for MSM with substance use disorders, and serve as an innovative sensor input to
guide behavioral interventions aimed at boosting adherence. This Avenir award applies digital phenotyping and
machine learning algorithms to digital pill PrEP adherence data to develop SmartSteps, an antecedent
intervention strategy that anticipates and mitigates nonadherence before it occurs.

application_id: 10844852
activity_code: DP2
title: Developing Human Pluripotent Stem Cell Tools for Region-Specific Pain Circuits
org_name: TUFTS UNIVERSITY MEDFORD
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Neither conventional preclinical models using rodents nor simple human stem cell models using pain-sensing
nociceptors capture the complexity of human pain circuits. This hampers the discovery of solutions for
treatment of chronic pain, which may potentially target multiple cell types (including both neurons and
supporting glia), localized cell populations with distinct positional identities and/or the environment where these
pain networks reside. This proposal will develop advanced human stem cell tools that will establish region-
specific cells, circuits, and tissues, enhancing our fundamental understanding of human pain networks along
the body axis and accelerating the creation of accurate models that can be used for high throughput drug
screening.

application_id: 11445511
activity_code: DP1
title: From 3D genomes to neural connectomes: Higher-order chromatin mechanisms encoding long-term memory
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Public Health Relevance/Narrative
Emerging evidence indicates that epigenetic mechanisms are centrally linked to the development
of the mammalian central nervous system and go awry in neurological disorders. Here, we unravel
the functional link between long-range 3D genome folding patterns and synaptic plasticity during
the encoding of long-term memory in the mammalian brain. Because many key neurological
disorders are thought to be diseases of the synapse, successful completion of this work will
provide a foundation for future studies unraveling the role for misfolded genome topology on the
onset and progression of neurodevelopmental and neurodegenerative disorders.

application_id: 11164795
activity_code: DP1
title: Mechanical Clocks During Fetal Development
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: RELEVANCE
Fetal organ development is an intensely physical process that requires coordinated signaling between
developing tissues and their surrounding microenvironment to ensure maturation prior to birth. Defects in the
timing of morphogenesis or differentiation result in suboptimal organ function and are associated with birth
defects and chronic diseases later in life. Here we present a unique combination of experimental approaches
to test the innovative hypothesis of a “mechanical clock” that controls the rates of lung, kidney, and pancreas
development.

title: Finding Sleeping Beauty: T Cell Biosensors for Dormant Cancer Detection
org_name: GEORGIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE

application_id: 11111898
activity_code: DP2
title: HIV and Cocaine Drive Bone-Marrow Blood (BMB) Barrier Dysfunction and Altered Hematopoietic Stem Cell (HSC) Differentiation Leading to Cardiovascular Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Although anti-retroviral therapy has allowed people living with HIV (PLWH) to live longer, an accelerated form
of cardiovascular disease (CVD) has now become the second most common cause of non-AIDS related
mortality in PLWH in the US. Therefore, herein we propose that the premature CVD is a function of chronic
immune activation as a result of bone marrow-blood (BMB) barrier dysfunction and altered stem cell/immune
maturation which is further worsen by substance abuse. Overall, these studies could open the door for the
development of new therapeutics and treatments that reduce the elevated CVD risk and improve the lives of
PLWH.

application_id: 10910639
activity_code: DP2
title: Developing a CRISPR-free mammalian recombineering system
org_name: SCRIPPS RESEARCH INSTITUTE, THE
phr: Developing a CRISPR-free mammalian recombineering system
Project Narrative
The ability to manipulate large segments of DNA in mammalian cells is essential for research and therapeutics,
but we lack genome editing techniques capable of making kilobase-scale DNA changes without cytotoxic
double-strand breaks. In this proposal, I describe the development and use of a mammalian recombineering
platform, a CRISPR-free genome editing system that enables large DNA insertions, deletions, and
substitutions on the human genome without the introduction of double-strand breaks or DNA scars. Our
compact recombineering machinery can enable “all-in-one” single-AAV correction of multi-allelic diseases, thus
impacting fields from basic research to therapeutics.

application_id: 11171138
activity_code: DP2
title: Learn from the Errors at the Start of Life: Dissect Cellular Fitness Through Dynamics of Chromosomal Instability in Embryogenesis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative
Chromosomal instability (CIN), characterized by changes in chromosome number and structure, is often
considered a hallmark of tumor transformation and cancer, yet is also surprisingly prevalent in human embryos
from the first cleavage event. The remarkable resilience of embryos to these chaotic chromosomal events
highlights embryogenesis as a unique platform for understanding cell-type specific and context-dependent
cellular fitness. The aim of this proposal is to establish a unique model system using embryonic and
extraembryonic stem cells to understand the intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms involved in CIN during
embryogenesis, with the ultimate goal of modulating CIN tolerance for potential therapeutic advancements.

activity_code: DP2
title: High-resolution approaches to dissect the role of subcellular redox circuits in vivo
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative
Oxidative stress is the major metabolic barrier for cancer metastasis, but little is known about the adaptive
mechanisms necessary for metastasizing cancer cells to survive and colonize distant organs. We will use a
comprehensive and innovative optogenetic and subcellular redox modification platform in cell lines and mouse
models to understand how individual organelles allow cancer cells to survive oxidative stress. The data
generated from this proposal will provide fundamental insights into redox biology that may be applicable to a
broad range of diseases impacted by redox alterations.

application_id: 10687584
activity_code: DP2
title: A physiologically-focused approach to training multi-modality AI algorithms in medicine
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The proposed research is relevant to public health because it will develop a new and broadly-applicable platform
to develop multi-modal AI algorithms that achieve higher-level physiologically-focused problem solving across
all medical specialties. This would not only improve AI performance overall, but also greatly expands the scope
of possible tasks that AI algorithms can accomplish in medicine, while also supporting AI-enabled data-driven
discovery of physiology and mechanism. This proposal’s fundamental physiologically-focused innovation in AI
algorithm architecture is expected to shift the paradigm by which medical AI is developed and integrates
alongside clinicians into the clinical work flow.

application_id: 10687660
activity_code: DP2
title: Optogenetic engineering of tumor topography in native tissue environments
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative: The anatomic site of tumor formation within a tissue is a major contributor to inter-tumoral
heterogeneity. However, understanding the mechanistic link between tumor topography and disease progression
has been limited by the lack of genetic models that can recapitulate the spatial and phenotypic patterns of human
disease. Thus, developing optogenetic based models to enable precise spatiotemporal in vivo control of tumor
formation could overcome these limitations and facilitate future mechanistic studies into how local tissue
environments influence tumor heterogeneity and enable development of targeted therapies.

title: Reliable AI for Medical Image Reconstruction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Reliable AI for medical image reconstruction
Project Narrative
 Modern Arti cial Intelligence (AI) algorithms have immense potential to revolutionize healthcare, however
their widespread use/adaptation has so far been stymied due lack of reliability and trustworthiness. This project
develops novel reliable AI algorithms for Magnetic Resonance (MR) imaging that can help overcome various
physical limitations of MR machines, leading to devices that are signi cantly faster, cheaper and more accurate.
This can in turn signi cantly enhance the quality of medical diagnostics and patient comfort while reducing the
cost and increasing widespread access to this important medical imaging modality.

org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The project aims at identifying and characterizing human T cell genes/molecules that contribute to suppressing effective antitumor T cell responses by utilizing culturable human T cells and cancer cell lines. application_id: 11173385
activity_code: DP2
title: Synthetic Cell State Engineering for Primary Human Cellular Therapies
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Human cells are capable of a wonderous diversity of functions in both health and disease, and the various cell
states that are naturally accessible can be therapeutically manipulated by drugs, biologics, and increasingly by
genetic medicines. But the set of desirable and therapeutically useful functions of human cells may not be
confined only to naturally accessible cell states. The proposed research uses a new generalized and arbitrarily
combinatorial method for making complex genetic modifications in primary human cells to explore how non-
evolved therapeutically useful human cellular states and functions can be generated by constructing new, non-
evolved human genes, initially through applications to improve human immune cell function against cancers.

title: Monitoring neuronal activity with a blood test - Released Markers of Activity (RMA)
org_name: RICE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative
Blood tests are among the most common clinical tools due to their low cost, simplicity, and ability to observe
many markers at once. However, currently blood tests can only monitor a fraction of physiological processes
that happen to have a serum marker. Here, we are developing synthetic markers that can exit from the brain

application_id: 10687634
activity_code: DP2
title: Defining and Exploiting the Circadian Clocks in Malaria Parasites
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: Project Narrative
Malaria’s immense public health burden is undisputable with numbers of deaths increasing since 2020, however
the mechanism for its characteristic rhythmic fevers remains poorly understood. This proposal combines a novel
approach with innovative methods to develop fundamental new knowledge about the molecular mechanism of
parasite clocks. An improved understanding of parasite biology and host-parasite-vector interactions could be
transformational and change how and when medication is given to treat these parasitic diseases in humans.

application_id: 10910663
activity_code: DP2
title: Towards a comprehensive understanding of the neurotoxic spread of α-synuclein from gut to brain in Parkinson's disease
org_name: AUGUSTA UNIVERSITY
phr: Parkinson’s disease is a devastating neurodegenerative disorder for which there is currently no cure. Extensive
evidence suggests that the disease may originate in the gastrointestinal tract; however, there is an urgent need
for animal models that allow for rapid, large-scale testing to uncover novel gut-to-brain mechanisms of disease.
In the proposed study, we will generate a suite of new gut-to-brain disease models using the small nematode
worm, C. elegans, and with a combination of high-throughput screening and single cell RNA-sequencing
analyses, we will provide the most comprehensive investigation to date of the mechanisms underlying neurotoxic
disease spread from the gut, with our results bearing significant implications for early therapeutic interventions
in Parkinson’s disease.

I propose to develop technologies for measuring input-output operations of neurons and their circuits, a novel

application_id: 10687647
activity_code: DP2
title: Imaging Spatiotemporal Regulation of Acetyl-CoA
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Cellular metabolism is highly compartmentalized, and subcellular localization of metabolites determines
use. A central metabolite, acetyl-CoA, is involved in many biochemical reactions and is known to be produced
and used in a highly compartmentalized manner, but the real-time spatiotemporal dynamics of acetyl-CoA
have remained elusive. In this proposal, we aim to illuminate the dynamic, subcellular nature of acetyl-CoA by
engineering an acetyl-CoA biosensor which can be used to probe acetyl-CoA with high spatiotemporal
resolution and ultimately advance NIGMS mission to develop new technologies which will enhance our
understanding of mechanisms and processes that underlie living organisms.

application_id: 10909534
activity_code: DP2
title: Novel extremophile-inspired radioprotectants
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: Project Narrative
 Most cancer patients will undergo radiation therapy at some point in their disease course, a treatment that
frequently causes collateral injury to healthy tissue, leading to severe complications or even treatment discon-
tinuation. Current methods of preventing this injury are wrought with challenges: 1) the inability to sustain radio-
protection and maintain tumor cytotoxic effects simultaneously, and 2) side effects. In this proposal, we aim to
develop a novel RNA strategy that delivers self-amplifying-RNAs coding for extremophile properties to tissues
at risk for radiation-induced normal tissue injury.

title: Illuminating the Molecular Logic of Mammalian Synaptic Circuit Assembly
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 The mammalian brain is composed of trillions of diverse types of synaptic connections that form precise
and stereotyped synaptic circuits essential for brain functions. Despite the importance of synaptic circuit

title: A Paradigm Shift in Health Behavior Change: Understanding When and How Social Comparison Supports Healthy Behavior
org_name: ROWAN UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative
Prompts to evaluate ourselves relative to others (i.e., social comparison) are increasingly engineered into
our daily lives to promote specific behaviors, including in digital health technologies and health behavior
change interventions, but we do not yet understand how social comparison affects health behavior –
including its demonstrated negative effects. The proposed DP2 New Innovator Award will address the
critical and overlooked opportunity to understand how social comparison affects health behavior in the
natural environment, by introducing a new paradigm for research in this area that is guided by a novel,

application_id: 10687565
activity_code: DP2
title: Massively scalable 3D electrophysiology and two-photon imaging in freely-moving animals
org_name: PURDUE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Performing high-density electrophysiology and concomitant two-photon calcium imaging is - at present- not
feasible in awake behaving animals. In this proposal we introduce NET-2P – a platform that facilitates high-
density electrophysiology across 3D volume alongside two-photon calcium imaging in freely-moving mice. We
propose to unravel how spontaneous electrical travelling waves shape underlying cellular ensembles during
sensory discrimination, and how ensembles representing learned associations are strengthened during sleep.

application_id: 10909709
activity_code: DP2
title: Untangling the influence of distinct sources of somatosensory feedback on the neural dynamics of dexterous movement control
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
To move successfully, we must be able to sense our bodies and our environment. While
theoretical work outlines computations that ought to take place for successful integration of
somatosensation into movement, how population neural activity in somatosensory and motor
nodes in the brain interact to accomplish this integration remains unclear. This proposal uses
multi-area electrophysiology to monitor functional interactions in distributed sensorimotor nodes
during the acquisition and execution of a dexterous manipulation task, mapping theoretical
computations onto the circuits and cross-area population dynamics in the sensorimotor network.

application_id: 10910692
activity_code: DP2
title: Deep-tissue targeted molecular imaging with a palette of NIR-II emissive DNA-stabilized nanoclusters
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project narrative
Fluorescence imaging is a crucial tool for monitoring biomolecular processes at high spatial
resolutions and in real time but has limited utility for deep tissue imaging because the few available
fluorophores in the NIR-II tissue transparency window (1,000 to 1,700 nm) suffer from challenges
such as low brightness, large size, and toxicity. This proposed work pioneers a new approach to
develop small, bright, tunable, and biocompatible NIR-II emitters for targeted in vivo molecular
imaging, by exploiting a new class of NIR-emissive DNA-stabilized silver nanoclusters. We
envision that these new NIR-II fluorescent probes will transform the field of deep tissue
fluorescence imaging, with applications ranging from cancer research to vascular imaging.

org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: 8. Project Narrative Overweight and obesity affect a large majority of Americans, increasing their risk for multiple chronic illnesses. It is recommended that physicians provide weight management counseling (WMC) to patients having overweight or obesity, but physicians have limited WMC training. application_id: 11169974
activity_code: DP5
title: Advancing CAR T cell therapy for solid tumors
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
CAR T cell therapy for solid tumors remains challenging in part due to inhibitory tumor microenvironments (TME)
that are seen even at the earliest stages of cancer. We hypothesize that targeting the TME can mitigate these
immunosuppressive effects and enhance CAR T cell killing of lung tumor. Our research strategy will develop a
new immunocompetent mouse model that faithfully recapitulates the immunosuppressive TME to test novel
therapeutic approaches to overcome these suppressive effects to ultimately advance CAR T cell therapy for
solid tumors.

application_id: 11171376
activity_code: DP5
title: Discovering T cell proteome turnover dynamics to overcome the solid tumor microenvironment
org_name: UNIV OF ARKANSAS FOR MED SCIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This study investigates T cell proteome turnover dynamics and the role it plays in tumor infiltrating T cell
persistence. Understanding how T cell protein turnover rates are optimized to balance energy-saving stability
and dynamic flexibility will provide conceptual advances in our understanding of the immune response to cancer.
The identified mechanisms which endow T cells with superior adaptability will be applied to models of cellular
therapy and utilized in clinical immune monitoring.

application_id: 10897186
activity_code: DP1
title: Sonogenetic Remote Control of Cellular Function
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: NARRATIVE To study biological systems in their critical in vivo context and develop effective cell-based therapies, we need new methods to manipulate the function of cells deep inside living animals. In this project we will develop a technology that enables cellular remote control with ultrasound – a form of energy that can be targeted non- invasively to deep tissues with sub-millimeter spatial precision. This technology will use genetically encoded “acoustic antennae” to convert ultrasound into local mechanical force and apply it to engineered mechanosensitive receptors, triggering desired cellular pathways for remote-control of neural activity and immunotherapy.

org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: NARRATIVE
To study biological systems in their critical in vivo context and develop effective cell-based therapies, we need
new methods to manipulate the function of cells deep inside living animals. In this project we will develop a
technology that enables cellular remote control with ultrasound – a form of energy that can be targeted non-
invasively to deep tissues with sub-millimeter spatial precision. This technology will use genetically encoded
“acoustic antennae” to convert ultrasound into local mechanical force and apply it to engineered
mechanosensitive receptors, triggering desired cellular pathways for remote-control of neural activity and
immunotherapy.

activity_code: DP1
title: Bridging cellular and systems neuroscience: synaptic dynamics underlying behavior
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
 Synaptic connections between neurons form the basis of network connectivity in the brain, and synaptic
plasticity is widely believed to underlie adaptive behavior and learning. However, the dynamic range and
modulation of synaptic strength in vivo is essentially unknown, raising a major challenge to our understanding
of nervous system function. Here, we combine conceptual and methodological innovations to drive a
pioneering exploration of the relationship between synaptic transmission and ongoing behavior.

application_id: 10911260
activity_code: DP1
title: Individual differences through self-reinforcement of suboptimal strategies
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Understanding the origin of individual differences in behavior is not only a question of deep fundamental interest, but is also central to understanding the variable penetrance of neuropsychiatric disease. We propose and intend to test a new hypothesis regarding the origin of individual differences: that they arise in part from small differences in initial conditions caused to be amplified by positive feedback, such that different individuals from different reward associations within the same environment. This results from the fact that the world is high-dimensional, in that there are almost always multiple possible actions or events that could be attributed to reward.

application_id: 10924034
activity_code: DP1
title: Ghost in the Machine: Melding Brain, Computer and Behavior
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: The goal of this project is to develop a revolutionary, new generation of implantable
neurodevices that will communicate with, learn from, and teach their human hosts to better
treat disease. Current implantable devices are blind to human actions, thoughts and behavior,
which limits their effectiveness. State of the art computer techniques that can measure

title: Mechanical Clocks During Fetal Development
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: RELEVANCE Fetal organ development is an intensely physical process that requires coordinated signaling between developing tissues and their surrounding microenvironment to ensure maturation prior to birth. Defects in the timing of morphogenesis or differentiation result in suboptimal organ function and are associated with birth defects and chronic diseases later in life. Here we present a unique combination of experimental approaches to test the innovative hypothesis of a “mechanical clock” that controls the rates of lung, kidney, and pancreas development.

title: Decoding the regulation of protein folding by synonymous codon usage
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NOTRE DAME
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Synonymous codon substitutions are typically regarded as “silent” and “neutral.” Yet, synonymous mutations can significantly alter the folding mechanism of the encoded protein, leading to adverse effects on cell fitness. title: Using microfluidics to realize patient-specific anti-cancer immunotherapies
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative T cells recognize diseased cells via molecular interactions between T cell receptors (TCRs) and peptides presented by MHC molecules (pMHCs), and an improved ability to predict which pMHC/TCR pairs drive strong T cell responses could improve our ability to engineer personalized anti-cancer therapies without dangerous side effects. T cell crawling during immunosurveillance applies biomechanical forces to pMHC/TCR complexes that are crucial for sensitive and specific recognition, with activating pMHC/TCR pairs forming ‘catch’ bonds that strengthen under force, but current screening methods test pMHC/TCR binding in the absence of force and thus fail to predict potent agonists in vivo. title: Exploring hidden determinants of splicing with genome-targeted proximity labeling
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
RNA splicing is an essential and typically accurate process that is responsible for generating mature messenger
transcripts. However, many human diseases such as cancer are associated with disruptions to global splicing
homeostasis and the underlying causes remain largely unknown. This proposal aims to examine the connection
between epigenetics and splicing to uncover their relationship in both healthy and unhealthy contexts and identify
new opportunities for therapeutic intervention.

application_id: 11014641
activity_code: DP2
title: Rethinking the "quiet" nature of apoptotic cell clearance.
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Clearance of apoptotic cells, termed ‘efferocytosis’, is thought to be essential for preventing autoimmunity and
inflammatory disease, yet these illnesses rarely present with defects in ability to clear apoptotic cells. Here, we
propose that autoimmunity and inflammatory disease arise because efferocytosis is inherently dangerous, and
macrophages (the body’s primary phagocyte) require mechanisms to detect and respond to dangerous engulfed
material. We will identify and characterize these mechanisms, termed rapid response circuits, that are used for
‘healthy’ efferocytosis, with the goal of developing a framework for understanding how failure to appropriately
digest apoptotic cells contributes to autoimmunity and inflammatory disease.

activity_code: DP1
title: Polyclonal Bi-Specific Vectored ImmunoTherapy to Functionally Cure HIV Infection
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative  The ability of HIV to escape from sub-optimal dosing of existing antiretroviral drugs and avoid neutralization by the immune system are two of the greatest challenges facing the development of effective strategies to control and eliminate infection. title: Life history of the menstruating uterus
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The goal of this work is to understand how the human uterine lining (endometrium) repairs itself each month

application_id: 11384395
activity_code: DP2
title: Deciphering Principles of Human Embryonic Patterning in Development and Disease
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project narrative
This study will combine cutting-edge technologies to design a new experimental platform that recapitulates key
embryological events outside of their natural contexts using human pluripotent stem cells (hPSCs). With this
exciting new technology, we will uncover the molecular and cellular events that are required for normal human
embryonic development and how these are influenced by cellular metabolism and, potentially, the in utero
environment. Upon completion, this proposal offers a unique opportunity to gain much-needed knowledge to
uncover the embryonic origin of human developmental diseases and to better engineer functional human cell
types for cellular therapies and disease modeling.

application_id: 11407916
activity_code: DP2
title: Dissecting the enhancer logic governing immune cell fate decisions
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
T cell dysfunction is a hallmark of cancer and chronic viral infections and targeting dysfunction-associated
molecules has had a major clinical impact in cancer immunotherapy. Here, we propose using CRISPR
epigenome editing and enhancer knock-in assays to identify exactly which regulatory interactions are
necessary and sufficient to induce dysfunction in specific tumor microenvironments. Identifying regulatory
mechanisms that govern when, where and how T cell dysfunction develops will be critical to improved
outcomes in tumor immunity and chronic viral infection.

title: Sequence models of genome regulatory architecture in 3D
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative The genomic sequence directs its 3D organization and gene regulation which is essential for the normal development and function of the human body. Genome variations across individuals may disrupt structural and gene regulations and contribute to susceptibility to diseases and response to therapeutics, yet the consequences of genome variations are generally unknown. Computational models proposed in this project will decode the structural and transcriptional impact of any genome sequence and open up new possibilities in assessing the impacts of genome variants and understanding their roles in disease genetics.

title: Deciphering multi-scale differentiation and patterning cues driving whole craniofacial joint regeneration

application_id: 11145320
activity_code: DP2
title: Harnessing sensory food circuits to influence feeding behavior
org_name: MONELL CHEMICAL SENSES CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
In our modern food environment, we are constantly exposed to compelling food cues – such as the smell and
taste of highly palatable foods – that interact with our physiology to promote feeding and weight gain. Here, we
take a unique approach to understanding obesity by (1) evaluating the power of taste, smell, and nutrient neural
circuits in modifying eating behavior, and (2) uncovering how sensory and nutritive information is integrated in
the brains of awake behaving mice to predict future weight gain. Successful completion of this proposal will
transform our understanding of how our brain and environment interact to promote overeating and obesity.

application_id: 11062814
activity_code: DP2
title: AI-assisted Imaging and Prediction of Cardiac Arrhythmia Origins using 4D Ultrasound
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Despite significant progress in the field of biomedical imaging, imaging of life-threatening heart rhythm disorders,
such as atrial fibrillation or ventricular tachycardia, remains a major technological and scientific challenge. In this
project, the applicant aims to develop a novel imaging technique capable of providing transmural 4D (time-
resolved 3D) visualizations of cardiac arrhythmias non-invasively, by predicting electrophysiological phenomena
at unprecedented resolutions from the heart’s deformations using 4D ultrasound and artificial intelligence (AI). If
successful, the new imaging technique could significantly advance the understanding of heart rhythm disorders,
greatly advance their diagnosis and be used to guide therapy more effectively.

title: Discovering mechanisms of tissue-resident immune aging
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Tissue-resident immune cells are responsible for coordinating adaptive stress responses and limiting
inflammation. In adipose tissue, age-associated perturbation of the resident immune compartment coincides with
the accumulation of inflammation and impaired adaptation to cold and fasting. Here we will leverage new
technology to uncover intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms of adipose-resident immune dysregulation to restore
immune function during aging and preserve adipose tissue health.

application_id: 10472787
activity_code: DP2
title: Closing the gap between structural biology and translational science for amyloid diseases
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The resolution revolution of cryo-electron microscopy has revealed that a given amyloid precursor can form
structurally different assemblies, or polymorphs, that seem to associate with distinct disease. However, the
association of these specific assemblies with the biology and pathology of an amyloid disease is still unclear. I
aim to establish a multidisciplinary high-risk program to obtain atomic information about amyloid fibrils using
cryo-electron microscopy, to analyze their pathological role in a co-culture cell system to be developed in my
laboratory, and to design structure-specific tools to use in the clinic.

application_id: 11094859
activity_code: DP1
title: Engineering Bacteriophage T4 as a Targeted Gene Therapy Drug for in vivo HIV Cure
org_name: CATHOLIC UNIVERSITY OF AMERICA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
More than 40 million people across the globe are now living with HIV-1, of which the
substance users constitute a disproportionate and “key population”, yet there is no cure or
vaccine on the horizon. Using our recently developed bacteriophage T4 artificial virus vector
technology, this proposal will establish a novel and affordable in vivo HIV cure platform that
can be easily administered to substance users through one or two infusions. The HIV cure
“drug” will target stem cells and introduce genetic modifications that confer resistance to HIV
infection and then, by repopulating the body with HIV-resistant blood cells including resistant
T cells, it replaces the HIV reservoir and leads to functional HIV cure.

application_id: 11062824
activity_code: DP2
title: Circulatronics: A New Paradigm for Biomedical Implants
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Modified Public Health Relevance Section
Bioelectronic implants provide powerful tools for healthcare as well as basic research but suffer from the major disadvantage that their installation requires invasive surgery cutting through skin and flesh leading to pain, tissue damage and other health related risks. Here we develop a technology in which ultra-miniaturized bioelectronic devices can get implanted in diseased regions and provide localized sensing and treatment without the need for surgery. This can drastically reduce invasiveness and open up new avenues for diagnostics, therapeutics and fundamental research.

application_id: 11009682
activity_code: DP2
title: Fusing motor neuroscience and artificial intelligence to create next-generation neural prostheses.
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: PUBLIC HEALTH STATEMENT
Brain-machine interfaces (BMIs) provide a pathway to restore function for people who are
paralyzed, such as those with spinal cord injury or amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. This proposal
aims to substantially increase the performance and robustness of BMIs by combining new
developments from the field of artificial intelligence with recent advances in our understanding of
brain function, with the goal of improving quality of life for people with severe movement
disabilities.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative
The proposed research will establish a revolutionary and widely applicable experimental strategy with the scale
and throughput to understand how diverse gene mutations found in cancer cooperate to influence aggressive

cancer and accelerate the generation of prostate cancer models that mirror the genetic complexity of the
disease.

application_id: 11381552
activity_code: DP2
title: A new framework for self-adaptive artificial intelligence to personalize assistance for patients using robotic exoskeletons and prostheses
org_name: GEORGIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: This research aims to innovate a new artificial intelligence (AI) framework for self-adapting wearable robotic control to
personalize assistance to a patient’s unique walking pattern. The aim is to improve the way a robotic exoskeleton or
prosthesis responds to a unique patient by providing a human-in-the-loop control framework for seamless self-learning of
optimal assistance over dynamic, unstructured locomotion tasks. These results have the potential to enhance quality of life
for patient populations such as those with stroke or amputation by improving stability and gait quality, increasing
preferred walking speed and improving independent community ambulation.

application_id: 10866501
activity_code: K00
title: Developing Quantitative Methods to Reveal Neural Circuit Dynamics
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative An understanding of how neural circuitry produces behavior would provide important insights into the basis of brain disorders; however, the tools are lacking. The nematode Caenorhabditis elegans gives us the opportunity to study how an entire nervous system functions together to extract ecologically-relevant information about chemical stimuli, and to couch these analyses in the known structural connectivity between neurons. This project will establish a theoretical framework for understanding fine- grained, yet holistic neural processing in many neural systems using graph theory and information theory, and will enable us to understand how neural circuits work in health.

application_id: 11062833
activity_code: DP2
title: Trillion cell culture to fuel organ biofabrication
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Progress towards the goal of bioprinting organs for therapeutic purposes has been greatly slowed by our inability
to affordably grow, and efficiently differentiate, the large numbers of stem cells required. Here, we propose novel
methods to dramatically reduce the costs of large-scale cultures and to co-differentiate multiple cell types in a
single bioreactor to form synthetic cardiac organoids for 3D bioprinting human-scale ventricle models. These
advances will significantly accelerate our ability to perform upcoming decades of research and translational
development required to reach the goal of on-demand autologous organs for transplantation.

application_id: 11014616
activity_code: DP2
title: Towards fully reconstituting mammalian transcription in a test tube
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
Genomes must be compacted and organized to fit within the confines of the cell but at the same
time remain accessible so that information within the DNA can be read by enzymes that are
responsible for expressing genes that determine cell identity. When the coordination between
compaction and accessibility is disrupted, cancer and developmental disorders emerge. Here I
will develop novel in vitro assays to directly investigate how genome compaction and
accessibility are coordinated to regulate gene expression with the long-term goal of
understanding how disease mutations disrupt this coupling.

The living cell is composed of highly dynamic organelles, but the events occurring inside organelles at the
intra-organelle level are still not well understood. Using advanced super-resolution microscopy, this proposal
will provide new insights into intra-organelle events within organelles, such as the dynamics of mtDNA, mtRNA
and mitochondrial chaperones inside the mitochondrial matrix, and shed light on how these dynamics inform
mitochondrial and cellular function, neuronal homeostasis and the pathways underlying multiple mitochondrial
diseases which have been linked to neurodegeneration. Elucidating the role and regulation of intra-organelle
events will ultimately advance our understanding of the dynamic cellular architecture of the living cell and

One goal of this Diversity supplement is to investigate the relative neurotoxicity of TRAP-derived PM vs. gases, the role of microglia in mediating the neurotoxic effects of TRAP, and the influence of sex and genotype on microglial response to TRAP. application_id: 11014208
activity_code: DP2
title: A New Approach to Study Mechanically Activated Ion Channels
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Mechanotransduction, the conversion of physiological force into biological signals, is crucial for many
physiological processes such as touch or pain sensation, blood pressure regulation, and muscle contraction.
The importance of mechanotransduction is underscored by its role in disorders such as chronic pain,
atherosclerosis, and muscular dystrophies. Mechanotransduction is driven by ion channels activated when force
is applied to cell membranes, but the lack of a highly precise method for MA channel activation in vitro has
hampered progress in the field. This proposal uses an innovative and tractable technique to activate and assay
MA ion channels that will enable their deep biophysical and pharmacological characterization, which will be vital

title: Directed Evolution of Cell-Type Specific On-Demand Signaling Control Systems
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: Project Narrative Receptors are specialized proteins in our bodies that we use to experience the world – from our sense of smell and taste to our perception of pain and our ability to learn new information. We do not possess the tools necessary to determine how and under what conditions each of the hundreds of unique receptors in our bodies promote health or disease. In this project I will apply a revolutionary new method I have developed in protein engineering to create the tools necessary to control these receptors and study how they make us who we are.

title: A single cell pooling framework for deciphering the regulatory wiring of allergy in pathophysiologic contexts
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Allergic disorders including food allergy, hay fever, eczema, and asthma, affect approximately
25% of people in industrialized countries and can cause life threatening reactions. Genetic
differences among individuals account for about half of the risk of allergy; identifying the
underlaying genes and biological pathways could suggest new strategies to treat and prevent
allergy. Whereas the genetics of allergy has largely been studied in cell lines, we will pioneer an
approach to study allergy at the single cell level in heterogenous human pathology specimens
encompassing diverse clinical subtypes.

title: Diversity Supplement to R01GM150698
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Martell project narrative Technologies that enable the identification of which proteins reside in specific locations inside cells are critical for elucidating biological processes and disease states, including neurodegeneration, pathogenic infection, and cancer. One promising strategy to achieve this goal is proximity labeling (PL), but in order to enhance the sensitivity and specificity of proximal protein detection, improved PL catalysts are needed. We propose that hybrid biological-abiotic PL catalysts developed in our laboratory will enhance both sensitivity and specificity in spatially- resolved proteomic mapping, and we are employing these catalysts in neurons to benchmark their performance against existing methods and establish their broad utility as research tools.

application_id: 11134806
activity_code: DP2
title: Defining and Exploiting the Neuronal Identity of Cancers
org_name: TUFTS UNIVERSITY MEDFORD
phr: Project Narrative
The proposed work seeks to define and exploit the neuronal identity of solid tumors to prevent cancer
metastasis, the leading cause of death in cancer patients. Recently, the presence of peripheral nerves in solid
tumors and the upregulation of neuronal genes within tumor cells themselves were both reported be
associated with poor outcome in cancer. We will use cell biology, engineering and clinically translatable
approaches to understand how neoneurogenesis occurs in tumors, how the neural identity of tumors impacts
the systemic regulation of metastasis and how we can leverage this information for non-invasive monitoring
and treatment of metastatic disease.

title: Improving drug design to eliminate side effects: From computational to animal models
org_name: PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY, THE
phr: Project Narrative
By understanding the molecular basis by which drugs promote unintended side effects, we can reduce them and
greatly increase the potential to design effective drugs. In this work, we will develop this molecular understanding
along with predictive tools that allow ligands to be screened for their potential to promote undesired interactions

application_id: 11212346
activity_code: DP2
title: Understanding and restoring speech production using an intracortical brain-computer interface
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
People who lose the ability to speak due to neurological conditions including ALS or stroke have an urgent and unmet
need for therapies that would restore their ability to speak. This proposal is developing a new way to help such patients
using an implanted electronic medical device called a ‘brain-computer interface’ which measures the person’s brain
activity with very high precision as they try to talk, and then outputs their intended words. Bypassing the injured parts
of the nervous system this way requires learning to decipher how individual brain cells in the motor cortex and superior
temporal gyrus – two areas of the brain involved in speaking – work together as a network to produce speech.

phr: Project Narrative
Digestive system diseases are causally associated with a wide variety of environmental factors including the
diet composition, eating and sleeping schedules and the gut microbes, yet the molecular basis remains un-

title: New classes of optogenetic and chemogenetic tools with a feedback control
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: New classes of optogenetic and chemogenetic tools with a feedback control
Project Narrative
 Neurological disorders, such as epilepsy and schizophrenia, are very debilitating by showing
unpredicted episodes of irregular neuronal activities. We propose to develop new optogenetic tools with
tunable temporal control and new chemogenetic tools with a feedback control to precisely silence the

therapeutic treatment.

application_id: 11014645
activity_code: DP2
title: Post-translational modification proteomics in 4D: Chemoenzymatic tools to map the dynamic spatial organization of eukaryotic signaling pathways
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Project narrative
Post-translational modifications to proteins are a key biological regulatory mechanism and are often dysregulated
in human disease. However, identification of post-translational modifications has far outpaced assignment of
their biological functions, an endeavor that requires detailed information about where and when these
modifications occur within the cell. The proposed research will develop new tools for spatiotemporally resolved
mapping of these modifications on a proteome-wide scale that will advance our understanding of fundamental
biological mechanisms, potentially suggesting new approaches for the diagnosis and treatment of human
disease.

title: Regulation of Proinflammatory Cytokine Responses by a Caspase-8-N4BP1 Axis
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: Project Narrative
Inflammation is a hallmark of many diseases, including cancer, autoinflammatory disorders, immunodeficiencies,
and neurodegeneration; thus, understanding the molecular mechanisms regulating inflammation is critical to
identifying opportunities for therapeutic intervention. The signaling pathway identified in this proposal represents
a completely novel mechanism by which an apoptotic caspase controls inflammation via a network of proteins
with both recognized and unknown roles in inflammation. Mechanistic elucidation of this pathway will shed light

application_id: 11014627
activity_code: DP2
title: Late Stage Stereochemical Editing to Transform the Synthesis of Bioactive Molecules
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
Chiral organic molecules play an essential role in the study and treatment of human disease.
However, synthetic challenges limit access to these important bioactive compounds. This
proposal describes a conceptually new approach to the selective formation – and revision – of
stereogenic centers across a wide range of architecturally diverse molecular scaffolds. If
successful, the research described here is anticipated to transform chemical synthesis and enable
unprecedented access to diverse bioactive chiral molecules.

application_id: 10812476
activity_code: DP1
title: HIV-CRISPR:  A novel approach to the comprehensive discovery of HIV latency factors
org_name: FRED HUTCHINSON CANCER CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The single most significant obstacle to total HIV clearance and therefore, a “cure” for HIV in humans is presence of a reservoir of silent HIV genomes that are not targeted by the immune system or by any available antiviral drug. We propose a strategy for the comprehensive cataloging of genes involved in HIV latency maintenance based on a novel technology for high throughput and versatile screening of 10s of thousands of genes in combination with other drugs. The goal is to find targets that more broadly activate HIV from latency while at the same time providing a greater degree of specificity for the virus in order to guide HIV elimination efforts.

application_id: 11211286
activity_code: R00
title: Measuring treatment response and residual disease in leukemia with personalized, sensitive, and quantitative genomic methods
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Better methods are urgently needed to measure therapeutic response and detect early relapse, especially in
acute myeloid leukemia (AML), where 50 percent of patients that have a complete response to initial treatment
go on to relapse within 2 years. Current methods for measuring minimal residual disease in AML are not sensitive
enough, do not apply to all patients, and do not fully capture the genetic heterogeneity of the disease. In this
project, we propose that utilizing personalized genetic mutations associated with a patient’s leukemia will result
in more sensitive, quantitative, and general measurements of minimal residual disease, allowing for better
evaluation of treatment efficacy and earlier detection of relapse, ultimately improving patient outcome in AML.

application_id: 11184348
activity_code: DP5
title: Innovative therapeutic approaches to treat chronic kidney disease
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Potassium deficiency increases rates of chronic kidney disease (CKD), an illness that has a worldwide prevalence of 10-15% and claims over 130,000 lives annually in the US. Total body control of potassium balance involves both the kidneys and skeletal muscle with potassium channels in both organs being key molecular regulators of this process. This study aims to understand mechanisms by which potassium deficiency affects kidney epithelial cells and macrophages in the progression of CKD and the role of potassium channels in this process; the overall goal of this project is to identify novel CKD treatment targets in both the kidneys and skeletal muscle.

application_id: 11146716
activity_code: DP1
title: Community-driven drug prevention implementation strategies for Native Hawaiian and Pacific Islander youth in rural Hawai'i
org_name: JUDGE BAKER CHILDREN'S CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This high-risk, high-reward proposal addresses racial health equity and challenges the typical scientific
paradigm through innovative approaches in youth substance use prevention for Native Hawaiian and Pacific
Islander (NHPI) rural populations. Implementation science and participatory methods will shift power,
ownership, and responsibility from the project's inception to NHPI communities to actively promote adoption
and sustainment of culturally grounded substance use prevention. Community-led innovation tournaments
and ecological momentary assessment will produce new generalizable findings for other indigenous and
underserved populations, promoting the spread and reach of culturally grounded substance use prevention.

application_id: 11088466
activity_code: R01
title: Axonal Transport of mRNA for Mitochondrial Proteins (Diversity Supplement)
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Neurons depend on the transport of mRNA down axons and dendrites so that the mRNA can be translated into proteins locally. We uncovered a mechanism by which mRNA travels on mitochondria by binding to a protein called synaptojanin2. We now ask what species of mRNA use this method of transportation and how important the mechanism is for the health of neurons, synapses, and neuronal mitochondria.

application_id: 11014659
activity_code: DP2
title: Unraveling the genetic basis of cellular behaviors with deep learning and imaging-based reverse genetics
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
Modern imaging technologies now allow us to measure the spatial, temporal, and genomic axes
of variation in living systems; as such, it is now possible for images to be a universal data type
for the life sciences. This proposal seeks to make this vision reality by developing new
computational methods for characterizing the behavior of single cells in biological images and
creating a new experimental approach to imaging-based reverse genetics. The proposed work
will make spatial genomics methods accessible to users of all skill levels and empower a new
generation of multi-modal single cell measurements – advances that will drive broad and
sustained impact throughout the scientific community.

application_id: 11171848
activity_code: DP2
title: The representation of human odor in the mosquito brain
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY (CHARLES RIVER CAMPUS)
phr: Narrative
Mosquito-borne disease is a major threat to global public health, resulting in more than half a million deaths
every year. A greater understanding of how mosquitoes detect humans would allow us to develop novel
strategies to intervene in mosquito biting behavior and disease transmission. The proposed projects will
develop new genetic methods to study key components of the central nervous system of Aedes aegypti
mosquitoes and apply those techniques to understand how mosquitoes combine multiple cues during the
search for a person to bite.

application_id: 11213076
activity_code: DP2
title: Deciphering fungal pathogen-specific cell cycle mechanisms to fuel drug discovery
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Fungal infections are a pressing and underappreciated threat to global public health, yet treatment options are
limited due to the cellular similarities between fungi and humans. Using an important human fungal pathogen
Cryptococcus neoformans as a model system, this proposal aims to dissect fungal-specific cell cycle variations
that are essential for C. neoformans’s ability to proliferate and evade the host immune system during infection.
Results from this work will uncover unique aspects of fungal pathogen biology and facilitate the identification of
new targets for antifungal development.

application_id: 11379626
activity_code: DP2
title: Crossing scales to predict and prevent bat virus zoonoses in a Madagascar ecosystem
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: The cross-species emergence of SARS-CoV-2 from wild, Rhinolophus spp. bats to human hosts highlights the
extreme public health, economic, and societal consequences that can result from zoonotic spillover. This
proposal leverages tools from mathematical modeling, field biology, and molecular immunology to decipher the
mechanisms underpinning the circulation of bat-borne viruses at a population-level, understand the processes
which shape their pathology within-host, and eradicate them from reservoir populations to prevent future
threats of zoonotic emergence.

application_id: 11376326
activity_code: DP2
title: Building a Systematic, Comprehensive Mammalian Cell Fate Map
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Cell fate maps, which chart the pathways of differentiation and showcase the dynamics of and coordination by
cells to achieve complex biological structures, are immensely powerful – yet the mammalian cell fate map has
remained elusive. A sufficiently detailed fate map would reveal the processes by which cells go from stem
cells to specific cell types, such as neurons or muscle, providing invaluable clues to make these cells in a dish
for future cellular therapies, such as for spinal cord injury, Parkinson’s disease, and diabetes. This project
utilizes a CRISPR-based lineage tracing technology to systematically characterize differentiation pathways and
aims to use that information to invent protocols for making different cell types in vitro.

activity_code: DP1
title: Decoding endocrine and paracrine communication through mammokines
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative
Recent studies have confirmed that endocrine interactions play a significant role in causing and contributing to
various common health issues, especially obesity, type 2 diabetes, and the resulting cardiovascular problems.
The objective of this proposal is to establish a strong connection between mammary gland and metabolic
tissue in order to thoroughly investigate the way by which mammokines communicate in health and disease
conditions. Our approach involves initially utilizing newly developed precision proteomics, transcriptomics,
computational methods, validation, and assess the potential for new endocrine interactions to be used as
therapies in mouse models of metabolic disorders.

application_id: 11063633
activity_code: DP2
title: Assessing Internalized Health-Related Stigma in Spanish-Speaking Latino Adults
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Social stigma due to health conditions increases disease burden and adversely impacts health. The internalization of health-related stigma (i.e., self-stigma) is associated with impaired mental health and quality of life, yet little is known about how to reduce internalized stigma. The current project will test the effects of a novel, transdiagnostic, group counseling intervention, and of general peer support, to determine the optimal method for helping patients cope with health-related stigma, reducing its internalization, and, ultimately, enhancing patient quality of life.

application_id: 11260183
activity_code: R36
title: A Mixed Methods Exploration of Prenatal Polysubstance Use
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: While the prevalence and severity of individual substance use during pregnancy is well-documented, less is known about perinatal polysubstance use. This research will inform prevention efforts by 1) conducting qualitative interviews among pregnant or postpartum women engaging in prenatal polysubstance use to understand factors contributing to such behaviors, and 2) utilizing an existing national pregnancy survey (CDC PRAMS) to quantitatively establish outcomes of polysubstance use behaviors among pregnant women, which will be the first to incorporate environmental factors into such a model. This research will improve understanding of risk for prenatal polysubstance use, highlight potential targets for intervention, and inform clinical efforts to improve healthcare for this population.

title: Is Obesity an Infectious Disease?: Gut bacterial and fungal translocation as an underappreciated driver of visceral adipose expansion.
org_name: CEDARS-SINAI MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Obesity is an insidious epidemic with few signs of abating. Over 70% of the adult U.S. population is overweight
or obese, as is nearly 20% of children under the age of 19. Despite knowing that diet and exercise can prevent
weight gain and promote weight loss, obesity and its associated health risks continue to rise. Is it all simply due
to human behavior, or are there other factors at play? This study aims to answer whether certain bacteria in the
gut can migrate out of the gut and into surrounding fat tissue where they do not typically belong. When this
occurs in other parts of the body, an immune response is mounted that can lead to chronic inflammation. This
may occur locally in fat tissue as well, leading to metabolic dysfunction. Therefore, findings from this study m...

application_id: 11198985
activity_code: F31
title: Intercepting the Evolution of Pro-Tumoral Myeloid Cells During the Initiation of Oral Cancer
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Oral epithelial dysplasia precedes head and neck cancer, presenting an ideal window for early treatment, yet
there are limited diagnostic criteria used to predict which lesions will progress to cancer. The interaction between
the immune system and the tumor greatly informs treatment response and tumor progression, therefore, this
project will investigate the modulation of the immune response at the onset of cancer initiation. This work will
describe how immune cells are co-opted in precancerous lesions to promote tumor survival, providing critical
information into key therapeutic interception points that may restore anti-tumor immunity at earlier stages of
disease.

application_id: 10862660
activity_code: K08
title: Physiologic Stress and Epigenetic Mechanisms that Precipitate Risk for Suicidal Behavior in Nurses
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The rate of nurses who die by suicide has reached alarming proportions, with some estimates higher than the general population and other similar workforces. However, there is limited evidence about individual and environmental factors that predispose a nurse to suicidal behavior in real-world clinical settings, nor are there significant investigations of the interplay between those factors and physiologic stress responses or epigenetics. This project will leverage advancements in stress measurement (hair cortisol) and epigenetic analysis (DNA methylation) to investigate which factors increase suicidal behavior in nurses, which may help to better identify high-risk individuals and to inform suicidal behavior prevention interventions.

application_id: 10950120
activity_code: K08
title: Multidimensional analysis of the regulatory T cell response in children with severe viral pneumonia
org_name: LURIE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL OF CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative Children are disproportionately affected by respiratory viruses and at risk of developing severe viral infections requiring admission to the pediatric intensive care unit. Once a child develops a severe disease, treatment options are currently limited to supportive measures that do not treat the underlying pathology. This proposal will provide insight into how the pediatric immune system promotes recovery from severe viral respiratory infections to inform the design of new treatments aimed at accelerating recovery and reducing the risk of complications from severe viral respiratory infections.

application_id: 11064628
activity_code: F32
title: Heme Homeostasis in the Malaria Parasite Plasmodium falciparum
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Blood stage malaria parasites encounter enormous amounts of heme and are extremely vulnerable to disruptions in heme homeostasis, yet it remains unknown how parasites regulate cytosolic heme concentrations. Use a cutting-edge click-chemistry-based technique, we will identify previously unknown heme binding proteins and then directly investigate the role of these candidate proteins as well as Heme Detoxification Protein in plasmodial heme homeostasis. Our work will reveal novel antimalarial targets that can be exploited to overcome current problems with antimalarial resistance.

activity_code: F31
title: Development & application of computational methods for the study of protein dynamics with PmHMGR as a model system
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NOTRE DAME
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Continuous and atomistic descriptions of enzyme mechanisms are crucial to the characterization of protein function and dysfunction—a common cause of disease—but unattainable with current techniques, necessitating novel methods which leverage computational-experimental synergy. The proposed work will investigate improvement of experimental data gathering and deconvolution through computational simulation and modeling as well as improve available tools for molecular dynamics simulations of biochemical events. The resulting methods will produce a more ideal, continuous picture of atomistic protein dynamics, bettering the ability of researchers to probe and describe the biophysical phenomena which often underly disease.

org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Peripheral nerve injuries (PNIs) account for approximately 3% of trauma cases in the clinic, which translates into an expenditure of approximately 150 billion dollars spent in annual care due to the increased morbidity and decrease in the patient’s quality of life caused by PNIs. An alternative to promote nerve regeneration across an injured nerve resulting in a gap are Acellular Nerve Allografts (ANAs), which are processed allogenic nerves that promote regeneration across short gaps (< 3 cm) but fail to promote regeneration across longer gaps (> 3 cm). title: Novel gene discovery in disorders of the liver and biliary tree
org_name: CHILDREN'S HOSP OF PHILADELPHIA
phr: Project Narrative
Because our understanding of hepatobiliary disease is incomplete, a significant number of children with biliary

title: Investigating the link between propionate metabolism and aging
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Mounting evidence support the idea that aging is rooted in metabolic dysfunction, which occurs when chemical reactions become dysregulated during aging and negatively alters the body's processing and distribution of nutrients, inducing the aberrant accumulation of certain metabolites. This project aims to unravel the association between propionate metabolism and aging, providing insights into potential diagnostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets for mitigating aging and age- related diseases.

title: Generative AI for synthetic data: A framework to expand health data reach for research and ensure algorithmic fairness
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Growing evidence suggests we can create privacy-respecting health datasets that are larger, more diverse, and more widely available through the integration of synthetic data produced by generative AI. However, it is currently unknown how best to appraise synthetic health data generation for the factors on which researchers need to trust (e.g., reliability of the data generated) and refine such technology to support equitable healthcare research and applications. This project will create a set of computational methods, realized in open-source software, to enable systematic evaluation of and multi-institutional development of synthetic health data, as well as the development of a fairness-aware medical AI pipeline through the integration of such data.

title: A Gene Drive Therapy for HIV: Single-Administration Intervention for High-RiskGroups
org_name: AUTONOMOUS THERAPEUTICS, INC.
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Persons who inject drugs (PWID) represent a key `superspreader' population for HIV/AIDS that drives the
epidemic in the US and other countries and it is exceptionally difficult to identify and reach these high-risk
populations with existing intervention strategies (e.g., ART and PrEP). This proposal will develop and test a
novel gene-drive concept to target the high-risk superspreaders who most need therapy. Gene drives would be
particularly useful for treating PWID and those who engage in injection drug use in resource-limited settings,
including the rural US.

title: Evaluating a mobile health tool to improve management of pediatric acute respiratory illness in Uganda
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project will help address one of the leading causes of mortality in young children worldwide, acute lower respiratory illnesses (ALRI), through a mobile health decision support tool called ALRITE. title: Mitochondrial signaling dynamics in cocaine use disorder
org_name: RESEARCH TRIANGLE INSTITUTE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The studies in this proposal aim to develop therapeutics to normalize mitochondrial function in cocaine use disorders. Using viral strategies to knockout specific mitochondrial genes and fluorescent sensors to measure aspects of mitochondrial function in awake, behaving mice, we will be able to validate currently available mitochondrial therapeutics, as well as develop a pipeline for testing future mitochondrial drugs. application_id: 11249217
activity_code: K08
title: Engineering protease biomarkers to guide surgical therapy for vestibular schwannoma and Neurofibromatosis Type 2
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Vestibular schwannoma (VS) is a brain tumor that severely impairs one’s ability to hear and communicate.
Surgical removal of VS through a craniotomy leads to significant morbidities including hearing loss, paralysis of
the face and injury to critical parts of the brain. Using a combination of the state-of-the art tools, including cell
and animal models of VS, genomic sequencing and nanotechnology, this proposed study identifies new
biomarkers that could help surgeons identify which VS are aggressive, and develops nanoparticles that home
to tumor cells to label areas of the tumor to make surgery more precise and safer for patients.

title: Changes in Enteric Microbiota and Inflammation with HIV PrEP
org_name: SEATTLE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This proposal will investigate changes to the viral and bacterial communities of the gut in people who take medicine to prevent HIV infection (PrEP). I will analyze stool and blood samples from volunteers to measure what viruses and bacteria are there, how they change after 3 months of using PrEP, and how those changes affect inflammation in the intestines or the entire body. A better understanding of these changes will allow us to improve the safety and tolerability of PrEP in addition to potential probiotic, prebiotic, or anti-inflamatory interventions to increase intestinal health.

title: Examining Iconicity with EEG: The role of Motoric Iconicity and Embodied Cognition in American Sign Language
org_name: GALLAUDET UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Caregivers of deaf/hard-of-hearing (DHH) may want to provide sign exposure to protect their child from linguistic deprivation but have to learn how to sign in order to do so. Adults have developed their embodied cognition skills – the ability to use stored sensory-motoric experiences during tasks – and may be able to capitalize on these skills when learning to sign, particularly for signs that directly access these stored representations. This proposal aims to assess the impact of motoric-iconicity–the structured mapping between a sign’s form (shape/movement) and its meaning–on embodied cognition in native signers and adult sign learners to determine if these signs can serve as a starting point for caregiver-centric interventions.

title: Training in Structural, Physiologic and Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The complexity of MRI, its multiparametric nature, and its myriad embodiments for the study of tissue structure, physiology and function, require systematic, modality-specific training. The proposed program entails all aspects of training physics and engineering concepts for MRI image acquisition, reconstruction and processing, in com- bination with preceptor-directed research.

application_id: 11251328
activity_code: K08
title: Determinants of Myeloid Clonal Evolution
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Myelodysplastic syndrome and myeloproliferative disorders currently affect over 400,000 individuals in the
United States alone; they affect blood production and predispose to leukemia. Their onset is age-dependent
but the genetic factors that determine their risk with aging is not understood. This proposal examines the role
of acquired mutations in the blood with aging that promote and protect against the acquisition of these blood
disorders with the goal of identifying new approaches to their early identification and prevention.
