application_id: 11065473
activity_code: K23
title: MRI Paramagnetic Rim Lesions as Biomarkers and Agents of Neurodegeneration in Multiple Sclerosis
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Multiple sclerosis is marked by inflammatory brain lesions. Most of these lesions start to heal
with time, but a small portion remain chronically active and likely contribute to the brain
shrinking at a rate about 2-4 times faster than normal. This project aims to look at how to
better identify these lesions, as well as potential treatment approaches to halt the chronic
inflammation.

application_id: 10842247
activity_code: F31
title: Accounting for selection and information bias in studies of Autism Spectrum Disorder
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Racial and ethnic disparities in health care services cause many Non-Hispanic Black and Hispanic children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) symptomology to go undiagnosed and under-represented in research studies generated from those clinical databases. Yet, many epidemiologic studies use healthcare databases to examine etiologic questions about ASD, often without necessary consideration of potential biases. Thus, this project will use quantitative bias analysis methods to examine the impact of information and selection bias in ASD research, with an application to the association between epidural analgesia use and ASD.

application_id: 10985404
activity_code: K99
title: Dissecting distinct contributions of different Prefrontal subregions on goal-directed visual attention
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: The prefrontal cortex is the part of the brain that hold our cognitive abilities, and it is thought to communicate with our visual system to produce attention towards visual information important for our current needs. In this proposal, we will study the nature of this communication to understand how we choose to attend to discrete aspects of our surroundings. With a thorough understanding of the underlying neuronal mechanisms, we can begin to understand how this communication malfunctions, and attention falls short, in disorders like attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and Schizophrenia.

application_id: 10913996
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the role of telomere failure on intestinal stem cell niche function
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative The intestinal epithelium plays critical roles in digestion and pathogen defense, and its integrity is maintained by a pool of proliferative intestinal stem cells. Recently a population of subepithelial mesenchymal telocytes have been identified as a critical niche population supplying cytokines to support stem cell activity. We address whether telomere dysfunction, which occurs during natural ageing and pathologically in telomeropathies, results in intestinal stem cell failure due to a loss of extrinsic niche support from the telocyte stem cell niche.

application_id: 10937114
activity_code: R25
title: Future Deaf Scientists Program at Wilmot Cancer Institute
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: NARRATIVE People with disabilities, including those who are deaf and hard of hearing, are still vastly underrepresented in STEM graduate education programs, health science professions, and cancer research settings. The University of Rochester James P. Wilmot Cancer Institute, in partnership with the Rochester School for the Deaf, seeks to establish the Future Deaf Scientists program in Rochester, NY and nationwide to inspire, grow, and increase deaf student participation in STEM and cancer research fields. The FDS program is an inclusive mentored research education and training program that empowers deaf high school students to envision themselves as future STEM researchers capable of decreasing cancer disparities.

application_id: 10842246
activity_code: F31
title: Single-cell dynamics of E2F and APC/CCdh1 coordination that regulate the proliferation-quiescence decision
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative The maintenance of quiescent cell populations in the body is essential to repair wounds, maintain healthy tissue and for pathogen defense, and once cells enter quiescence, they must also be able to re-enter the cell cycle and divide again. This project will investigate multifaceted cellular pathways regulated by E2F and APC/CCdh1 activity and how they synergize to create two pathways into S phase that must be suppressed to allow cells to enter quiescence, and how this synergy allows for the licensing of origins of DNA replication in G1. Completion of this study in single cells will support the identification of potential treatment targets for quiescent cancer cell populations and pro-proliferative treatments for increased cellular regeneration.

application_id: 10914043
activity_code: K08
title: Elucidation of the Development and Function of the Cardiac Conduction System
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Coordinated beating of the heart relies on specialized heart cells known as the cardiac conduction system
(CCS). Damage to this system can result in a host of diseases including life-threatening arrhythmias. The
proposed research will examine the mechanisms underlying CCS development and function, creating
translational opportunities aimed at the prevention and treatment of cardiac rhythm disorders.

activity_code: K99
title: Emerging Immune Checkpoint Therapies Reshape the Cardiac Immune Landscape and Promote Myocardial Vulnerability
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative This proposal for mentored career development is designed to train the principal investigator in order to confer the skills necessary to be a successful independent investigator. The first focus is on commitment to ensuring that the principal investigator receives the mentorship and training to address any gaps in technical knowledge and ensure attainment of skills required of an independent biomedical investigator. The second focus is on understanding unexplored mechanisms by which a new combination immunotherapy (OX40 activation+PD-1 inhibition) reshapes the cardiac immune landscape and promotes myocardial vulnerability.

application_id: 11170387
activity_code: K08
title: Revealing the mechanisms of increased intestinal epithelial cell barrier permeability in peanut allergy
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Peanut is the most common cause of death from an allergic reaction to food, and epinephrine is the only life-
saving treatment available. We found that the severity of allergic reaction to peanut in pediatric patients
correlated with intestinal barrier permeability, but we don’t understand how or why this happens. This application
will teach us more about the relationship between intestinal permeability and peanut allergy to better diagnose,
predict, and treat severe allergic reactions to peanut and other food allergens.

activity_code: K08
title: The role of Wnt signaling in aggressive thyroid carcinoma
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE: Wnt signaling is essential for numerous tumor-types and may play an essential role in the development of aggressive thyroid cancers. In this proposal, I will study the role of Wnt signaling in aggressive thyroid cancer using next generation sequencing, novel thyroid cancer organoids, and a patient-derived xenograft mouse model to discover a potential new therapy for anaplastic thyroid carcinoma.

application_id: 10985592
activity_code: K99
title: The uncharacterized nuclear protein HMGXB4 in occlusive vascular diseases
org_name: AUGUSTA UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Occlusive vascular diseases are the leading cause of death in the United States and worldwide. The proposed study focuses on investigating the function and the underlying mechanisms of the uncharacterized nuclear protein HMGXB4 in vascular smooth muscle cells related to occlusive vascular diseases. Completion of the proposed studies will provide novel insights into the mechanisms regulating smooth muscle inflammation and phenotypic switching, and will determine if inhibiting HMGXB4 is an attractive novel therapeutic strategy for ameliorating occlusive vascular diseases.

application_id: 11170392
activity_code: F31
title: Role of FOXK subfamily in KSHV infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative Kaposi's sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV) is an oncogenic gammaherpesvirus that's main route of transmission occurs through saliva in the oral cavity. KSHV encodes an immediate early virion-associated tegument protein, that is vital for efficient lytic reactivation and virus production and that we have established interaction with key host transcription factors. Little is known about the importance of this host pathogen interaction, and this proposal will reveal the mechanisms by which an oncogenic virus utilizes host transcription factors to promote its own lytic lifecycle.

title: Midcareer Mentoring in Patient-Oriented Research on Genitourinary Aging in Women
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This proposal will have unique public health importance because (1) it will increase mentoring of new
clinical investigators interested in conducting patient-oriented research to improve evaluation, prevention,
and management of genitourinary conditions in older women, and (2) it will advance a multi-system,
geriatric perspective on treatment of urinary incontinence as one of the most common, costly, and
debilitating genitourinary conditions among older women living in the community.

application_id: 11066050
activity_code: F30
title: Characterizing IgA-microbe interactions in type 1 diabetes
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE How the microbiota contribute to type 1 diabetes (T1D) pathogenesis is not well understood, though the increased relative abundance of Akkermansia muciniphila (A.M.) is associated with protection against T1D in both mouse models and patients. In this proposal, we aim to determine how B cell signaling contributes to mucosal immunity, with the goal of understanding the role of IgA-A.M. interactions in T1D. Ultimately, these findings will generate new knowledge of immune-microbial crosstalk and potentially create therapeutic opportunities to prevent the onset of T1D.

application_id: 11126939
activity_code: R00
title: Racial Disparities in Delayed Dementia Diagnosis: Structural Determinants and Validation of Timeliness Measures
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE As we shift toward a new era of disease-modifying therapies, racial disparities in diagnosis and care of Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD) will become even more evident and will likely increase unless we intervene in conditions driving such disparities in timely ADRD diagnosis. The proposed research validating timeliness measures and examining structural drivers of racial disparities in timely ADRD diagnosis will inform policies and interventions that focus on modifiable structural barriers to reduce disparities in timely ADRD diagnosis.

application_id: 10840801
activity_code: F32
title: Elucidating the mechanisms of alphavirus subgenomic RNA translation
org_name: ROCKEFELLER UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alphavirus infection causes a robust innate immune response in host cells, including global protein translation shutoff, to reduce viral replication and prevent spread. However, alphaviruses have evolved unclear mechanisms to continue effective viral protein synthesis under conditions of translation shutoff. This proposal seeks to identify the mechanism by which alphaviruses initiate translation and modulate the host translational shutoff response with the goal of identifying novel targets for antivirals.

title: The benefits of nicotinamide riboside upon cognition and sleep in older veterans
org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY OF NEW YORK AT BUFFALO
phr: Project Narrative Poor sleep quality and short sleep duration may be a mechanistic component of cognitive impairment in older adults, associated with a decline in brain-derived neurotrophic factor. Increasing the availability of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD+) with supplementation of its precursor, nicotinamide riboside (NR), may increase the expression of brain-derived neurotrophic factor. We propose to examine the benefits of NR supplementation upon sleep and cognitive function in older adults with comprehensive subjective and objective measures and to explore its impacts on serum brain-derived neurotrophic factor. activity_code: DP2
title: Network Dissection of Host-Pathogen Interactions in Mycobacterium tuberculosis Infection
org_name: SEATTLE CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: NARRATIVE
Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) infection fate is the product of interactions between host and pathogen
coordinated through biological networks. I will systematically dissect host signaling and Mtb gene regulatory
network activities that impact Mtb viability and host inflammation during Mtb infection in primary human
monocyte-derived macrophages (MDMs), a major infected host cell in vivo. This characterization will uniquely
enable dissection of infection phenotypes in primary human host cells at an unprecedented scale and granularity,
maximizing clinical relevance of biological insights.

title: Complexity: Innovations in Promoting Health through Team Science
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Complex chronic conditions account for a large portion of disease, death, and disability in the US. org_name: SALK INSTITUTE FOR BIOLOGICAL STUDIES
phr: Project Narrative
The proposed research is relevant to public health as it seeks to uncover the mechanisms by which maternal
immune cells regulate milk composition and influence offspring immunity, which could help reduce the incidence
of chronic diseases. By enhancing understanding of the entero-mammary axis, the findings may inform strategies
to optimize maternal nutrition and immune function during lactation, ultimately improving health outcomes for
both mothers and children. This knowledge can contribute to reducing healthcare burdens associated with early-
life infections and chronic intestinal diseases and promote healthier populations across generations.

title: Prenatal per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) exposure and associations with common childhood infections and allergies: A study of risks and resiliencies in the ECHO Program
org_name: DREXEL UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
No U.S. cohort to date has evaluated associations with per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS)
with measures of health consequence of reduced immune function including common childhood
infections and some allergies, despite the use of decreased antibody response following vaccine as a
critical endpoint for the regulation of PFAS. We propose to combine pediatric birth cohorts with
available prenatal PFAS serum concentrations from across the Environmental Influences on Child
Health Outcomes cohort, to evaluate associations with childhood infections (bronchiolitis, croup,
pneumonia and bronchitis, ear infections) and allergies (allergy symptoms, and atopic dermatitis) at
infancy and early childhood for individual PFAS and mixtures. Additional analyses will include effect

application_id: 11180168
activity_code: DP1
title: Wastewater surveillance to enhance the public health response to HIV and TB in Eswatini
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: NARRATIVE
This project will introduce and validate wastewater surveillance for HIV and TB in Eswatini, a high burden setting
for both diseases. First, we will introduce and validate techniques for wastewater surveillance of HIV and TB;
second, correlate wastewater detection of HIV and TB with public health epidemiological data; and third, evaluate
the impact of proven public health prevention and case-finding strategies on levels of TB and HIV in wastewater.
The candidate’s approach blends innovative science with strong long-term partnerships with multiple
stakeholders in Eswatini, and if successful will help catalyze the drive to end HIV and TB in high burden settings.

application_id: 11477327
activity_code: K08
title: Gene Regulatory Mechanisms of the Coronary Artery Disease Gene PRDM16 on Smooth Muscle Cell Phenotypic Modulation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF OKLAHOMA HLTH SCIENCES CTR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Much of the attributable risk of coronary artery disease (CAD) lies in smooth muscle cells, which undergo a
complex process of “phenotypic modulation” whereby cells migrate, de-differentiate, and proliferate over the
course of disease. The molecular mechanisms of how CAD-associated genes affect this phenotypic
modulation remain poorly understood. This proposal will determine how the CAD-associated gene PR domain
containing 16 (PRDM16) regulates phenotypic modulation, paving the way for new targets to treat CAD, the
world’s leading cause of death.

activity_code: R36
title: Influences of Prenatal Phthalate Exposure on Early Childhood Overweight/Obesity and Potentially Protective Dietary Strategies
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF DELAWARE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Phthalates are common chemicals in the environment that can disrupt hormones and change how the body
accumulates and stores fat. When pregnant women are exposed to these chemicals it may have negative
impacts on their children, including increasing their risk for obesity. We will investigate how being exposed to
these chemicals before birth is linked to childhood obesity, and whether certain diets can help prevent this.

application_id: 10864335
activity_code: K22
title: Cancer Misinformation and Use of Complementary and Alternative Therapy (CAM) among Patients with Cancer.
org_name: HACKENSACK UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Narrative Up to 70% of cancer patients utilize complementary and alternative therapies (CAM) in cancer care, which was associated with lower adherence to standard care. Unfortunately, many patients make CAM decisions without involving their healthcare providers, missing the opportunity for CAM-related information validation. Dr. Fridman develops an intervention to overcome patient and provider barriers to transparent welcoming patient-provider conversations about CAM and CAM misinformation.

application_id: 11159232
activity_code: F32
title: End-to-end Impedance Modeling Towards Task-Invariant Control of Powered Prosthetic Legs
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Current prosthetic leg control methods are unable to realize seamless, accurate, and reliable performance across
all primary activities of daily living (ADLs), which is critical for the clinical relevance of these devices. This
research aims to model the relationship between lower-limb kinematics and desired knee and ankle joint
impedance and to develop a novel end-to-end impedance control paradigm from these findings. This work can
significantly impact the mobility, independence, and quality of life of individuals with lower-limb loss, advancing
the deployment of powered prosthetic legs for everyday use.

application_id: 11066140
activity_code: F32
title: The Role of Mental Imagery in Perceptual Abnormality Formation in Individuals at Clinical High-Risk for Psychosis
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative  Hallucinations and other perceptual abnormalities are hallmark symptoms of psychosis spectrum disorders, but as of yet, there is no definitive mechanism to target for intervention and prevention. One modern theory of hallucination formation leverages a predictive coding model of belief updating, but this model might be expanded upon by considering the mechanisms driving the vividness of mental imagery to explain perceptual abnormality severity. By combining the predictive coding theory with novel research on the mechanisms of mental imagery, the present study aims to identify a potential mechanism for the formation of perceptual abnormalities and thus identifying a target for intervention.

application_id: 11066228
activity_code: F31
title: 3D Human Bone Marrow Microphysiological System Used to Investigate HSPC Mobilization via Inflammation-Derived ExtracellularVesicles
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The bone marrow microenvironment (BMM) is a highly dynamic system that plays a significant role in pathogenesis by regulating the production of blood cells to maintain homeostasis and responding to stress signals that contribute to cell production. Inflammatory cues trigger the bone to produce immune cells aiding in immune response modulation. In this proposal, we hypothesize that extracellular vesicles derived from sites of inflammation play a role in mobilizing neutrophils and hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs) from the BMM to the peripheral circulation.

application_id: 11238484
activity_code: R36
title: Outpatient Mental Health Treatment, Median Household Income, and Neighborhood Demographic Patterns: Improving Measurement of Spatial Accessibility for Medicaid Recipients
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This proposal examines two structural factors that may contribute to reduced spatial accessibility to Medicaid-accepting mental health providers: neighborhood-level median household income and the spatial distribution of white and non-white populations. The results of this dissertation will aid in reducing access disparities by identifying potentially modifiable factors that could increase provider access for low-income and non-white individuals. Moreover, the developed methods will provide a pragmatic and iterative technique for identifying provider shortage areas and reducing access disparities over time.

application_id: 11136539
activity_code: DP1
title: Identifying specific genetic pathway interactions for drug use and abuse through integrative omics
org_name: SCRIPPS RESEARCH INSTITUTE, THE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cannabis use disorders are highly prevalent in the U.S., and frequently co-occur with alcohol and other
substance use disorders. The proposed research seeks to identify and determine how key genomic variants
and pathways interact with each other in a disease-relevant tissue specific context to affect an individual’s risk
for drug abuse. The obtained knowledge will be incorporated into a larger disease model and lead to more
precision in the understanding of the mechanism underlying risk for cannabis use disorders and thus provide a
substrate for more effective prevention and intervention.

title: Investigating COUP-TFII in human vein development
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Knowledge of human blood vessel formation, including veins, is crucial for successfully vascularizing engineered tissues in regenerative medicine and effectively treating organs affected by vascular diseases. However, the processes guiding the development of human veins remain poorly understood due to the scarcity of samples and the lack of relevant model systems. Here, I propose to study COUP-TFII, a transcription factor implicated in venous differentiation in other species, and its mechanisms of action by leveraging a recently developed human pluripotent stem cell differentiation protocol with the long-term goal of identifying new therapeutic pathways.

application_id: 11247166
activity_code: R36
title: Probing the Proteome: Depressive Symptoms, Inflammation and Mitochondrial Perturbations in Acute HIV Infection
org_name: FLORIDA INTERNATIONAL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Depressive disorders are three times more prevalent among people with HIV than in the general population
and predict worse health outcomes along the HIV care continuum. Thus, there is a need to elucidate the
biobehavioral determinants of depressive disorders to catalyze the development of novel pharmacologic and
behavioral treatment for this prevalent HIV comorbidity. This project will advance our mechanistic
understanding of how HIV-induced pathophysiological alterations beginning in acute HIV infection may amplify
risk for depressive disorders by probing the proteome to identify differentially expressed proteins in PWH that
have persistent depressive symptoms over 96 weeks despite initiation of suppressive ART.

application_id: 11070405
activity_code: DP1
title: Gut microbiota-mediated fiber fermentation in HIV-associated cardiovascular disease
org_name: CEDARS-SINAI MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:
 Hypertension and cardiovascular disease occur at substantially greater rates in optimally
treated people with HIV, though the etiology of this elevated comorbidity risk remains poorly
understood. The gut microbiota of people with HIV exhibits a loss of microbes that metabolize dietary
fiber into key metabolites that can ameliorate hypertension. We will investigate the dynamics of fiber
metabolism and uptake in the setting of HIV, the role of these phenomena in cardiovascular
comorbidities, and whether capacity for fiber metabolism can be restored within the HIV-associated
gut microbiome.

application_id: 10863618
activity_code: K01
title: Targeted mRNA Delivery to Improve Diabetic Wound Healing
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Non-healing diabetic foot ulcers affect millions of Americans annually and existing therapeutic interventions are not efficient. This application will investigate how lipid nanoparticles loaded with pro-healing gene encoding mRNAs, and surface-functionalized to target specific cell types, can be delivered with novel biomaterials to accelerate diabetic wound healing. Additionally, using multi-omics approaches, the spatial cellular transcriptomic and proteomic landscape of the wounds will be mapped to determine the exact mechanisms of action.

application_id: 11163530
activity_code: DP1
title: Impact of HIV co-infections and comorbidities on circadian rhythm in the liver
org_name: EMORY UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
HIV co-infection accelerates liver cirrhosis and liver cancer in chronic hepatitis B virus (HBV) carriers. In addition,
HIV is associated with more severe fatty liver disease. The use of unique primary cell culture models and
humanized mice under conditions that maintain or disrupt the circadian rhythm of hepatocytes will determine the
interaction of HIV co-infections and co-morbidities with the circadian rhythm. These studies will provide
information for HIV pathogenesis in the liver and may help the development of novel antiviral strategies.

application_id: 11305570
activity_code: DP2
title: Decoding the fundamental principles of autonomous clocks: mechanism,design and function
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Project Narrative:
Classic knowledge on cellular time control has revolved around the regulation of the Cdk/Cyclin oscillator, what
is considered a master clock for events in the cell cycle. Challenging the textbook paradigm, this work leverages
state-of-the-art molecular techniques to unravel the design principles of autonomous clocks – mechanisms that
can run independently of the cell cycle oscillator to serve a list of quality control, failsafe and pace-making roles
in physiology. The outcomes will generate original knowledge on this emerging concept of biological time control,
and help crystallize their relevance in health and a variety of pathologies.
terms: N/A

application_id: 10983443
activity_code: K23
title: Electrophysiological biomarkers of social engagement in autism
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The lack of objective, clinically relevant brain-based biomarkers of social engagement hinders our ability to test novel interventions and treatments to improve functioning in autistic children. The proposed study will test how several promising measures of brain activity in young, autistic children relate to core features such as social attention, social interaction with caregivers, and standardized clinical assessments of social communication functioning. Results from this study will give us tools to better understand heterogeneity in autism as well as more effectively evaluate response to therapies and treatments.

application_id: 11082607
activity_code: DP1
title: Immune drivers of oncogenic virus progression to cancer in people living with HIV
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF NEBRASKA MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
With rates of oncogenic viral infections higher in people living with HIV (PHIV) there is a need to understand
the immune drivers of persistence of such an infection before it progresses to cancer. A novel derivation of a
widely available assay was recently produced and is proposed here as an innovative way to first, evaluate oral
HPV persistence in PHIV and then expanded for utilization at other HPV-related anatomic sites and within the
scope of other oncogenic infections. As immuno-oncology is a relatively new area of research in the study of
oncogenic virus progression to cancer, the results of these studies would inform design of future research in
the field, including the use of immune markers.

application_id: 10914760
activity_code: K22
title: Understanding initiation and progression of IPMNs in pancreatic cancer
org_name: DREXEL UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative In 10% of elderly patients, pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) originates from mucin-producing cystic precancerous lesions called intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms (IPMNs). Activating mutations in GNAS(R201C) are thought to drive IPMN-derived PDAC, but the cell of origin for IPMN initiation is not known. This proposal aims to take advantage of human stem cell-derived pancreatic ductal and acini organoids to understand how oncogenic GNAS supports initiation and progression of IPMNs in pancreatic cancer.

activity_code: D43
title: Vanderbilt-Mozambique Biomedical Informatics (VM-BMI) Training and Research Program
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: NARRATIVE Mozambique (a country in Africa with the fourth highest HIV prevalence globally) has made good progress in HIV pandemic control, but significant gaps remain. Leveraging proven leadership and expertise as Mozambique’s lead HIV Strategic Information partner and a history of highly successful partnerships between the University of Eduardo Mondlane (UEM) and Vanderbilt University Medical Center (VUMC), the Vanderbilt- Mozambique Biomedical Informatics (VM-BMI) Training and Research program will increase biomedical informatics (BMI) capacity for HIV research at UEM. Over the course of the five-year project, VM-BMI will train 6 UEM faculty fellows, 10 master’s-level UEM students, and 200 Mozambican workshop and bootcamp participants with an eye towards long-term sustainability.

application_id: 10863832
activity_code: T32
title: Boston University Rheumatology Research Training (BURRT) T32 Program
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: The Boston University Rheumatology Research Training (BURRT) T32 Program will provide a comprehensive two-year multidisciplinary training program in rheumatic and musculoskeletal diseases for postdoctoral MD and PhD scholars to pursue academic and scientific clinical research careers. The program will train 3 postdoctoral scholars every 2 years in a core curriculum and several specialized tracks under the guidance of experienced and funded mentors, and supported by a broad curriculum with coursework, seminars, roundtables, team research meetings, and hands-on experiences. Ultimately, the program aims to have T32 scholars successfully transition to independent clinical research careers as the next general of leaders in rheumatic and musculoskeletal disease research.

title: Engineering tumor-targeting bacteria to sense and deliver therapeutics
org_name: HARVARD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The engineering of living cells as programmable therapeutic system is poised to transform therapeutic paradigms, particularly for cancer where some bacteria selectively colonize solid tumors. A central challenge for developing living microbial therapies is the inaccessibility to study this dynamical system consisting of interacting populations of bacteria and cancer cells. This research proposal leverages 3D multicellular coculture technologies and syn- thetic biology tools to engineer novel bacterial systems that sense and express therapeutics specifically in tumors.

phr: Project Narrative The University of Utah has over a four-decade long history of training exceptional biomedical informatics students who go on to have a profound and lasting impact on health discovery and delivery. title: Improving integration of treatment burden information in primary care for older adults with multiple chronic conditions
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Older adults with multiple chronic conditions (MCCs) are prone to experiencing high treatment burden due to the complexity of their care, yet no systematic approach exists to use data routinely collected in clinical care (e.g. accessible in electronic health record or claims data) to estimate their treatment burden and integrate this information in clinical decision-making. The objectives of this K38 StARRTS proposal are to develop a scalable model to estimate treatment burden among older adults with MCCs using routinely- collected Medicare claims data, and to understand patient, caregiver, and PCP perspectives on integrating treatment burden information in decision-making in primary care. application_id: 11036351
activity_code: R38
title: Mayo Clinic StARR Program
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: The Mayo Clinic Stimulating Access to Research in Residency (StARR) program achieved substantial success in its inaugural round with eight resident-investigators completing or currently active in research training. The program—both in model design, operation, and outcomes—has made important strides in supporting heart (and vascular), lung, hematologic (blood), and sleep (HLBS) disorders/diseases research training and career development, building a solid and sustainable research career pipeline. In our next cycle, we will continue to enhance the Mayo Clinic StARR program by extending opportunities to residents in Laboratory Medicine and Pathology and adding experienced preceptors from the same department, and by invigorating recruitment and training to residents.

application_id: 10864380
activity_code: K01
title: The Impact of Testosterone on Vaginal Atrophy and the Vaginal Microbiome in Transgender Men
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Most transgender men using testosterone gender-affirming hormone therapy develop vaginal atrophy and have a non-Lactobacillus dominated vaginal microbiota (i.e., vaginal dysbiosis). This study will investigate if testosterone use and vaginal atrophy severity are associated with vaginal microbiota composition, taxonomic diversity, or vaginal infections. This research will fill a critical gap in our understanding of the gynecological health of transgender men using testosterone and support the development of culturally-tailored, evidence- based interventions to prevent and treat vaginal atrophy, vaginal dysbiosis, and vaginal infections in this population.

phr: Addressing the prominent health disparities in West Virginia and Appalachia depends on the recruitment, training

application_id: 11066847
activity_code: F31
title: Regulation of co- and post-transcriptional pre-mRNA processing in response to environmental stress in green algae
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Human activity contributes to increased temperature and water pollution, reducing water quality and activating stress responses in all aquatic organisms. While stress is known to affect messenger RNA (mRNA) abundance and isoforms in mammals and plants, how these environmental stressors affect the molecular mechanisms that determine which mRNA isoforms and corresponding proteins are expressed is unknown. Here, we are identifying the RNA processing steps that are altered by environmental stressors in Chlamydomonas reinhardtii, a biomedically- and climate-relevant green alga, which will have valuable implications for green energy, ecology, and human health.

application_id: 11057333
activity_code: R38
title: Wilmer Eye Institute Stimulating Access to Research in Residency Program
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The Wilmer Stimulating Access to Research in Residency (StARR) program creates a
pathway for residents interested in pursuing research during residency to build their
skills through mentored research conducted within a Wilmer Ophthalmology residency.
Wilmer StARR residents will conduct 1-2 years of dedicated research, with research
preparation and progress actively monitored and guided by Executive and Advisory
Committees. Clinician scientist training will be supplemented by dedicated workshops
on manuscript writing, grant writing, and career development.

activity_code: DP1
title: Informing optimal investments along the preconception to postpartum eMTCT continuum
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: NARRATIVE Global progress to eliminate mother-to-child transmission of HIV (eMTCT) has stagnated in recent years, highlighting the need for a multifaceted approach that complements the existing focus on treatment initiation among pregnant women living with HIV. This project aims to use simulation-based modeling to evaluate the cost- effectiveness of a wide range of eMTCT interventions and identify optimal intervention portfolios tailored to Kenya, Nigeria, and Botswana, three diverse country contexts that will serve as highly translatable case studies for other African settings. Our findings from this comprehensive eMTCT intervention analysis will provide urgently needed support for eMTCT policymaking and pave the way for a new frontier in eMTCT progress.

application_id: 11132946
activity_code: DP1
title: Impact of NAFLD on Metabolic Brain Function via CEST MRI in HIV-Positive Individuals
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: Project Narrative
Liver disease is a top cause of morbidity and mortality in people living with HIV, with emerging data indicating
high risk for non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) development. The goal of this proposal is to develop a
novel artificial intelligence-based approach for rapid and high-quality clinical chemical exchange saturation
transfer (CEST), a molecular MRI technique used to assess metabolic brain dysfunction. CEST imaging as well
as other MRI metrics of neuroinflammation, and their associations with blood markers and cognitive performance
will help us to better understand and monitor potential interventions.

application_id: 10990117
activity_code: F99
title: Identifying and Targeting Drivers of Impaired Translation in Tumor-infiltrating CD8+ T-cells.
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Lack of durable CD8+ T-cell response to produce inflammatory cytokines upon tumor infiltration remains a barrier to immunotherapy for most cancer patients, prompting the need for a deeper understanding of how T-cells rapidly become dysfunctional. application_id: 10913375
activity_code: K08
title: Investigating CD56 signaling in multiple myeloma growth and immune escape
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Multiple Myeloma (MM) is an incurable disease that affects vulnerable elderly adults and minorities, causing morbidity and poor quality of life. We identified the protein CD56 as a key player able to support MM growth and escape from the immune system. title: The unleashed microbiome of cancer patients as a discovery platform for rational microbiome engineering
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative This project seeks to provide a platform for the discovery of causal health effects of the human gut microbiome. title: Genetic and pharmacologic inhibition of ALDH1A3 as a treatment of beta cell failure
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative
Type 2 diabetes can be caused by failure to maintain β-cell identity, resulting in dedifferentiation into endocrine

phr: Project Narrative The proposed project will test the novel hypothesis that aberrant vascular lipid metabolism is a component of Alzheimer’s disease (AD). activity_code: DP2
title: Maternal immune activation remodeling of offspring glycosaminoglycan sulfation patterns during neurodevelopment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Maternal immune activation (MIA) is a primer for long-term neuropsychiatric consequences in offspring, although the exact mechanisms underlying offspring neurodevelopmental defects resulting from these maternal immune insults are unknown. Here, we provide evidence that MIA-induced changes in the developing offspring’s extracellular chondroitin and dermatan sulfate glycosaminoglycan (CS/DS-GAG) sulfation patterning, which are key regulator of axonal guidance and synaptogenesis during neurodevelopment, may result in developmental defects that predispose the offspring to mental health disorders in the adult, and studies outlined within this proposal aim to further investigate this possibility.

application_id: 10901981
activity_code: DP2
title: Exploring metabolic governance of immune cell form and function
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Tissue-resident macrophages (TRMs) are vital executives of tissue homeostasis and play important roles in tumors, yet the (metabolic) pathways important for their development, tissue- specific identity, and function are not well understood. Here, we put forward a framework to identify and characterize the metabolic programs overseeing these key aspects of TRM biology, including innovative approaches to explore macrophage metabolism within human tissues. Through these studies, we will obtain a deeper understanding of TRMs across health and disease, and ascertain novel metabolic vulnerabilities that can be exploited for immunomodulation of these essential cells.

application_id: 11145815
activity_code: K01
title: Diagnosis, Treatment, and Monitoring of Polymicrobial Infections in Diabetic Wounds
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative.
Our ongoing study explores the use of Glutamate 1-Semialdehyde (GSA) in inducing porphyrin accumulation in
bacteria, facilitating their visual identification and elimination via fluorescence imaging and light-based killing,
notably without affecting mammalian cells. While preliminary data showcase the in vitro efficacy of GSA-
phototherapy on common wound-infecting bacteria, the proposed research will delve into its detection thresholds,
biosafety, and killing efficacy utilizing diabetic mice and mini-pigs. This approach proposes a multifaceted
solution for the timely diagnosis, real-time monitoring, and non-antibiotic treatment of bacterial infections in
diabetic wounds, potentially preventing severe outcomes like amputation and sepsis.

application_id: 11170540
activity_code: DP2
title: The molecular basis of ferrosome organelle biogenesis and its impact on host-microbe interactions
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE:
Our prior research and preliminary data have demonstrated that the Gram-positive anaerobic pathogen
Clostridioides difficile produces membrane-bound ferrosome organelles as part of its defense strategy against
host-imposed iron sequestration; however, the molecular basis of ferrosome formation remains poorly
understood. The experiments outlined in this application aim to elucidate the underlying mechanisms of
ferrosome biogenesis, define the function of the ferrosome iron minerals within the gut community, and determine
the impact of nutrient iron on host-microbe interactions. The findings of this proposal will uncover novel factors
critical for C. difficile infection and create a framework for developing effective antimicrobial therapeutics to
combat this important infection.

application_id: 10892836
activity_code: DP2
title: Characterizing Macrophages as "Hide-Outs" for Chronic Pathogens
org_name: UNIV OF MASSACHUSETTS MED SCH WORCESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Both HIV and Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) establish life-long infections that, despite the development of antiretroviral therapy and antibiotics, cause millions of deaths every year, emphasizing the need to develop a cure. This proposal aims to define the methods by which HIV and Mtb evade immune-mediated clearance by characterizing their common target cell, the macrophage, and how this cell acts to shield these pathogens from immune responses. Insight into these mechanisms will help identify macrophage-specific pathways that can be exploited to make these infected cells more susceptible to immune-mediated clearance, potentiating the development of cure-based strategies for HIV and Mtb infection.

application_id: 10983624
activity_code: R01
title: Clinical, Neuroanatomic, and Pathologic Signatures of FTLD-tau in Dementia Phenotypes - Diversity Supplement (Antwan Howard)
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: PUBLIC HEALTH RELEVANCE.
Inclusions of the transactive DNA binding protein 43 (TDP-43) are present in an overwhelming majority of elderly
individuals with Alzheimer's disease (AD). These co-occurring diseases lead to dementia, functional decline, and
brain changes in the elderly—a population that is steadily rising worldwide. The current study will address this
major public health challenge by investigating the underling neurobiological signatures and mechanistic
contributions of TDP-43 in AD, with the long-term goal of contributing to the development of targeted
interventions for dementia.

experience to DVMsNMDs to increase the number of veterinarian-scientists working successfully in biomedical
research fields and conducting research around the "One Health" concept for the ultimate benefit of human

title: Determining the neuroplasticity role of inhibitory NDNF neurons in motor execution and learning

title: Spatial-Temporal Dissection of Stratified Host Tissue Responses to Severe acute respiratory syndrome-related coronaviruses in situ to Understand Intra-host Pathogenesis

application_id: 11238063
activity_code: DP2
title: Spatial subcellular mapping of the intestinal LncRNAome
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Current treatments for ulcerative colitis (UC) only address the symptoms or maintain remission of the disease;
thus, new prevention and treatment strategies are urgently needed. Targeting long noncoding RNAs (lncRNAs)
to treat UC is a promising strategy, as lncRNAs have been associated with intestinal inflammation. The proposed
research will identify candidate lncRNAs involved in UC, determine where they are localized in human biopsy
samples and mouse models, and elucidate their function and role in disease pathogenesis, leading to new drug
targets to alleviate the burden of UC and other intestinal diseases.

application_id: 10894215
activity_code: DP2
title: Discovery of bacterial defense and phage counter-defense strategies
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The interplay between anti-phage defense systems in bacteria and counter-defense strategies in phages can determine phage infection outcomes, which in turn have widespread impacts on the evolution and treatment of infectious disease. Most defense and counter-defense systems in nature have not been identified, so we will apply a novel functional selection scheme to identify these systems from various metagenomes and libraries of synthesized phage genes. We will then use genetic and functional techniques to characterize these systems mechanistically and reveal fundamental insights into the molecular arms race between bacteria and phage.

application_id: 11046709
activity_code: K01
title: Molecular Mechanism of Sickle Cell Hepatic Crisis
org_name: VERSITI BLOOD HEALTH, INC.
phr: Project Narrative
Hepatic crisis affects 10-40% of hospitalized sickle cell disease (SCD) patients, which is characterized by liver
injury and cholestasis that can progress to fatal liver failure. The current treatment for hepatic crisis in SCD is
primarily supportive, and the molecular mechanism is largely unknown. This proposal will determine novel
inflammatory and nuclear receptor signaling pathways that may serve as useful therapeutic targets for the
future treatment of Sickle cell hepatobiliary injury.

application_id: 10935638
activity_code: DP1
title: The Genetic and Epigenetic Interplay of Cocaine Addiction: A Cell-Type, Circuitry, and Functional Dissection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This research aims to systematically investigate epigenomic regulation in the reward circuitry following cocaine self-administration, employing a range of single-cell multi-omics and spatial genomics methods. Additionally, by utilizing a diverse panel of mouse strains, the study aims to discern how various genetic backgrounds influence these molecular alterations, to shed light on the complex interplay between genetics and epigenetics in regulating gene expression that modulates cocaine self-administration behavior. The ultimate goal is to construct comprehensive gene regulatory networks that encapsulate this interplay, thereby advancing our understanding and providing avenues for targeted therapeutic strategies to combat cocaine addiction.

title: When actin’s not actin like actin: Nuclear actin impacts transcription and aging
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: Project Narrative  The actin cytoskeleton has been historically studied for its functional role in the cytoplasm, although its existence in the nucleus has been identified over 50 years ago. In this proposal, we aim to study the elusive nuclear actin in multiple fields that it has never been explored before: understanding its function and consequences of its breakdown during aging, investigating how nuclear actin regulation changes during stress, and investigating the impact of nuclear actin on DNA repair and transcriptional regulation. In this administrative supplement, we focus primarily on nuclear actin function in a cell-type specific manner, having a combination of C. elegans and mammalian cell culture experiments to maximize training potential for Dr. Gilberto Garcia.

title: Staphylococcus aureus Cardiac Device Infections - From the Vascular Viewpoint
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Staphylococcus aureus is the leading cause of cardiac device infections, a devastating complication that jeopardizes the function of these lifesaving devices. This research proposal will build novel bioengineered model systems to examine how specific patient and bacterial factors interact to influence why some people with S. aureus bloodstream infection and cardiac devices (pacemakers, artificial heart valves) develop an infection of their cardiac device while others do not. The results from this research can be used to develop new diagnostics to identify patients at high risk for developing a cardiac device infection or new therapeutics to augment a person's ability to fight these infections.

phr: Project Narrative  The objective of this project is to investigate cellular and molecular mechanisms that regulate synovial joint development. application_id: 10904761
activity_code: DP2
title: Decoding the Interferome by Mapping Genetic Interactions in Human Tissue
org_name: COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Supplemental to its classic role as macrophage activating protein, the cytokine IFN-γ has emerged as a critical mediator of barrier immunity, mobilizing elaborate transcriptional programs within non-lymphoid tissues to combat infection. However, the functions, molecular activities, and regulatory circuits of the interferon-stimulated genes (ISGs) that serve as the effectors of this response are largely a mystery. Focusing on the human lung epithelium, I develop an experimental pipeline to map a functional genetic interaction network that underpins the ability of IFN-γ to restrict different classes of pathogens while balancing pathological tissue damage.

application_id: 10916716
activity_code: F31
title: Client and clinician priorities for same-day PrEP and DoxyPEP awareness, uptake, and persistence in primary care.
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Populations of sexual and gender minorities (SGM) are disproportionally impacted by higher rates of human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) and other sexually transmitted infections (STIs). Effective biomedical prevention, such as same day preexposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and doxycycline postexposure prophylaxis (DoxyPEP), are effective strategies to reduce rates of both HIV and other STIs; however, their use in the primary care setting have not been widely explored. This study aims to leverage key stakeholder (e.g., SGM client and primary care clinicians) perspectives to identify factors influencing awareness, uptake, and persistence of same day PrEP and DoxyPEP in the primary care setting.

activity_code: K01
title: Tunneling nanotubes are a novel mechanism for the propagation of HIV infection and neurodegeneration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS MED BR GALVESTON
phr: NARRATIVE The human immune deficiency virus (HIV) affects 38 million people worldwide, and 50% of the people living with HIV (PLWH) show signs of HIV-associated neurocognitive disorder (HAND). Despite effective antiretroviral treatment (ART), viral reservoirs are resistant to treatment and are the major contributors to the cognitive decline in PLWH. We will examine the mechanisms of TNT formation and their contribution to the transfer of viral material and neurotoxic proteins during chronic HIV CNS infection.

application_id: 10828745
activity_code: DP2
title: Translation of evolution-guided insights for new models of human infectious disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: NARRATIVE  Inflammasomes can distinguish between pathogens and non-pathogenic microbes via the detection of ‘patterns-specific activities.’ This proposal uses an evolution-guided framework to translate novel interactions between the human NAIP/NLRC4 and NLRP1 inflammasomes with the human pathogens Shigella flexneri and Dengue virus, respectively, to generate new, rationally-designed models of human infectious disease and immunity.

application_id: 11135537
activity_code: DP2
title: Developing a renewable and dissectible human liver for the study of HBV/HCV infection
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: Project Narrative
Despite the availability of highly effective HBV vaccine and HCV treatment, their infections are still among the
leading causes of chronic liver disease worldwide. We propose to utilize an innovative platform to address
several clinically important but understudied questions including a continued disease progress following cure of
HCV infection, an accelerated disease progression with HBV/HCV co-infection, HBV reactivation following cure
of HCV, and how genetic variants affect the course of disease and treatment. These studies will not only
provide new insights that could inform development of effective diagnostics and treatments for HBV/HCV
infection, but also lead to technology advance that open up new avenues for future studies focusing on other
liver diseases.

application_id: 11078658
activity_code: F31
title: Itaconate mediated control of inflammatory hemophagocyte accumulation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative Maintaining the balance between red blood cell (RBC) production and clearance is critical for maintaining proper oxygenation of the body tissues. Unrestrained differentiation of inflammatory hemophagocytes during Plasmodium infection and chronic Toll-like receptor (TLR) signaling can lead to severe anemia. The goal of this proposal is to uncover the role of the anti-inflammatory immunometabolite itaconate in depleting inflammatory hemophagocytes through cell death mediated mechanisms.

application_id: 10917062
activity_code: K08
title: Mechanisms Determining Dysregulation of the NRF2 Oxidative Stress Response in Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Aberrant activation of the transcription factor NRF2, a key regulator of the cellular oxidative stress response, is frequently seen in HPV negative head and neck squamous cell carcinoma and is associated with a poor prognosis. Functionally validated somatic mutations of this pathway can only account for 30% of the observed activation. The objective of this proposal is to identify and characterize novel genomic and proteomic mechanisms of NRF2 activation in order to advance strategies for the diagnosis and targeted treatment of patients with NRF2 active tumors.

application_id: 10917075
activity_code: F32
title: Modeling gene regulatory mechanisms contributing to the evolution of the human cerebral cortex
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The human cerebral cortex is greatly expanded compared to other primates, which is ultimately caused by genetic changes that affect gene expression regulation and how the human cerebral cortex develops. My goal is to reveal how a specific region of human-specific genetic change and human innovations in gene regulation shape cortical development and evolution, by using novel mouse models. This knowledge of how uniquely human genetic and developmental change affect growth of the cerebral cortex, an important structure for higher-order cognition, is crucial for understanding the etiology of disorders affecting humans, such as neurodevelopmental disorders and cognitive impairment.

application_id: 11257266
activity_code: K02
title: Restoring Cortical Network Function in Rett Syndrome
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project will provide mechanistic insight and novel therapeutic targets for treating cognitive dysfunction in
Rett syndrome, a neurodevelopmental disorder with loss of cognitive, motor and sensory function in early
childhood. The project uses a novel human cerebral organoid model–3D neuronal cultures generated from
induced pluripotent stem cells from patients with Rett syndrome–to study how brain cells form functional
connections in early life and how these networks are altered by the genetic change in Rett syndrome. Our goal
is to identify strategies for restoring cellular-scale brain network activity and development to reduce disability and
enable children and adults with Rett syndrome to more fully participate in their family and community life.

application_id: 10843833
activity_code: K23
title: Targeting inflammation to improve rescue of CFTR by modulator therapy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KANSAS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  The advent of CFTR modulators, a new class of therapies which correct the underlying protein defect in CF, have led to a dramatic improvement in the lives of those with CF. However, underlying airway inflammation persists and currently no studies that have examined the effect of inflammation on response to modulators or trialed therapies to augment response. The knowledge gained in this study will improve understanding of the impact of airway inflammation in the post-modulator era and potentially identify a widely-available, well-tolerated therapy (losartan) to improve response to CFTR modulator therapy.

title: Developing a renewable and dissectible human liver for the study of HBV/HCV infection
org_name: CLEVELAND CLINIC LERNER COM-CWRU
phr: Project Narrative Despite the availability of highly effective HBV vaccine and HCV treatment, their infections are still among the leading causes of chronic liver disease worldwide. We propose to utilize an innovative platform to address several clinically important but understudied questions including a continued disease progress following cure of HCV infection, an accelerated disease progression with HBV/HCV co-infection, HBV reactivation following cure of HCV, and how genetic variants affect the course of disease and treatment. These studies will not only provide new insights that could inform development of effective diagnostics and treatments for HBV/HCV infection, but also lead to technology advance that open up new avenues for future studies focusing on other liver diseases.

application_id: 11137627
activity_code: DP2
title: Identification of mosquito brain neurons that drive blood-feeding behavior
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Mosquitoes spread pathogens to humans through their behavior of blood feeding and identifying and
understanding the neurons that control this behavior would enable new strategies to prevent it. Here, I describe
an approach using genomics, transgenic mosquitoes, and behavior studies to identify the neurons that are
required for mosquitoes to seek out and bite humans. The identification and genetic targeting of these neurons
will create a way to understand what causes the attraction of mosquitoes and open new avenues for its
prevention.

application_id: 10844385
activity_code: K08
title: Characterization of CDKL5 function in host antiviral defense
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative The autophagy pathway plays a key role in antiviral immunity by facilitating the clearance of viral proteins and particles in host cells, but how viruses are specifically targeted for autophagic destruction is still poorly understood. In preliminary studies, this proposal shows a novel autophagy regulator cyclin-dependent kinase- like 5 (CDKL5) that protects mice against lethal infections with neurotropic viruses. The goal of this K08 proposal is to provide a 5-year rigorous training plan for the PI, Dr. Josephine Thinwa to cultivate the skills and resources necessary to build a robust independent research program aimed at investigating the interphase between autophagy and viral pathogens.

application_id: 11237143
activity_code: DP2
title: Revealing the Biophysics of the Germinal Center Microenvironment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS MED BR GALVESTON
phr: Project Narrative
The fact that a small subset of HIV infected individuals develop broadly neutralizing antibodies (bnAbs) capable
of neutralizing 98% of HIV viruses gives substantial promise that an HIV vaccine is possible. However, there has
been a lack of understanding of the biophysical constraints of the germinal center microenvironment (GCME),
which is the arena in which B cells compete to develop into bnAb producing B cells. We aim to uncover the
composition of the extracellular space within the GCME, with the goal of identifying novel biophysical constraints
and metabolic pathways that may be recruited during vaccination to elicit generation of bnAb producing B cells.

application_id: 11079430
activity_code: F31
title: Uncovering the function of emerin and its regulation by secretory trafficking in human cardiomyocytes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy type 1 (EDMD1) is a hereditary muscular dystrophy that causes profound
atrial arrhythmia. EDMD1 is caused by loss-of- function mutations to the nuclear membrane protein emerin, but
how emerin influences atrial cell health remains a mystery. We propose a new model system to study this
disease, and will use this system to define how emerin is regulated by dynamic trafficking through the secretory
pathway in human atrial cells.

application_id: 10877683
activity_code: F32
title: Astrocyte-specific molecular cues guiding retinal angiogenesis
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Retinal vascularization in humans occurs in gestation, reaching completion at 36 weeks, and it is a crucial developmental event involving precisely timed and localized events. Defects in any step of the process or halting it before its full completion will lead to mispatterned or incomplete vascular coverage of the retina, which are hallmarks observed in retinopathologies such as retinopathy of prematurity (ROP). Completion of this work will elucidate unknown mechanisms of glial regulation of retinal angiogenesis, while also pinpointing important molecular cues that may be involved in angiogenesis during pathogenic conditions.

title: MEK PATHWAY INHIBITION COMBINED WITH 5-AMINOLEVULINIC ACID-PHOTODYNAMIC THERAPY FOR THE TREATMENT OF DIFFUSE MIDLINE GLIOMA
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Diffuse midline glioma (DMG) is the leading cause of death among pediatric brain tumors yet disease outcomes have not improved due to the lack of effective treatments and limited diagnostic tools. This proposal uses an innovative, multimodal treatment approach, involving mitogen- activated protein kinase (MEK) inhibition and aminolevulinic acid-photodynamic therapy, that addresses the barriers to successful DMG clinical trials and exploits the molecular composition of DMG cells to reduce morbidity and mortality. If successful, this treatment can be applied to other inoperable central nervous system (CNS) tumors.

application_id: 11163703
activity_code: DP1
title: Developing Tools to Facilitate Dynamics-Based Drug Discovery of Receptors of Drugs of Abuse
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Some drugs of abuse, for example opioids, bind to the same receptor but with significantly differences in the
response and addictive potential in the body. In this grant we propose to develop tools to enable us to use
computer simulations and electron microscope imaging to make ‘movies’ of different drugs of abuse signaling at
their receptors to better understand this behavior. To demonstrate the value of these movies we will use them to
generate new compounds for modulating drug use disorders, and test their effects in rodent models.

org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Pediatric traumatic injury (PTI) is a public health priority, with nearly 300,000 children experiencing injuries that
require hospitalization each year. These children, and their caregivers, are affected in many ways that may
affect quality of life, emotional and behavioral health, physical recovery, family roles and routines, and
academic functioning; yet, US trauma centers do not adequately address these outcomes and a scalable
national model of care for these families is needed. The present research will develop, evaluate, and test
CAARE (Caregivers’ Aid to Accelerate Recovery after pediatric Emergencies), an intervention that was
informed by, and designed for, caregivers of young children after PTI to address the behavioral and emotional
needs of caregivers and childr...

application_id: 11317558
activity_code: F31
title: Insights from Mural Cells for Precision Medicine in Brain Arteriovenous Malformations
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Brain arteriovenous malformations (bAVMs) are a leading cause of intracerebral hemorrhage, yet current
prognostic tools remain imprecise for incidentally discovered bAVMs, limiting effective treatment strategies.
This research will generate a multi-modal molecular atlas of the normal vasculature and bAVMs across age
groups, identifying novel mechanisms of vascular instability and hemorrhage risk while providing a valued
reference atlas for the broader blood-brain barrier field. By advancing precision medicine approaches for
bAVMs, this work has the potential to improve patient stratification and develop targeted therapies, ultimately
reducing the morbidity and mortality associated with these cerebrovascular malformations.

activity_code: DP2
title: Antiviral Discovery in Microbial Eukaryotes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: Project Narrative While much recent research has focused on identifying the antiviral systems of prokaryotes and how those are conserved in metazoans, most eukaryotes are free-living microbes whose antiviral mechanisms remain a mystery like the amoeba species that are home to the enigmatic Megavirales family. Using an approach that combines in silico prediction, discovery-based metatranscriptomic libraries, classical molecular, biochemical, and cellular techniques, and next generation sequencing, this proposal will identify the antiviral machinery of microbial eukaryotes, specifically Acanthamoeba species. This proposal will yield results that impact our understanding of eukaryotic evolution, identify new strategies of viral defense, and provide fresh tools to exploit for biotechnology and...

application_id: 10844379
activity_code: F31
title: Genetic and Transcriptomic Mechanisms of Progressive Ethanol Consumption in the Diversity Outbred Mouse
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative  Alcohol use disorder (AUD) is a major global healthcare burden, but effective therapies are lacking in part due to the complex genetic architecture of alcohol consumption and dependence. A combination of genetic and transcriptomic factors has been proposed as mechanisms underlying variation in alcohol-related behaviors, but these factors are difficult to study in humans due to confounding environmental factors. We hypothesize that by using a novel complex mouse genetic model, we can identify candidate genes and functional pathways which may be involved in the genetic mechanisms affecting ethanol consumption and validate individual candidate genes as potential therapeutic targets by modulating gene expression in a mouse model.

activity_code: K08
title: Intratumoral Administration of CCL21-gene Modified Dendritic Cell With Intravenous Pembrolizumab for Advanced NSCLC
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative
Lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer-related mortality in the United States, leading to more deaths than
the next three cancers combined. Among patients with metastatic disease, median survival is less than 1.5
years, and less than 6% of patients are alive after 5 years. Rational, novel, therapeutic approaches combining
in situ vaccination with immune checkpoint blockade offer the possibility of significant improvements in
outcomes for patients with lung cancer.

application_id: 11136334
activity_code: DP2
title: Using three-dimensional protein networks to uncover immuno-modulatory molecular phenotypes in infectious disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative
I propose to develop and use a novel framework to integrate host genetic data with three-dimensional
structurally-resolved host-pathogen protein networks to uncover immuno-modulatory molecular phenotypes
in infectious disease. The incorporation of structural information provides a significant refinement over the
traditional principle of “guilt-by-association”, and allows us to hone in on specific molecular phenotypes that
modulate infectious disease risk. While the focus of the proposal is to use this approach to infectious diseases,
the framework itself is generalizable and can be used across other immunological disease contexts.

title: The cytokine code and neural circuits for sensing inflammation state
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Many non-brain disorders accompanied by acute or chronic systemic inflammation result in common
behavioral symptoms like extreme fatigue and diminished motivation. The neuro-immune mechanisms

application_id: 10833213
activity_code: DP1
title: Developing a computational platform for induced-fit and chemogenetic drug design
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Small molecules that selectively activate targeted signaling pathways of the μ-opioid receptor (MOR), such as biased/partial agonists and/or chemogenetic probes, can help to disentangle the mechanisms of analgesia versus dangerous side-effects involved in opioid addiction, as well as potentially act as novel pain management drugs without addictive properties. Here, we will develop computer-aided drug discovery tools that can model large-scale induced-fit conformational changes during drug design and simulate chemogenetics design (receptor-ligand co-design), which we will then apply to identify partial and biased agonists of the MOR and MOR-based DREADDs. In silico molecules and DREADD designs will be experimentally validated and enter hit- to-lead optimization.

application_id: 10948374
activity_code: DP2
title: Defining HIV evolution at the innate immune interface
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  HIV evolves to escape the human immune system and drug therapies, but it is constrained by the need to interface with host cell components that allow for infection. This proposal will take an innovative high- throughput approach to comprehensively parse the functional and innate immune-imposed evolutionary constraints on the HIV capsid protein, a critical immune and therapeutic target. These results will reveal how HIV structurally interfaces with and escapes from human innate immunity, and they will allow us to identify viral Achille's heels that can be synergistically constrained by native human immunity and capsid-targeting drugs to limit viral escape, which is a major barrier to effective antiviral therapy.

application_id: 10878750
activity_code: F31
title: Designing synthetic transcription factors to study drug-specific transcription and behaviors
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Transcription factors directly bind DNA to regulate the expression of targeted genes within brain cells, a process that is initiated by drug use. While the expression and function of transcription factors is correlated with addiction, the exact causal contribution of these factors in worsening drug use behaviors often remains unclear. Here, we demonstrate the potential of utilizing re-engineered synthetic transcription factors to query the causal contribution of ZFP189 to the drug-specific phenotype to resolve their exact role in drug-specific transcription and behaviors.

activity_code: DP1
title: Building Bridges to Allow Cross-species Translational genetics for the Study of Addiction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Human and model organism research has generated oceans of genetic and genomic data that can provide insights into the neurobiological mechanisms of addiction. However, we lack the tools to integrate these data across species, which is hindering our ability to make actionable breakthroughs. This project will develop a toolkit for making cross-species integration a routine task; such framework should enable a follow-up of genetic association results with multi-omics investigations of molecular mechanisms, to facilitate their clinical translation and improve diagnosis, monitoring and treatment of addiction.

application_id: 11164812
activity_code: K01
title: Integrating the Social Context, Sensory Functioning, and Inflammation in a Longitudinal Study of Cognitive Aging and Alzheimer's Disease and Related Dementias
org_name: BOSTON COLLEGE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cognitive impairment and Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD) are major public health issues in
the United States that are expected to burden an increasing number of older adults and their families in the
coming decades. This proposal will examine how characteristics of the social context shape trajectories of
cognition and ADRD, focusing on changes in social networks, social activities, and neighborhood social
infrastructure, and the roles of olfactory functioning and inflammation as potential mechanisms. The results of
this project will inform research on the social pathways of sensory and cognitive functioning, and illuminate the
social context as a potential site of intervention for preventing or slowing age-related cognitive decline.

application_id: 11080191
activity_code: F31
title: A New Phase in ABI1 Biology: AR We Abl to Characterize the Intractable AR-DNA-ABI1 Axis?
org_name: UPSTATE MEDICAL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The androgen receptor (AR) plays a major role in transcriptional regulation and cell signaling in normal prostate cells, and is often dysregulated in most prostate cancers (PCa’s), thus AR is targeted for therapeutic treatment in PCa. Recent and emerging experimental data from the Bah-Kotula lab research collaboration has unveiled that Abl kinase-Interactor 1 (ABI1), a multi-isoform and scaffolding cytoskeletal adaptor protein, binds to AR and to chromatin in order to regular gene expression. However, the molecular underpinnings of this critical ternary interaction remain unknown; thus, I will elucidate the biophysical and structural basis governing the AR-ABI1- DNA axis, setting the stage for the development of novel therapeutics against PCa.

org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE

sepsis researchers with a tool that uses a fundamentally different approach to causal effect estimation than

title: Mechanisms of Endosomal Dysfunction at Synapses in A-Synuclein Pathology
org_name: MARINE BIOLOGICAL LABORATORY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Aggregation of α-synuclein at synapses, the functional contacts between neurons, has been linked to cognitive deficits and dementia in Parkinson’s disease and Dementia with Lewy Bodies. Understanding these mechanisms will help advance the development of therapies aimed at improving synapse function in neurodegenerative diseases.

application_id: 11051267
activity_code: F31
title: Role of Phosphorylation in Determining Circadian Period Length and Temperature Compensation
org_name: DARTMOUTH COLLEGE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 A cell-autonomous molecular clock powers the 24-hour cycles of endogenous regulation of physiology,
known as circadian rhythms, which align physiology and behavior to the inherent rotation of the earth.
Dysregulation of circadian rhythms has negative physiological effects, such as causing sleep disorders. Our
project will increase the understanding of the molecular basis of circadian rhythms, informing the etiology of
circadian-based sleep disorders and thereby leading to the development of novel therapies.

activity_code: K08
title: The Impact of Anesthetics on Lymphatic Vessel Contractility and Extracellular Fluid Uptake
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative The lymphatic system is essential for regulating fluid and maintaining tissue water balance, and its dysfunction can result in significant edema. Commonly used anesthetics may impact the lymphatic system and contribute to dangerous edema in critical illness, but this remains largely unexplored. The goal of this research project is to understand how anesthetic medications interact with the lymphatic system, with the potential to guide safer medication choices and improve patient outcomes in surgeries and intensive care settings.

application_id: 11143009
activity_code: DP2
title: Shifting immunodominance of humoral immunity against influenza viruses
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Current influenza viruses do not provide broadly protective immunity due to the immunodominance of humoral
immunity against evolving epitopes of the hemagglutinin head domain rather than against the conserved stalk
domain. In this study, we will rationally design and test a vaccine that shifts immunodominance of humoral
immunity towards the stalk domain by increasing the diversity of head epitopes, which will allow stalk-specific B
cells to more readily acquire antigen and be selected into the long-lived memory B cell pool. Using multiple
models of preexisting immunity, our study will reveal that increasing head epitope diversity augments broadly
protective humoral immunity.
