application_id: 10844487
activity_code: K99
title: Maternal stress and undernutrition: Interactive effects on newborn and child outcomes in Ethiopia”
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed K99/R00 study will use a clinical nutrition intervention trial in rural Ethiopia to determine interactive effects of maternal prenatal stress and nutrition intervention on newborn weight-for-age and stress-sensitive child outcomes related to visual attention, memory, stress reactivity, and socio-emotional functioning at 36 months postpartum. We will also examine whether maternal and newborn telomere length serves as biomarkers of the biologic mechanism underling hypothesized associations. Insight from this project will cast light on the importance of antenatal intervention to reduce commonly co-occurring maternal prenatal stress and undernutrition among millions of women residing in low- resource settings.

application_id: 10949165
activity_code: DP2
title: Spatial and Temporal Resolution of EosinophilSpecialization in Allergic Microenvironments
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: NARRATIVE Eosinophils are a key type of white blood cell in allergic disease that were historically characterized as only promoting inflammation, but compelling evidence is emerging that eosinophils have a variety of functions in distinct tissue environments. This proposal will research the range of eosinophil functions during allergic disease, which may improve the understanding of how eosinophil-depleting drugs affect human health. This work may substantially expand the role of eosinophils to include resolving allergic inflammation and may inform strategies to bring personalized and predictive medicine to the treatment of allergic, inflammatory, and autoimmune diseases.

application_id: 11139601
activity_code: DP2
title: Dissecting microbiota-driven lymphangiogenesis in immune health and disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative:
The gut houses billions of resident microbes, including bacteria, viruses, and fungi that actively
support human health; however, when unbalanced, these microbes can activate various
immune responses that cause disease or increase disease severity, such as cancer,
autoimmunity and infection. This proposal seeks to examine how gut microbes impact health
and disease through immune cell activation and immune structure formation. Understanding the
mechanisms by which the gut microbiota support these immune structures during disease will
contribute greatly to our ability to treat patients with chronic difficult to treat diseases, including
cancer, autoimmunity, and cancer.

The proposed research is relevant to human health because Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) is the number
one infectious disease killer in the world. The ability of innate immune cells to kill or control Mtb infection is critical
for disease outcomes. Therefore, I aim to understand how this process is regulated in hopes of one day
developing therapeutics designed to enhance macrophages' antibacterial responses and control infection in
human patients. The proposed research is relevant to the part of NIH's mission that pertains to making
fundamental discoveries that will help prevent and treat disease.

application_id: 10844615
activity_code: F32
title: Gene expression and functional evolution in the Drosophila female reproductive tract
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT DAVIS
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Despite their importance for every aspect of reproduction, the somatic tissues of the Drosophila female reproductive tract are relatively understudied. This work will use single-cell RNA- sequencing of mated and unmated females from two diverged natural populations to characterize reproductive tract cell diversity and gene expression evolution for the first time. Next, a set of reproductive tract genes will be knocked out and evaluated for reproductive function, including a) receptors in the seminal receptacle which may mediate the female post- mating response and b) newly-originated genes.

application_id: 10844822
activity_code: T32
title: Musculoskeletal Research Training Program
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Musculoskeletal diseases are some of the most common reasons why people of all ages visit a doctor and are leading causes of disability around the globe. In the United States, 1 of every 7 people reports a musculoskeletal impairment and musculoskeletal conditions cost our society an estimated $269.3B every year. The prevalence of musculoskeletal conditions will continue to escalate in the next 20 years as the population ages. This trans- disciplinary program provides focused training to a new generation of biomedical researchers and physicians who will advance the science and treatment of arthritis and musculoskeletal diseases.

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The primary goal of the Research Training in Neuroendocrinology and Metabolism Program at the University of Virginia is to prepare early-postdoctoral fellows for future research careers in the various fields of metabolism and neuroendocrinology. This investment promises availability of the next generation of scientists and investigators that would help fashion newer treatment strategies for a host of important health problems such as obesity, diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular illnesses, neurological ailments, renal disorders and infertility.

title: Cell-Types Specific Neuroadaptations in the Nucleus Accumbens Shell Associated with Individual Differences in Cocaine-Seeking
org_name: SOUTHERN ILLINOIS UNIVERSITY SCH OF MED
phr: Project Narrative There are currently no effective treatment options for cocaine addiction. While some of the risk factors and neural substrates involved have been elucidated, our limited understanding has impeded the development of effective treatments for this brain disorder. In this proposal, we examine cellular substrates associate with cocaine aversion, whose elucidation may help develop more effective therapies against this devastating condition.

application_id: 11031167
activity_code: K99
title: Distributed Neural Activity Patterns Underlying Practice-Based Learning
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE People and animals learn associations between sensory cues and motor actions through trial and error to survive in a changing environment, yet, in some cases, people act on harmful learned associations, for example, in addiction, PTSD, OCD, autism and anxiety. We know that such cue-action learning requires a “rewiring” of brain circuits, i.e., synaptic plasticity between the neurons representing the sensory cue and the neurons representing the motor action, but we still do not know where in the brain this associative synaptic plasticity occurs. Here I propose to apply an innovative behavioral paradigm and optogenetic tools to dissect a candidate pathway underlying the learned association between a cue that is the activation of visual cortex and a forelimb motor action.

application_id: 11127763
activity_code: R00
title: De novo design of functional metallocofactor-binding proteins
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA RIVERSIDE
phr: Project Narrative
Metalloproteins perform a wide range of chemical transformations whose functions are imperative for
maintaining human health. The entire protein scaffold that houses the metallocofactor is responsible for
controlling active site geometry, electronic structure of the metal center, reaction pathway, and substrate
specificity. The ability to design proteins from scratch that bind metallocofactors and control substrate
orientation will establish key structure-function relationships that govern the function and selectivity of
efficient metalloenzymes.

application_id: 10845295
activity_code: DP2
title: Differential regulation of T cell plasma membrane proteins by N-glycan branching and clathrin-mediated endocytosis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The N-glycan branching pathway controls immunity and inflammation by controlling turnover rates of proteins on the surface of T cells. However, which proteins are regulated in this manner, and how this pathway promotes expression of some proteins while inhibiting expression of others is unknown. We propose using a quantitative proteomic approach to analyze all surface proteins simultaneously and to examine the hypothesis that these extracellular glycans cooperate with intracellular endocytosis machinery to differentially regulate cell- surface protein turnover.

application_id: 11137588
activity_code: DP2
title: The evolutionary landscape of HIV broadly neutralizing antibody development
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Broadly neutralizing antibodies are a necessary component of an effective HIV vaccine, but an incomplete
understanding of broadly neutralizing antibody development has impeded attempts to elicit these desirable
antibody responses via vaccination. Here, I use high-throughput mutational scanning techniques to elucidate
the molecular evolutionary forces and constraints that guide HIV antibody development. These detailed
mutational maps will enable the design of improved HIV vaccine immunogens and therapeutic antibodies and
reveal the general principles of broadly neutralizing antibody function and development with relevance for
various pathogens where broad antibody responses are necessary for pathogen control.

application_id: 11194256
activity_code: DP5
title: Predictive modeling of viral RNA cellular behavior
org_name: SCRIPPS RESEARCH INSTITUTE, THE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
RNA-protein interactions are essential to fundamental biological processes in humans and pathogens, and as
such represent important drug targets. Structural details of these interactions are easily obtained; however, we
do not yet have a predictive understanding of their biological functions. This proposal aims to investigate the
structural dynamics of an RNA-protein interaction in human immunodeficiency virus 1 (HIV-1) in order to build
a first of its kind quantitative model of its cellular function, as well as target it for antiviral drug development.

application_id: 10903924
activity_code: DP2
title: Dissecting the impact of immune environment on Candida albicans pathogenic potential in the gut
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Candida albicans is a commensal fungus that colonizes the intestine of most healthy people but has the capacity to cause infections and exacerbate inflammation in susceptible people. Immune status determines risk for C. albicans-associated disease, but the impact of immune environment on C. albicans inherent pathogenic potential is not well understood. This proposal aims to comprehensively define the impact both homeostatic and pathogenic immune environments on C. albicans pathogenic potential.

org_name: SCRIPPS RESEARCH INSTITUTE, THE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE RNA-protein interactions are essential to fundamental biological processes in humans and pathogens, and as such represent important drug targets. application_id: 11081615
activity_code: F30
title: Elucidating the spatially coordinated mechanisms of transcriptional silencing in fragile X syndrome
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Fragile X syndrome (FXS), the most common form of heritable intellectual disability, is caused by the expansion of a CGG short tandem repeat (STR) at the FMR1 gene on chromosome X where local DNA methylation silences FMR1 expression. At the FMR1 locus and on multiple autosomes, we have recently found Megabase-scale H3K9me3 domains that form inter-chromosomal contacts in FXS. Using CRISPR/Cas9 engineering of the CGG STR, genomic assays and multimodal single nuclei-imaging, I will elucidate how expansion of the CGG STR at FMR1 spatially coordinates heterochromatinization and 3D genome miswiring.

title: Early Stage Training in the Neurosciences
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
Understanding and successfully treating disorders of the brain and nervous system is critically
important due to the major impact of neurological and psychiatric disease on public health and
the losses in productivity that result. This broadly based training program is designed to provide
a solid foundation in neuroscience, from molecules to circuits and systems, in combination with a
strong research training using the newest research tools and approaches to understand the
complexities of the brain. The purpose of this renewal is to provide outstanding individualized
training to talented predoctoral students who will devote their careers to address key challenges
in both basic as well as translational neuroscience.

activity_code: DP2
title: Essential functions of the mitochondrion in malaria parasites
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In the face of increasing resistance to frontline antimalarials, there is a critical need for new drugs that can act against both human and mosquito stages of malaria infection. The highly divergent mitochondrion of the malaria parasite is an attractive drug target, but harnessing its full potential requires in-depth understanding of the organelle’s biology and metabolic pathways. This project aims to identify core functions of the mitochondrion that are likely to be essential across all life stages of the parasite.

application_id: 11141023
activity_code: DP1
title: The role of DNA breaks and repairs in opioid addiction
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KANSAS LAWRENCE
phr: Project Narrative
DNA breaks and repairs have been linked to opioid use disorders. To elucidate the mechanism for this, we
propose to characterize the changes in DNA damage and repair sites and the associated changes in chromatin
accessibility and gene expression in specific types of neurons in mice that self-administered heroin. Our study
will therefore provide a novel mechanism for the epigenetic regulation of the transcriptional and behavioral
maladaptations underlying opioid addiction, which will fundamentally advance the understanding of the
neurobiology of drug addiction.

title: Dissecting the thermosensory biology of soil-transmitted parasitic nematodes
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Soil-transmitted parasitic nematodes infect over a billion people around the world and can cause devastating
and sometimes fatal illness, but their basic biology remains almost entirely unexplored. The proposed research
will investigate how temperature cues shape the specialized behavioral and physiological responses of parasitic
nematodes. Our results will elucidate the key molecular and cellular mechanisms that underlie the remarkable
thermal adaptability of mammalian parasitic nematodes, establishing an essential foundation to enable the
development of novel interventional strategies.

activity_code: DP2
title: Harnessing Broad-Specificity Phage Recombinases for Universal Bacterial Editing platforms
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This research aims to identify broad-specificity phage-recombination systems that can facilitate straightforward and efficient genome editing across a diverse spectrum of human pathogens. This approach offers an innovative means of pinpointing the genetic determinants of bacterial pathogenicity. By comprehending how a bacterium's genetic makeup determines its capacity to cause disease, we can uncover novel strategies to counteract bacterial threats to human health.

application_id: 11140343
activity_code: DP2
title: Parallel phenotyping to dissect genetic determinants of bacterial strain diversity
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Mycobacterium avium is an environmental bacterium that is capable of infecting humans and causing serious
disease, requiring months to years of antibiotic treatment to cure. This proposal aims to use novel genetic tools
to identify the bacterial genes that allow some strains of M. avium to establish chronic infection, and the
pathways that allow M. avium to tolerate prolonged antibiotic exposure once infection is established. In doing
so, this work will provide fundamental insights into how environmental bacteria evolve into pathogens, and lay
a foundation for the development of improved therapies for this and other chronic bacterial infections.

application_id: 11000314
activity_code: DP2
title: Partial maturation in mosquito-borne flaviviruses: developing new approaches to characterize the role of lattice heterogeneity in fusion, infectivity, and antibody neutralization
org_name: PURDUE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Mosquito-borne flaviviruses present a significant global health burden, with Dengue, West Nile and Zika viruses causing millions of infections and thousands of deaths annually. A better understanding of the viral surface will aid structure-based pharmaceutical design efforts to develop more effective treatments and safer vaccines. This proposal describes research to characterize poorly understood heterogeneity in the viral surface lattice of E proteins, combining novel technologies and analyses to improve structural models and antibody neutralization stoichiometry predictions, and clearly establishing which lattice arrangements are most important for infectivity.

title: Connecting the Spatiotemporal Organization of Gut Bacterial Communities to the Emergence and Spread of Antibiotic Resistance
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Antibiotic resistant bacteria are a growing global threat to human health and wellbeing. I propose to
investigate how the spatiotemporal organization and physiology of gut bacteria could be targeted to stop the
emergence and spread of antibiotic resistance. This work will inform the design of new strategies for
combating antibiotic resistance through the therapeutic manipulation of the intestinal microbiome’s physical
architecture.

application_id: 11127773
activity_code: R38
title: Tri-I Stimulating Access to Research in Residency program (Tri-I StARR - NIAID)
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative:

Physician-scientists have repeatedly made critical contributions to our understanding of human health and disease that have had an outsized impact on patient and population level health. However, current clinical training programs have made the physician-scientist career path less and less accessible. This program seeks to restore the pipeline of physician-scientists focused on disorders of infection, immunity and inflammation by creating a new program that integrates clinical and scientific training in a vocationally structured manner.

application_id: 11174477
activity_code: DP5
title: Characterizing a Common Degradation Pathway for Nuclear Hormone Receptors
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative
Nuclear Hormone Receptors (NRs) are a structurally related family of transcription factors that are valuable
targets in inflammation and cancer therapies. This proposal seeks to better understand a common ubiquitin-
proteasome dependent degradation mechanism governing NRs and its role in transcriptional regulation. The
long-term goal is to interrogate the relationship between and decouple hormone induced NR activation and
degradation; we will first conduct basic research in biochemistry to understand the interactions between E3
ligases and NRs, and test the feasibility of this overall approach in cellular models.

application_id: 10825628
activity_code: DP1
title: Development of High-Affinity and Selective Ligands as a Pharmacological Tool for the Dopamine D4 Receptor (D4R) Subtype Variants
org_name: HIGH POINT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The ultimate goal of this work is to discover candidate medications and develop new molecular tools to study the dopamine D4 receptor (D4R) and its variants, the more common D4.4R variant, and the ADHD- and substance use disorder-associated D4.7R minor variant. Developing new molecular tools to elucidate the role of D4R variant signaling through the discovery of D4.4R- and D4.7R-selective compounds can untangle the important signaling effects of this receptor and identify potential targets for medications development for ADHD and substance use disorders.

application_id: 10913470
activity_code: DP5
title: Dissecting the interaction between DNA damage repair defects and the tumor microenvironment
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 Up to 80% of high grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) patients succumb to their disease due to both
lack of early detection and limited therapeutic options. Genomic analysis of these tumors has suggested that
up to 50% may have difficulty in repairing damage to their DNA which might make them therapeutically
sensitive to a variety of available drugs; and functional analyses of HGSC tumor cells has revealed that many
tumor cells are defective in repairing specific damage generated when the cells duplicate their DNA. The work
in this proposal will help to better understand how HGSC tumor cells with defects in repairing this type of
damage interact with the surrounding normal cells, and how these interactions lead to sensitivity and
resistance to certain therapies.

application_id: 10844832
activity_code: T32
title: Child & Adolescent Mental Health Research
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Research innovations in the field of child mental health conducted by specially trained clinician- scientists are desperately needed to adequately respond to the unprecedented youth mental health crisis in the United States. This T32 training program will build upon previous success in recruiting, engaging, and mentoring a diverse group of scientists who can advance the field in order to reduce the burden of child mental disorders. The program is designed to target current priorities in child mental health research, including: improving our understanding of pathogenesis, course and treatment of child mental health disorders; enhancing health equity; utilizing technological advances to enhance knowledge and care; and partnering with communities in the conduct of research.
terms: adolescent mental health;Child;Research

application_id: 10845374
activity_code: K23
title: Radiomics signatures and patient outcomes in intracerebral hemorrhage
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) is a severely debilitating cerebrovascular disease, which affects over 70,000 patients per year in the U.S., and is responsible for over half of stroke-related disability worldwide. So far, the diagnosis, surveillance, and prediction of outcome in intracerebral hemorrhage have mainly relied on visual and subjective assessment of head CT scans. Computerized assessment of medical images and machine learning algorithms can extract hidden features from CT scans and provide innovative tools for accurate outcome prediction and personalized treatment decisions in patients with hemorrhagic stroke.

application_id: 11210863
activity_code: DP2
title: Unveiling the hidden regulators: deciphering endogenous retroelement control of allergic Type 2 immune response
org_name: GEORGE WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE
Pulmonary allergic diseases, including asthma, represent a growing global health challenge, with over 262 million
cases linked to common aeroallergens like house dust mites (HDM). This study aims to uncover the role of
endogenous retroviruses (ERVs) in initiating HDM-induced Type 2 immune responses at mucosal barriers,
hypothesizing that their reactivation during allergen sensitization enhances allergic inflammation. By utilizing
advanced next-generation sequencing analysis of the transcriptome and epigenome, conditional knockout
mouse models, and in vitro models of human and mouse cell lines, we will elucidate how ERVs influence Type
2 immunity, ultimately paving the way for new therapeutic strategies against asthma and related conditions.

application_id: 11167748
activity_code: DP2
title: Evolution of heat tolerance and drug resistance in Cryptococcus
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF GEORGIA
phr: Narrative
Many pathogens live in the environment prior to encountering a mammalian host. As a result, human
interactions with the environment can alter the population of pathogens we are likely to encounter and thus
with which we are infected. This proposal aims to understand how heat tolerance and drug resistance are
controlled by genetic variation at two different time scales (across a genus and within a species) of
opportunistic environmental fungal pathogens in order to better predict how pathogens are likely to be altered
by human activities.

title: HWW

activity_code: DP1
title: Functional convergence following disruption of diverse genes associated with cannabis use and major depression
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative In the United States, millions of people use cannabis and there is now convincing evidence linking its use with depression and suicidal behavior. By combining human postmortem genomics, MPRA genetic engineering, and human induced pluripotent stem cell derived brain organoids, we propose to test the molecular and functional impact of cannabis use perturbing the genotype of MDD risk alleles. We hope to find insights into the mechanism of disease initiation and progression of MDD in people with substance use disorders.

application_id: 11101241
activity_code: DP2
title: Immune regulation of tissue iron in health and disease
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Anemia is a frequent complication of inflammatory diseases marked by dysregulated iron homeostasis.
Inflammation-associated anemia is often refractory to treatment, and we lack a basic understanding of how
inflammation and anemia influence each other. The focus of this proposal is to define the role of a novel
immune-mediated pathway of iron regulation in the course and resolution of diseases involving inflammation.

title: AGTC: Attaining Genomics Talent Cloud through Undergraduate Research Education
org_name: TENNESSEE STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Minorities and disadvantaged populations are significantly underrepresented in genomics and biomedical research professions. The AGTC (Attaining Genomics Talent Cloud) through Undergraduate Research Experience Program, a joint proposal from Tennessee State University and Vanderbilt University Medical Center, seeks to address this underrepresentation through excellent didactic training, mentoring and near-peer mentoring by Tennessee State University and Vanderbilt faculty and students, and a rich research experience at Vanderbilt. The outcome of project is that trainees will persist in genomics and biomedical research and contribute to workforce diversity.

application_id: 11031962
activity_code: T32
title: Translational research training in cardiovascular science
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Surgeon-scientists in training have competing clinical responsibilities but must compete for contentious funding lines, which means that state-of-the art training is critical for producing the best surgeon-scientists. With the aging of the population, the number of patients with cardiovascular disease is markedly increasing. The proposed program, with its overarching focus on vascular biology and three distinct tracks including (1) basic/translational, (2) health sciences and (3) biomedical engineering, under multi-disciplinary preceptor guidance, will produce the next generation of surgeon-scientist leaders who will pave the way to new discoveries in human health.

activity_code: K00
title: The Effects of Obesity on Limiting Brain Health and Stroke Recovery
org_name: MEDICAL UNIVERSITY OF SOUTH CAROLINA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE After a stroke, individuals with sensory impairments have high instances of balance problems, falls, and lower extremity dysfunction, implicating the role of sensorimotor reorganization (i.e., plasticity). Here, we take an initial step in characterizing post-stroke sensorimotor plasticity and its relationship to walking using paired associative stimulation. The proposed work will lay the foundation for a career in which I aim to better inform lower extremity rehabilitation efforts by providing functional biomarkers as targets for modulation, and structural neural correlates to improve prognoses.

application_id: 10912669
activity_code: DP5
title: Targeting Clonal Hematopoiesis of Indeterminate Potential using Human Genetics
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CENTER
phr: Project Narrative More than 10% of adults over 70, have a somatic mutation in their blood stem cells, a newly identified disease termed ‘Clonal Hematopoiesis of Indeterminate Potential’ (CHIP), which increases blood cancer risk by 10-fold, heart attack risk by 2-fold and risk of death by 1.5-fold. To understand the fundamental causes and disease consequences of CHIP, we will perform human genomic analyses of hundreds of thousands of individuals with and without CHIP. Our work identifying the causes of CHIP may lead to the first treatment for patients with CHIP that would prevent multiple age-associated diseases.

application_id: 10910581
activity_code: DP2
title: Expanding CAR T cell applications through high-throughput forward- and reverse immune engineering
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Immunotherapies have revolutionized the treatment of cancer, but only a small fraction of patients benefit from
these therapies. In my recent work, I pioneered a new class of chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cells, peptide-
centric (PC)-CARs, paving the way for a clinical trial slated to open in 2024 and offering a roadmap for
developing immunotherapies for the lethal cancers that don't benefit from first-generation CAR T cells. Here I
introduce a multi-pronged approach to build on these methods, offering innovative new strategies for
developing safer and more effective therapeutic strategies that can to lead to new therapies for the most
challenging cancers.

title: Identifying Endogenous Retroviral Factors in Viral Lymphomagenesis
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Virus-associated malignancies may account for an estimated 15 percent of all human cancers worldwide, which represents a significant component of the global cancer burden. Both HIV and EBV have documented associations with a number of malignancies and people living with HIV are at a higher risk of developing cancer despite antiretroviral treatments. This study aims to identify and characterize novel interactions between exogenous viral factors and endogenous retroviral oncogenes that play an essential role in virus-associated cancers.

application_id: 11139644
activity_code: DP1
title: DEVELOPING GPCR MODULATORS OF SOMATOSTATIN INTERNEURONS FOR THE TREATMENT OF OPIOID USE DISORDER
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Approved medications and available treatments for opioid use disorder (OUD) do not adequately address
persistent symptoms that can lead to relapse; thus, we desperately need to identify new molecular targets for
medication development. In this proposal, we will identify targets expressed by a specific subtype of neuron
within prefrontal cortex that has been implicated and regulating motivated and affective behaviors. We will use
a combination of transcriptomics, electrophysiology, pharmacology, and genetic engineering to define how
candidate targets regulate brain function and opioid use behaviors.

title: Dendritic morphology, patterns of input, and calcium signal heterogeneity in a novel subpopulation of cerebellar Purkinje cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative Current theories of how cerebellar cortical circuitry optimizes motor, cognitive, and emotional performance rely on two core assumptions: 1) circuit connectivity is rigidly conserved and stereotypical across cells and regions; and 2) Purkinje cell (PC) dendrites act as one homogeneous unit to process information. Challenging both assumptions, this proposal describes a PC subpopulation with segregated dendritic compartments that receive distinct inputs and exhibit heterogeneous signaling that influences PC computations and output. application_id: 10949070
activity_code: K00
title: Keratin and filament cell-cell junction cooperation in developmental and tumorigenic fate decisions
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Cancers arising from epithelial tissues, including skin, breast, lung, and colon cancers, represent the majority of the cancer burden in the United States. Epithelial cells are normally able to constrain cancer formation - despite continuously regenerating throughout life - by sensing the mechanical properties of the surrounding cells and connective tissue. This project aims to determine a fundamental aspect of how epithelial cells sense mechanical forces to balance cell division and tissue specification to better understand how epithelial cancers form.

application_id: 10912515
activity_code: DP5
title: Defining malignant hematopoiesis via single-cell multi-omics
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Clonal blood disorders exhibit overproduction or underproduction of blood components and develop upon the acquisition of genetic mutations in genes involved in cell signaling and other cellular processes. Recently, we developed a novel tool that can sequence many genes in individual cells (‘single-cell sequencing’), by which we demonstrated that these mutations alter the cells uniquely in different types of blood cells. Now, we seek to study how other factors that are critical for blood cell development, like modifiers of DNA (‘epigenetics’) and the bone marrow environment, affect the changes induced by the gene mutations to impact clinical pictures, by developing and applying innovative single-cell sequencing technologies.

application_id: 10844563
activity_code: R00
title: Synergistic effect of maternal insulin-resistance and cortisol in pregnancy on fetal programming of child mitochondrial function and obesity risk
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: The overall goal of this application is to provide Dr. Lauren Gyllenhammer support and protected time for an intensive, 
supervised career development experience in the biomedical, and developmental sciences, leading to research independence. Based on a rigorous research question and methodology she has developed, Dr. Gyllenhammer will conduct 
trans-disciplinary research to investigate the potential for maternal prenatal stress to potentiate the effects of maternal 
insulin resistance on the development of offspring adiposity and mitochondrial health. This application describes her

application_id: 10932120
activity_code: DP5
title: Therapeutic Potential of a Novel MGMT-Dependent DNA Interstrand Crosslinking Agent in the Treatment of DNA Repair Deficient Cancer
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE DNA repair abnormalities occur frequently across human cancer types, presenting the potential for targeted tumor treatment via synthetic lethal approaches. We recently discovered a novel DNA modifying agent which generates DNA interstrand crosslinks specifically in tumor cells lacking the DNA direct repair enzyme O6- methylguanine-DNA methyltransferase (MGMT) and is effective in temozolomide (TMZ)-resistant glioma models. This proposal seeks to understand the therapeutic potential of this compound in a broader array of MGMT-deficient cancers, specifically by targeting additional defects in interstrand crosslink repair and by circumventing TMZ resistance.

activity_code: DP5
title: Novel Functional Anatomic and Biomarker Indices of Radiation-Induced Female Sexual Toxicities in a Multi-Center Cohort
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: Project Narrative The objective of this study is to define novel dosimetric predictors of female sexual dysfunction and to identify quantitative imaging and microbiome-based biomarker indices associated with radiation damage to specific sexual organs using a classification of sexual dysfunction structured on functional anatomic domains (dermatologic, vaginal and erectile). Towards this end, we will conduct an observational prospective cohort study of 110 female patients treated with pelvic radiation at institutions in New York City, Atlanta, and Houston. Our results will rapidly provide data and inform innovative, personalized interventions to preserve female sexual function or mitigate the effects of radiation in this understudied population.

application_id: 10923236
activity_code: DP5
title: Spatial Regulation of Epigenetic Memory
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: The mechanistic relationship between 3D genome folding, chromatin dynamics, and stability of epigenetic memory is unclear due to challenges of simultaneously measuring these parameters in single cells. As such, we have a limited understanding of how chromatin folding impacts epigenetic stability and how this effects development processes and disease. Drawing on recent advances in multiplexed microscopy and super resolution imaging, I aim to develop new imaging technologies to study the dynamic feedback between chromatin folding and epigenetic memory across time scales.

org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL

application_id: 11185297
activity_code: DP5
title: Molecular Mechanisms of Mutant IDH inhibition and DNA Damage in IDH-mutant Glioma
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative:
Gliomas are the most common primary brain tumors in adults, a subset of which harbor mutations in the
metabolic gene isocitrate dehydrogenase 1 (IDH1). My project uses a novel genetically engineered mouse model
of mutant IDH1-driven astrocytoma and other models to understand how mutant IDH inhibitors exert efficacy in
glioma and how mutant IDH1 alters response to DNA damage. These studies will address clinically relevant
questions that will inform use of mutant IDH inhibitors in the clinic and nominate combination treatment strategies
for patients with IDH-mutant gliomas.

application_id: 10914125
activity_code: DP5
title: The role of non-nutritive sweeteners-microbiome interactions in the pathogenesis of metabolic syndrome
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Consumption of non-nutritive sweeteners (NNS) is a popular dietary approach, yet epidemiological evidence suggest that NNS promote, rather than prevent, obesity and hyperglycemia. In my graduate work I demonstrated that NNS promote weight gain and glucose intolerance by disrupting the microbiome, yet the underlying mechanisms remain unclear. This work will seek to identify the mechanisms through which NNS shape the microbiome, and the NNS-perturbed microbiome affects host metabolic health.

application_id: 11186789
activity_code: DP5
title: Decoding the beat: physiological control mechanisms for robust heart development
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The maturation of form and function must proceed in lockstep for robust organ development, but the mechanisms
of this coupling are with few exceptions understudied. Calcium dynamics are a prominent part of the heart’s
physiology, and their dysregulation is implicated in cardiac disease, but their role in the progression of normal
cardiac development is poorly understood. This proposal will investigate the encoding rules that map emerging
calcium physiology of the embryonic zebrafish heart to downstream decisions important for its development,
using innovative technologies for quantitative, spatiotemporally resolved measurement and control of cardiac
physiology.

title: Postdoctoral Training in Reproductive Mood Disorders

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Among females, reproductive mood disorders (peripubertal depression, postpartum depression, premenstrual dysphoric disorder, and perimenopausal depression) are prevalent and associated with substantial morbidity and mortality. application_id: 10921797
activity_code: DP5
title: Response and resistance to chimeric antigen receptor T cell therapy in human solid tumors using spatial multi-omics
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project narrative  Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy has revolutionized the treatment of blood cancer, but it has yet to demonstrate efficacy in solid tumors, and our understanding of barriers is limited. I have developed an integrated spatial multi-omic analytical pipeline that I will apply to a comprehensive biobank from five CAR T- cell clinical trials to define the tumor microenvironment with greater precision than previously possible. My preliminary data suggest that CAR T-cell therapy fundamentally reorganizes the solid tumor microenvironment, and my proposed work will reveal mechanisms of resistance and response to guide the development of next- generation CAR T-cell therapies.

application_id: 11181994
activity_code: DP5
title: Multicomponent hydrogels activating wound-induced hair neogenesis for scarless wound healing.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
Scarring in the skin is associated with significant morbidity and characterized by the loss of the hair
follicle, which has crucial roles in regeneration. In mice, wounds can heal with hair follicle regeneration
without scarring through a unique pathway known as wound-induced hair neogenesis. The objective of
this research is to engineer a complex hydrogel system that releases two important, sequential signals
to mimic the biology of wound-induced hair neogenesis as a mechanism to prevent and treat human
skin scars.

activity_code: DP5
title: High-throughput imaging of 3D chromatin regulation events in the nervous system
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative This project aims to establish a multiplexed imaging and synthetic biology platform to mechanistically understand chromatin regulation and its functional role in the peripheral olfactory system. Specifically, Aim 1 is to develop a high-throughput, multi-modal imaging method to directly visualize the 3D organization of DNA together with key regulatory proteins and the transcriptional output controlling the fates of single-neurons in the olfactory epithelium. Aim 2, is to deliver and image large libraries of compact viruses containing different combinations of regulatory elements and gene promoters to enable specific transgenic control of the different sub-populations of olfactory neuronal types.

application_id: 10923999
activity_code: F31
title: Topoisomerase 1-catalyzed genomic ribonucleotide excision, its regulation, and its implication in transcription.
org_name: TRUSTEES OF INDIANA UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE:  Ribonucleotides are being constantly incorporated into the genome by eukaryotic DNA polymerases. This project will investigate the processing of these genomic ribonucleotides by topoisomerase 1 (Top1) and the possible ramifications of this Top1 activity for transcription by introducing a novel mechanism for DNA supercoil relaxation by Top1. Our proposal will also provide insight into the mechanism of PARP1 regulation of genomic ribonucleotide excision by Top1, which will help us understand the way human cells respond to the lesions that arise from Top1 cleavage of genomic ribonucleotides.

activity_code: DP5
title: MDMA as a Treatment for Social Deficits in Schizophrenia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA LOS ANGELES
phr: Project Narrative The goal of this project is to test the prosocial compound ± 3,4-methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA) as a novel treatment for a debilitating symptom of schizophrenia, impaired social motivation. This project will proceed in two phases; the aim of Phase 1 will be to establish the tolerability of MDMA in this patient population, and the aim of Phase 2 will be to test the effects of MDMA on social motivation. Results from these studies will be foundational in identifying effective psychopharmacologic and combined psychopharmacologic- psychosocial therapies for social deficits in schizophrenia.

application_id: 10918091
activity_code: DP5
title: Cellular phenotypic heterogeneity and resistance to radiotherapy in pancreatic adenocarcinoma
org_name: OHIO STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Cellular phenotypic heterogeneity within tumors, such as subpopulations of cells with increased capacity for self-renewal referred to as cancer stem cells, exists within many cancers is believed to drive resistance to therapies such and radiation and chemotherapy. Our preliminary results in pancreatic cancer revealed a supportive niche of Wnt-secreting cancer cells that drives a Lgr5+ stem-cell-like state in another cancer cell subpopulation, thereby promoting and maintaining stemness within pancreatic tumors. This proposal will test the hypothesis that Wnt-driven cellular phenotypic heterogeneity and stemness promote radioresistance in pancreatic tumors.

application_id: 10846261
activity_code: T32
title: Biology of Aging and Age-Related Diseases
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Project Narrative The major mission of this T32 is to train dedicated, motivated, and well-qualified predoctoral and postdoctoral fellows in aging biology and translational gerontology research. Given the projected increase in the number of older adults living in the US by 2050, it is critical that significant advances be made toward understanding the biology of aging and related diseases, and that these findings translate into enhanced care of older patients. The present T32 Program will provide state-of-the-art training in aging biology research to talented scientists, preparing them for academic careers at institutions throughout the country and equipping them to make novel contributions toward understanding the biology and enhancing management of disabling diseases of aging.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE There are no targeted treatments for the dysregulated autonomic responses (‘dysautonomia’) of autism; yet, these disruptive physiologic responses that consist of alterations in heart rate, blood pressure, and perspiration affect over 70% of autistic individuals and contribute to significant disability. Building on a novel finding that the cerebellum, a brain region affected in autism, contains specific sites that control autonomic output, the proposed research will determine which cerebellar sites selectively influence autonomic physiologic responses and identify which sites are altered in a model of autism. title: A machine-learning platform to illuminate the chemical dark matter in mass spectrometry-based metabolomics
org_name: PRINCETON UNIVERSITY
phr: 8. PROJECT NARRATIVE –––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––– Whereas scientists can now readily measure the DNA, RNA, and protein content within any biological sample, measuring the small molecules in that same sample has remained more challenging. This project aims to develop a suite of machine-learning tools that will allow us to more comprehensively measure both known and unknown small molecules in any biological sample by analyzing mass spectrometry data. application_id: 10918104
activity_code: DP5
title: Functional ontogeny of pair bonding neural circuits
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO
phr: Project narrative The mesolimbic dopamine system plays a crucial role in our ability to form close bonds with loved ones, and atypical activity of this circuitry is a common feature of several neuropsychiatric diseases that feature an onset during adolescent development. Social experiences play a crucial role in scaffolding brain development, but the ways in which changing adolescent reward circuits are shaped by early social interactions to facilitate adult bonding remains unknown. I will leverage the use of the socially monogamous prairie vole system to discover the activational, functional, and transcriptional features of dopaminergic reward circuit development, furthering our understanding of the pathophysiology of diseases that involve impaired social bonding behaviors.

title: Dissecting the logic of mammalian gene regulation using synthetic biology and single-cell sequencing
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The precise control of gene expression is crucial for the faithful orchestration of the developmental program, but mis-expression of genes can be pathogenic in many contexts. The proposed work will combine advances in large-scale DNA synthesis and single-cell sequencing to study the regulation of genes that pattern the developing nervous system, but are also implicated in diseases such as Alzheimer’s and cancer. application_id: 10912720
activity_code: DP5
title: Determinants of yellow fever pathogenesis in humans
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Yellow fever results in approximately 200,000 cases and 30,000 deaths per year in South-Central America and Sub-Saharan Africa, with increasing potential for spread to Asia, Oceania, North America, and Europe. Death from yellow fever is correlated with liver damage and the development of hemorrhagic disease; however, the mechanisms underlying these disease processes have not been defined. In this study, we will identify key aspects of yellow fever disease progression that culminate in liver damage and hemorrhagic disease, leading to a better understanding of the disease and how it might be treated.

application_id: 11183347
activity_code: DP5
title: Modification of the predisposition for ectopic calcification
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cardiac valve and arterial wall calcification are associated with a 3- to 4-fold increased risk of mortality in adults;
there are many predisposing processes including aging, hypertension, diabetes, and chronic kidney disease.
Given the overall aging population and the increasing incidence of these common and chronic conditions, it is
imperative to delineate the underlying mechanisms of pathological calcification to better understand inciting
events and develop new intervening treatments. This project seeks to explore genetic modifiers of rare Mendelian
calcification disorders to discover and leverage potent therapeutic strategies for monogenic, common, and
complex presentations of ectopic and vascular calcification.

application_id: 10919235
activity_code: R00
title: Neuromodulation of maternal immune adaptations in pregnancy
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Pregnancy relies on finely-tuned maternal immune adaptations to establish and maintain immune-tolerance to the semi- allogeneic fetus, which appear to be disrupted in women experiencing complications, such as preterm birth. Outside the context of pregnancy, the central nervous system can modulate systemic immunity via sympathetic signals but strikingly, its role in modulating the maternal immune dynamics during pregnancy remains unknown. In this proposal, we will investigate whether sympathetic (adrenergic) signals regulate innate immune responses during mouse, and human term and preterm pregnancies, with the aim to elucidate neuroimmune mechanisms of brain-pregnancy interaction – a key step to advance the early risk assessment and treatment of pathological pregnancies.

application_id: 11171429
activity_code: DP5
title: Deciphering the lipid composition of primary cilia in human metabolic disease
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Obesity currently affects 42% of U.S. adults, costing 173 billion dollars in health care costs annually. Human
genetics links obesity to defects in an organelle called the primary cilium, where G protein-coupled receptors
(GPCRs) transmit extracellular information across the cell membrane to direct cell signaling events. This project
will decipher how the primary cilium membrane controls GPCR activity and how it responds to dietary lipid
consumption with the long-term goal of finding better strategies to treat metabolic disease.

title: Building precision disease-responsive gene therapy

phr: Project narrative
This project addresses the challenge of uncontrolled gene expression in gene therapy. We aim to develop

activity_code: DP5
title: Life After Sport: Prior Injury and Sedentary Behavior as Mechanisms of Later Poor Health
org_name: MARQUETTE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Approximately 8.5 million Americans participate annually in competitive high school or collegiate sport, greatly
elevating their risk for musculoskeletal injury and perhaps positioning them for poor long-term health and
function. The proposed DP5 research will evaluate the impact of prior knee injury and sedentary behavior as
two potential mechanisms of later poor health and reduced function. The findings will inform the way we
rehabilitate, counsel, and educate athletes for long-term health and lead to future longitudinal and intervention
studies that will ultimately decrease chronic disease in mid-life, enhancing healthspans.

application_id: 11026388
activity_code: R38
title: Promoting Clinical and Translational Science Training and Career Development for Residents
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This Stimulating Access to Research in Residency program is designed to provide clinical residents with the tools, mentorship, and support necessary to conduct high-impact research at a time when their emerging patient care responsibilities may be awakening a new appreciation for the need for clinically-relevant scientific investigation. title: THWART-TB : Testing Health Workers At Risk to advance our understanding of TB infection
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
It has been estimated that 1.7 billion people have tuberculosis (TB) infection; yet current
tests are unable to predict which people are at highest risk of developing TB disease,
which can be life-threatening. This project will use a range of novel tests to characterize
the profile of immune responses to TB over time in a high-risk population of South
African health workers. The knowledge generated will improve our understanding of TB
infection and help to identify which people exposed to TB may remain at risk, enabling
us to better target preventive strategies.

title: Testing just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) to prevent goal disengagement in response to weight regain following behavioral weight loss treatment
org_name: DREXEL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Although current behavioral weight loss treatments achieve clinically significant weight loss and corresponding health benefits, most participants regain their weight soon after treatment. The proposed project will test the feasibility, acceptability, and preliminary effectiveness of using just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) that facilitate mindfulness, acceptance, and self-compassion to help participants respond more adaptively to small weight regains and ultimately, achieve better long-term weight control. If effective, these interventions can be used as an easily disseminable, low-cost supplement to behavioral weight loss treatment.

application_id: 11040220
activity_code: R01
title: Diversity Supplement: Measuring arterial material properties using wave-based approaches with ultrasound and computational models
org_name: MAYO CLINIC ROCHESTER
phr: NARRATIVE Increased arterial stiffness has been associated with indicators of damage to the brain including presence of white matter hyperintensity, infarctions, brain atrophy as assessed with magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Ultrasound is a first-line imaging modality for evaluation of vascular geometry and flow because of its real-time capabilities, but many scanners do not have the capabilities to measure arterial mechanical properties in an accurate, quantitative manner. Successful completion of this project will characterize the relationship between ultrasound-based measurement of carotid artery mechanical properties and MRI measured indicators of brain health.

application_id: 11371042
activity_code: K01
title: A novel method to resolve the complex genome rearrangements of the large copy number variants (CNVs) associated with psychiatric disorders
org_name: TEXAS A&M AGRILIFE RESEARCH
phr: Project Narrative
The major goals of this study are to develop a generalizable method to resolve the exact genome segmental
duplication rearrangements underlying large copy number variants (CNVs), to apply the method in a group of
affected individuals with the 16p11.2 CNVs and determine the rearrangement diversity across different
individuals, to develop high-throughput genotyping assays for future large-cohort association studies in an
expand group of affected individuals, and to investigate the functional consequences of rearrangement
diversity using neuronal organoid models. The methodological framework established here for studying the
16p11.2 CNVs can be used to study the other large CNVs that are strongly associated with psychiatric
disorders such as 22q11, 15q13, and 1q21.

application_id: 10996446
activity_code: F30
title: Inflammatory Caspase Activation in Sickle Cell Disease
org_name: BAYLOR COLLEGE OF MEDICINE
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Sickle cell disease is an inherited red blood cell disorder characterized by excess inflammation and many severe acute and chronic inflammatory complications. Our lab found that heme, a major inducer of inflammation in patients with sickle cell disease, activates the pro-inflammatory protein caspase-4. To determine the suitability of caspase-4 as a drug target, this project will investigate the mechanism of activation of caspase-4 and characterize how activation of caspase-4 contributes to inflammatory complications of sickle cell disease.

title: Multispecies investigation of early pregnancy loss mechanisms using bioengineered stem cell models
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative: Early pregnancy loss can be a devastating outcome for people with the ability to become pregnant, and nearly two-thirds of all losses occur prior to and around the time of implantation. title: Ubiquitin-mediated regulation of kinetochore assembly and function
org_name: FRED HUTCHINSON CANCER CENTER
phr: Project Narrative Accurate chromosome segregation is essential to an organism’s growth and development. In eukaryotic cells, chromosome segregation is facilitated by the kinetochore, a conserved multiprotein complex that mediates the attachment of microtubules to chromosomes. This proposal seeks to identify and define the mechanisms of ubiquitin-mediated regulation of the kinetochore; these findings will illuminate principles governing a central biological process with implications for diseases marked by chromosome segregation errors.

application_id: 10770522
activity_code: K01
title: A novel method to resolve the complex genome rearrangements of the large copy number variants (CNVs) associated with psychiatric disorders
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The major goals of this study are to develop a generalizable method to resolve the exact genome segmental duplication rearrangements underlying large copy number variants (CNVs), to apply the method in a group of affected individuals with the 16p11.2 CNVs and determine the rearrangement diversity across different individuals, to develop high-throughput genotyping assays for future large-cohort association studies in an expand group of affected individuals, and to investigate the functional consequences of rearrangement diversity using neuronal organoid models. The methodological framework established here for studying the 16p11.2 CNVs can be used to study the other large CNVs that are strongly associated with psychiatric disorders such as 22q11, 15q13, and 1q21.

application_id: 10846746
activity_code: K23
title: Macrovascular and Microvascular Response to Fluid Removal during Hemodialysis for Acute Kidney Injury
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Acute kidney injury is a common and serious complication among hospitalized patients and confers substantial risks of end-stage kidney disease and death. While dialytic support for severe acute kidney injury can be life- sustaining, outcomes are poor and methods to guide a safe rate of fluid removal during hemodialysis are inadequate. The objective of this work is to leverage noninvasive devices to simultaneously quantify the systemic and tissue hemodynamic response to fluid removal throughout hemodialysis to better understand mechanisms underlying adverse consequences of hemodialysis and advance the development of more precise approaches to the delivery of renal replacement therapy.

application_id: 11311477
activity_code: K08
title: RNA: Polymerase III specific CD8+ T cells: a mechanistic insight into cancer-induced autoimmunity in scleroderma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS HLTH SCI CTR HOUSTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The goal of this proposal is to study RNA polymerase III-specific CD8+ T cells in the peripheral blood and target
tissues of patients with co-incident cancer and scleroderma. Identifying distinct RNA polymerase III epitopes
recognized by CD8+ T cells, which we believe to play a fundamental role in disease initiation and propagation,
may inform us of candidate disease-specific diagnostic, monitoring and therapeutic tools. Moreover, this proposal
seeks to enhance our understanding of the mechanistic relationship between cancer and autoimmunity in
general.

application_id: 11361739
activity_code: K08
title: Understanding diet-microbiota interactions in small intestine eosinophil tissue residency during homeostasis and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA, formerly known as Churg Strauss syndrome) is a rare,
serious disease of blood vessel and internal organ inflammation associated with high levels of immune cells
called eosinophils. Patients who have EPGA affecting the intestine tend to do worse, underscoring a need to
better understand eosinophils in the intestine. We discovered that signals derived from nutrients regulate small
intestine eosinophils during normal conditions, and propose to understand how these signals work, to figure out
the impacts of certain aspects of the microbiome, and to study small intestine eosinophils along with these factors
in a mouse model of EGPA.

application_id: 11194155
activity_code: K08
title: Impact of the Therapeutic Alliance on Reduction of Disparities in Latino End-of-Life Cancer Care
org_name: RUTGERS BIOMEDICAL AND HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Cancer is the leading cause of death among Latinos in the US, who now represent the largest minority group.
Latino cancer patients engage in advance care planning less often and receive less value-consistent care at
the end-of-life more often than non-Latino cancer patients. The proposed research addresses the pressing
need to reduce Latino EoL care disparities by developing an intervention for improving the therapeutic alliance
between oncologists and their Latino cancer patients.

application_id: 10939938
activity_code: DP2
title: 3D spatial single-cell multiomics mapping of Alzheimer's disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project Narrative Little is known about the changes in single-cell level protein composition in the brain during Alzheimer’s disease (AD) progression. This project will use a novel Gel-based Optical-isolation 3D (GO3D) single-cell-type multiomics to spatially profile proteins, RNAs, and chromatin accessibility of an AD-associated cell type, astrocyte. The outcomes of this project will unbiasedly provide a list of AD-associated proteins and PTMs, which can be new potential targets for AD treatment.

title: The UCSF IRACDA Scholars Program.
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE – UCSF IRACDA Scholars Program Recruiting and retaining exceptional talent from diverse populations is essential for maintaining our nation’s outstanding technical and scientific ranking worldwide. The UCSF IRACDA Scholars Program is addressing this challenge by training highly motivated postdoctoral scholars for academic faculty careers in biomedical research, who often hail from underrepresented groups. activity_code: F30
title: The Role of PICALM in Regulating Neurogenesis in Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative: The cause of memory loss in Alzheimer’s disease remains largely unknown. It is essential to understand the molecular mechanisms underlying hippocampus-dependent learning and memory, the earliest and most affected by the disease. This project will determine the role of the Late onset Alzheimer’s disease – linked PICALM in adult hippocampal neurogenesis and establish PICALM as a novel contributor to hippocampus-dependent memory impairments in Alzheimer’s disease.

application_id: 10805412
activity_code: K08
title: DNA Damage and Immunogenicity in Ewing Sarcoma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: This work aims to better understand the pediatric cancer Ewing sarcoma, specifically investigating the immunobiology in the setting of tumor DNA damage. The studies will be performed by examining the cancer cells themselves as well as by examining the immune cells in proximity to the tumor that can influence growth and spread of the cancer. This career development grant will provide the applicant with training in tumor immunology and bioinformatic analyses, an area of growing importance in pediatric oncology.

application_id: 11329019
activity_code: K08
title: Determinants of Resistance to Engineered T-cell Therapies Targeting CD19 in Lymphoma
org_name: BECKMAN RESEARCH INSTITUTE/CITY OF HOPE
phr: Project Narrative
Immune based treatment strategies are increasingly employed to treat patients with Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma
(NHL), but many patients still experience relapse. In this study I seek to identify biomarkers that are predictive
of relapse following immunotherapy, and also to gain an understanding of the mechanisms underlying these
relapses. This project will build a foundation for future clinical trials by informing the development of improved
immunotherapies for this illness, thus directly contributing to the NIH mission to seek basic knowledge about
the nature of living systems that will help to improve health, reduce illness and disability, and lengthen life.

application_id: 10808415
activity_code: K08
title: The Role of Propionate Metabolism in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative Recent studies have shown that genetic changes explain only 50% of lung cancer disease progression, strongly suggesting there are unidentified, non-genetic mechanisms that are driving drug resistance and metastasis. In preliminary experiments, we have identified that propionate metabolism, a pathway previously not known to play a role in cancer, is dysregulated in lung cancer, and its dysregulation can drive drug resistance and metastatic programs. This proposal aims to improve our understanding of propionate metabolism in lung cancer by defining the mechanisms and consequences of its dysregulation, potentially identifying a new pathway that can be targeted therapeutically.

application_id: 10871881
activity_code: K99
title: Molecular mechanisms of load-induced t-tubule regulation in the mammalian heart
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Narrative
This proposal will determine how changing mechanical load, such as occurs when the heart has to pump
against a high pressure, influences structures of the heart muscle membrane called transverse tubules. These
(t)-tubules are disrupted in diseases such as heart failure, but how changing the load of the heart affects them
is not known. Understanding this process, and the molecules responsible, might help in discovering new
treatments for heart failure.

application_id: 11061042
activity_code: R25
title: The Summer HIV/AIDS Research Program (SHARP)
org_name: PUBLIC HEALTH FOUNDATION ENTERPRISES
phr: SHARP (Summer HIV/AIDS Research Program) is an innovative and highly-applied HIV and substance use summer research experience for undergraduates. SHARP scholars engage in research under the mentorship of leading HIV investigators, engage in peer-to-peer support, didactics, workshops and lectures, and have opportunities to shadow and participate in community events and other learning experiences designed to inspire them to pursue further studies and careers in public health-oriented clinical, socio-behavioral, and community-focused research. SHARP is a pathway program that aims to train the next generation of scientists.

org_name: WEILL MEDICAL COLL OF CORNELL UNIV
phr: Project Narrative: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is one of the deadliest cancers worldwide; but when detected early, it is readily curable. application_id: 11383007
activity_code: K08
title: Myogenic Mechanisms of Craniofacial Skeletal Muscle Regeneration
org_name: VIRGINIA COMMONWEALTH UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Craniofacial skeletal muscle is essential for basic life functions including chewing, swallowing, and speaking;
therefore, understanding how this tissue regenerates in response to injury and disease will provide the basis
for therapeutic advances in the treatment of craniofacial muscle diseases and disorders. Our group has
demonstrated that craniofacial skeletal muscle has the ability to regenerate following damage, but to what
extent remains unknown. The goal of this proposal is to comprehensively investigate the regenerative capacity
of craniofacial muscle and its stem cell population to inform developments in treatment modalities for
individuals suffering from craniofacial muscle injury or disease.

org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Narrative
As the population of people living with HIV (PLWH) age globally, there is a need to better
understand the burden of comorbidities including those related to aging, especially in low- and
middle-income country settings where the highest burden of HIV exists. The goal of this
supplement, embedded into our existing D43, is to use multiple methods to assess the burden
of medical and geriatric comorbidities and their association with quality of life (QOL) in a cohort
of PLWH age>50 in urban Tanzania and also use explanatory mixed methods to better
understand factors associated with QOL. Results of this study will be important to identify
priorities for interventions to address the burden of comorbidities and to improve QOL in this
population while supporting development of researchers...

application_id: 11085054
activity_code: F32
title: Transcriptional variation in human milk
org_name: J. DAVID GLADSTONE INSTITUTES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Human milk provides significant health benefits to infants as well as the lactating parent, and while breastfeeding is recommended for the first six months of life, many families struggle to initiate and maintain lactation. Few clinical interventions exist to improve lactation outcomes, in part due to lack of understanding of the cellular process of lactation by cells in the mammary gland. This study will leverage existing transcriptomic data of mammary gland cells from human milk to identify the gene pathways underlying the process of lactation and its response to disease.

application_id: 10886299
activity_code: K08
title: Donor DNMT3a-mediated regulation of Graft-vs-Host-Disease and the Graft-vs-Tumor effect in allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HCT) remains the only curative therapy for many aggressive malignancies and is increasingly used for non-malignant disorders, but successful outcomes are limited by graft- vs-host-disease (GVHD) and inadequate graft-vs-tumor (GVT) activity. De novo DNA methylation catalyzed by DNMT3a is a major epigenetic mechanism that controls the post-HCT immune response. This project will define the DNMT3a-dependent pathways that can be targeted in the regulation and separation of harmful GVHD from beneficial GVT activity, which remains a critical unmet need toward improving patient outcomes.

title: Elucidating structural, mechanistic, and allosteric determinants of mTOR Complex 2 (mTORC2) signaling.
org_name: BROWN UNIVERSITY
phr: Taylor, Martin | K08 (PA-20-203) | PROJECT NARRATIVE
Selective therapeutic modulation of the two mTOR effector complexes, mTORC1 and mTORC2, is a key goal
for the treatment of diabetes and many types of cancers because dysregulation of these signaling complexes
play central and often opposing roles in disease pathophysiology, but we lack the detailed structural and
mechanistic information about both complexes that would enable development of these therapies. For example,
in type 2 diabetes, overfeeding results in hyperstimulation of mTORC1, which measures nutrients, resulting in
inhibition of mTORC2, which then fails to transmit the critical insulin signal and leads to insulin resistance; in this
context, it would be advantageous to selectively inhibit mTORC1, preventing this feedback loop...

application_id: 11160274
activity_code: F31
title: Understanding Suicidality among Sexual and Gender Minority Young Adults Experiencing Homelessness
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Sexual and gender minority young adults experiencing homelessness (YAEH) are disproportionately affected
by suicidality (i.e., suicidal ideation and attempt) and related comorbidities including substance use, violence
exposure, and mental health issues. However, prevention and intervention efforts are hampered without
understanding how these prevalent exposures intersect to amplify suicidality. Guided by syndemic theory, this
project will employ latent class analysis, social network analysis, and complex systems science methodologies
to develop models of co-occurrence among three high-priority adversities; substance use, violence exposure,
and mental health issues, and their implications for suicidality.

title: Innate Immunity to Viral Infection of the Retina
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Acute retinal necrosis is a viral infection of the retina with devastating complications resulting in life-long visual
impairment, a worse quality of life, reduction in productivity, and can be associated with central nervous system
infection and death despite rapid treatment. This project aims to provide the necessary skills to understand
how the viral-innate immune response interplay unfolds within nonregenerative retinal tissue and whether there
are pathways that can be targeted to improve visual outcomes following viral infection of the retina.

title: Integrating cell identities and morphodynamics through extracellular cues
org_name: CALIFORNIA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY
phr: Project Narrative
How cells acquire their identities must be tightly coordinated with how tissues develop to their proper shapes in

(the changes in synaptic connections between neurons in the brain), and a major regulator of neuroplasticity is
the moment-to moment-state of the brain (attention, emotions, arousal, etc.), but this is poorly understood,
especially in humans, and may be particularly important for plasticity-induction brain stimulation therapies
like repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS), although this is rarely considered in practice. The
goal of this research is to measure how attention (an experimentally tractable, well-understood, and disease-
relevant brain state) regulates rTMS-induced neuroplasticity in two different parts of the human visual spatial
attention ci...

application_id: 11237719
activity_code: K08
title: Gestation Dependent Immune Response to Group B Streptococcus Infection During Pregnancy
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: Narrative
The immune system in the uterus is required to fight infection while at the same time maintaining the
pregnancy and protecting the pregnant person and the fetus. As pregnancy progresses, the immune system
evolves to accommodate the unique needs of the pregnancy which changes over the course of gestation. This
proposal aims to define the immune programs activated in response to infection with the ultimate goal of
identifying novel diagnostic and therapeutic strategies to improve pregnancy outcomes.

org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Schizophrenia is a highly heritable neuropsychiatric disorder for which diagnostic and treatment options are limited. application_id: 11224881
activity_code: K18
title: A Comparative Analysis of Age Differences in Social Networks
org_name: GEORGIA STATE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
 This project will examine how social networks vary with age in both humans and capuchin monkeys to
determine whether social network narrowing is a uniquely human phenomenon or a general response to aging.
By investigating how declines in cognitive function contribute to shifts in social behavior across species, and by
linking these changes to biomarkers sensitive to aging and pathology, this research will provide insights into
the health implications of social aging and help identify factors that promote healthy aging.

application_id: 10846800
activity_code: K23
title: Using Small Area Variation Analysis to Investigate Sources of Practice Variation for Febrile Infants at Risk for Invasive Bacterial Infections
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ROCHESTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Emergency department clinical decisions vary widely for febrile infants, who are at risk for potentially fatal invasive bacterial infections; some infants may experience harms from under-testing or over-treatment. There is a gap in scientific knowledge regarding how clinicians make decisions for febrile infants and the extent to which key sources contribute to practice variation. Addressing this gap will provide a foundation for addressing NICHD priorities of improving care to febrile infants and promoting health equity.

title: STD and AIDS Research Training Grant
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Sexually transmitted infections, including HIV, are major public health issues in the United States and globally.
This training program, now in its 45th year, has trained 250 predoctoral students and postdoctoral fellows,
including infectious disease physicians, in STD and HIV/AIDS research, 80% of whom have continued in
academia, public health or government, or in other biomedical research venues. Continued support for this

application_id: 10865573
activity_code: K08
title: Somatic mutations in autism spectrum disorder
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder that affects 1 in 36 children in the United States. Somatic variants, variants that arise after fertilization, have been implicated in ASD risk, but the types of variants that are enriched in ASD probands as well as the potential contribution of somatic variants in non- coding gene regulatory regions are largely unknown. This proposal will apply innovative approaches to test the hypothesis that somatic variants in both gene coding and gene regulatory regions contribute to ASD risk and can reveal new genes and mechanisms involved in ASD.
