application_id: 11061825
activity_code: K01
title: The role of maternal obesity in osteoarthritis
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Parental high-fat diets that are rich in omega-6 fatty acids have been shown to increase osteoarthritis severity
in adult mouse offspring. I hypothesize that maternal obesity programs offspring osteoarthritis risk by
epigenetically programming the cells of the developing knee joint. Here we propose that re-balancing the fatty
acid ratio in the presence of maternal obesity will improve pregnancy health and protect the offspring from
susceptibility to injury-induced osteoarthritis.

activity_code: K08
title: Somatic mutations in autism spectrum disorder
org_name: RESEARCH INST NATIONWIDE CHILDREN'S HOSP
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder that affects 1 in 36 children in the United
States. Somatic variants, variants that arise after fertilization, have been implicated in ASD risk, but the types
of variants that are enriched in ASD probands as well as the potential contribution of somatic variants in non-
coding gene regulatory regions are largely unknown. This proposal will apply innovative approaches to test the
hypothesis that somatic variants in both gene coding and gene regulatory regions contribute to ASD risk and
can reveal new genes and mechanisms involved in ASD.
terms: N/A
---

application_id: 11085241
activity_code: K23
title: The Microbiome, Metabolome, and Genome in Multiplex IBD Family Clusters
org_name: ICAHN SCHOOL OF MEDICINE AT MOUNT SINAI
phr: The pathogenesis of Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) involves interactions between genetics, environmental
and microbial factors, and immune responses with an inherent complexity making prevention and cure elusive.
Current cohorts do not allow for characterization of the evolution of this multifactorial milieu before disease
inception limiting our ability to predict disease. We aim, in a novel, pre-disease-inception, high-risk, familial IBD
cohort to characterize the microbial and genetic contributions to IBD to allow for improved stratification of those
at highest risk for developing IBD.

application_id: 10886865
activity_code: K08
title: BCOR/PRC1.1 Inactivation In Splicing Factor Mutant MDS Progression
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS) are defined by the hierarchical acquisition of genetic mutations in the bone marrow resulting in ineffective hematopoiesis and propensity to transform to acute myeloid leukemia (AML). This project seeks to determine the mechanisms by which inactivating mutations in BCOR, a recurrently mutated core component of the non-canonical polycomb repressive complex 1.1 (PRC1.1), interact with mutations in the RNA splicing factor U2AF1 to mediate MDS progression. The proposed research will advance our understanding of PRC1.1 dysfunction in hematopoiesis and myeloid malignancy development with the potential to improve surveillance and treatment strategies for MDS patients.

application_id: 10885099
activity_code: K01
title: Novel Methods for Evaluating the Association of Electronic Cigarette Use with Cardiovascular Health
org_name: BOSTON UNIVERSITY MEDICAL CAMPUS
phr: Project Narrative
Population-based evidence on the cardiovascular (CV) effects of e-cigarette use is sparse. The proposed
research will use high-quality population data with state-of-the-art causal inference methods to assess the
effects of e-cigarette use on incident CV events, examine urinary exposure biomarkers to identify specific
components of e-cigarette aerosols that mediate CV harm, and stratify adult CV risk according to biomarker
signatures and their association with e-cigarette use behaviors and product characteristics. The study will
generate critically needed evidence on the CV health risks of e-cigarette use, which may inform new regulatory
standards for product safety and guide primary and secondary public health interventions that aim to reduce
tobacco-related CV harm.

title: Immune control of oncogene selection in preneoplastic colon
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Colon cancer is the fourth deadliest cancer with ~150,000 new cases and 53,000 deaths in the US in 2021. application_id: 11310666
activity_code: K08
title: Defining the Role of Developmental Context in Oncohistone-Driven Gliomagenesis
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: Project Narrative
This work seeks to define the role of developmental context in oncohistone-mediated glioma initiation and
maintenance using a novel inducible transgenic cerebral organoid system. The overall approach is to alter the
timing and sequence of oncogene induction during human cerebral organoid development, interrogate the
functional consequences at single cell resolution, and evaluate the impact on tumor phenotype and maintenance.
The long-term goal of this research program is to identify better therapeutic targets for oncohistone-driven
pediatric high-grade gliomas, one of the most lethal childhood cancers.

application_id: 10887467
activity_code: T32
title: Predoctoral Training Program in Immunology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF IOWA
phr: The immune system provides humans with critically important protective responses to infectious pathogens such as viruses and bacteria, as well as to tumor cells; but inappropriate immune responses can also cause inflammation and autoimmune diseases. Studies that advance understanding of immune responses, and how to apply this knowledge to preventing and treating human diseases have great potential to reduce the disease burden and promote the health of the nation. The Predoctoral Training Program in Immunology is designed to provide rigorous training in a supportive environment for graduate students to pursue multiple approaches to understanding the mechanisms underlying immune responses, and apply this knowledge toward the treatment of human disease.

application_id: 11146526
activity_code: F32
title: Regulation of the intestinal colonization niche by epithelial cell death
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative
The mechanisms by which the gastrointestinal microbiota influences host immunity remain poorly characterized.
The intestinal epithelial cells integrate information from the microbiota, and relay these signals to the downstream
immune system, therefore playing a key role as intermediaries in the effect of the microbiota on the entire body.
In this project, we propose to study how the microbiota regulates intestinal epithelial cell death and how this can
influence disease susceptibility and infectious disease outcomes with the goal of furthering our understanding of
epithelial cell function, which will aid the design of new therapies to restore host immune function.

application_id: 10890151
activity_code: F30
title: Dissecting the functional role of LINE1 retrotransposon-mediated interferon signaling in myeloid leukemia
org_name: UT SOUTHWESTERN MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Acute myeloid leukemia is one of the deadliest blood cancers, yet decades-old chemotherapies remain the standard of care for treatment due to its molecularly heterogeneous pathobiology. The epigenetic repression of LINE1 retrotransposons (“jumping genes”) is a newly recognized feature of AML-initiating cells to maintain malignant self-renewal, differentiation blockade, and genomic stability, whereas aberrant LINE1 reactivation impairs AML development. This project aims to elucidate the functional and mechanistic role of retrotransposon- mediated interferon signaling in AML pathogenesis, knowledge of which can inform whether and how the retrotransposon-innate immune crosstalk may be leveraged to selectively eradicate AML-initiating cells.

title: Characterization of native AMPA receptor structure and function in glioblastoma
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The enzyme Dot1L drives overexpression of oncogenes that are essential for the initiation and progression of mixed lineage leukemia, an aggressive subset of acute leukemias with no known cure and limited effective clinical treatments. activity_code: DP2
title: Reactivating regulatory programs for regeneration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA BERKELEY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Human cardiac injury, such as a heart attack, leads to irreparable damage and life-long heart complications.
This proposal explores the gene regulatory controls that dictate heart repair in a regenerative organism, the
zebrafish, which will reveal candidates for reprogramming human-derived cardioids to elicit a regenerative
response. Distinctive synergies between multiomics approaches and diverse cardiac models will make feasible
a longstanding goal of regenerative medicine: reactivating the latent capacity of the human heart to regenerate
post-injury.

activity_code: K00
title: Characterization of native AMPA receptor structure and function in glioblastoma
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The enzyme Dot1L drives overexpression of oncogenes that are essential for the initiation and progression of mixed lineage leukemia, an aggressive subset of acute leukemias with no known cure and limited effective clinical treatments. Here we illustrate the molecular basis for Dot1L activity by identifying the interfaces between Dot1L and its nucleosome substrate that are essential for oncogene activation by Dot1L. By establishing the disease-relevance of these Dot1L-nucleosome interaction interfaces in mixed lineage leukemia, this work will inform the development of novel targeted therapeutics to combat this deadly disease.

activity_code: DP2
title: Revealing the Biophysics of the Germinal Center Microenvironment
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS MED BR GALVESTON
phr: Project Narrative The fact that a small subset of HIV infected individuals develop broadly neutralizing antibodies (bnAbs) capable of neutralizing 98% of HIV viruses gives substantial promise that an HIV vaccine is possible. However, there has been a lack of understanding of the biophysical constraints of the germinal center microenvironment (GCME), which is the arena in which B cells compete to develop into bnAb producing B cells. We aim to uncover the composition of the extracellular space within the GCME, with the goal of identifying novel biophysical constraints and metabolic pathways that may be recruited during vaccination to elicit generation of bnAb producing B cells.

activity_code: K38
title: The Role of Developmental Dysanapsis in Lung Development Throughout the Lifespan in Experimental Bronchopulmonary Dysplasia
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative: Bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD), the chronic lung disease following preterm birth and the most frequent complication of prematurity with persistent mortality and substantial life-long morbidity. Our lab has shown that there is a disproportionate, dysanaptic growth pattern in airways and airspace in experimental BPD. This proposal will utilize a rodent model of chorioamnionitis- induced BPD to study interactions between airway, parenchyma, and vascular growth over the lifespan, and how antenatal stress contributes to long-term growth and susceptibility to future insults which will lead to further understanding of the biomechanical mechanisms that cause sustained lung development abnormalities after preterm birth, as well as the perinatal origins of adult lung disease.

title: Investigating the Role of Cell Plasticity in Malignant Transformation
org_name: SLOAN-KETTERING INST CAN RESEARCH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Acquired resistance to targeted therapy poses a major barrier to curing cancer, but the molecular mechanisms
that allow cancer cells to adapt to therapy remain poorly understood. I propose to elucidate mechanisms of
resistance to multiple therapies in use or in trial to treat non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) by identifying
novel regulators of the cell cycle and lung cancer cell identity. This work will provide novel insight into the

application_id: 10897048
activity_code: K00
title: Single Molecule Biophysics of Intrinsically Disordered Proteins in Disease
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative: Despite their lack of a fixed structure, disordered proteins have emerged as central drivers of biological function, with key roles in cell signaling, transcription, and genome organization, all of which contribute to diseases ranging from viral infections to cancer. Although clear examples in which disordered proteins are essential for disease progression exist, our understanding of the role of intrinsically disordered regions in disease etiology is limited. This proposal will study disordered protein-nucleic acid interactions in SARS-CoV-2 and in the context of cancer using a collection of single- molecule techniques.

org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative The medial prefrontal cortex governs many aspects of cognitive control and is hypothesized to play a major role in individual differences in cognitive function both before and after alcohol exposure that drive maladaptive behaviors that characterize alcohol use disorder (AUD). org_name: WHITEHEAD INSTITUTE FOR BIOMEDICAL RES
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Molecular drivers and cellular interactions drive changes in cancer cell state that contribute to tumor growth and progression, but how these factors coordinatively drive tumorigenesis is not well defined. I propose to elucidate mechanisms of chromatin regulation and evasion of immune response by using innovative mouse models and functional genomics analyses. This work will provide novel insight into the principles of chromatin regulation and tumor heterogeneity that contribute to progression in lung and pancreas cancers, which may also be applicable to other cancer types.

application_id: 11129845
activity_code: R00
title: Mechanistic Understanding of Hypoxia-Induced Peroxisome loss: Implications for Heart Failure
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative:
A common feature of heart failure is fatty acid (FA) oxidation inhibition and boosting FA oxidation is
beneficial in animal models of heart failure; however, the mechanisms that suppress FA oxidation
remain unclear. In this proposal, I uncover an unappreciated role for peroxisomes, an essential
organelle for FA oxidation, in ischemic heart failure and define the mechanism by which ischemia
promotes peroxisome loss. These studies provide important insight into metabolic remodeling in heart
failure and will help develop a novel therapeutic strategy in this disease.

application_id: 11083074
activity_code: K00
title: Deciphering the role of tumor-macrophage crosstalk during metastatic dissemination to the peritoneum
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Description/ Narrative Most cancer patients still fail to respond to current checkpoint blockade immunotherapy, thus understanding the protective immune responses in long-term cancer survivors and studying the cancer immunotherapy resistant mechanisms are urgent. In this proposal, we will further characterize the durable tumor protective immune responses in melanoma survivors with dermal immune-related adverse events (irAE) and expand the study to explore the roles of microbiomes in current immunotherapy resistant cancer types. The findings of this work will reveal the durable, successful anti-tumor immune responses linking the skin irAEs with tumor immunity and provide novel insights into the key immune mechanisms of microbiomes associated long-term tumor elimination.

application_id: 11127027
activity_code: R00
title: Oxidative Cysteine Modification by Thiol Isomerases in Sickle Cell Disease
org_name: BLOODWORKS
phr: Project Narrative Vaso-occlusive events remain a clinical burden in sickle cell disease and is the major cause of organ failure. Although a major contributor to vaso-occlusion in this disease is redox stress, the mechanism by which redox stress promotes vaso-occlusion is not well understood. The goal of this project is to determine the role of oxidative cysteine electron transferring events in vaso-occlusion that are driven by thiol isomerases, which would reveal redox targetable mechanisms to prevent vaso-occlusion in sickle cell disease.

application_id: 11077202
activity_code: R25
title: Substance Abuse Research Education and Training (SARET)
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative
Despite the great toll substance abuse takes on society, the pace of discovery of new and effective prevention
and treatment strategies is only modest, and major delays exist in translating such knowledge into improved
health of patients and populations. There is an urgent need to increase the number of health care
professionals conducting clinically applicable research in the prevention and treatment of substance use
disorders. The goal of this grant is to expand, and deepen the health equity focus and national impact of, a
substance use disorder research training program targeting students in the fields of medicine, nursing,
dentistry, social work and public health.

application_id: 11061204
activity_code: T32
title: UCSF Musculoskeletal Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Musculoskeletal diseases, which affect more than half of the United States population age 18 and over and
nearly three-quarters age 65 and over, are among the most debilitating nonfatal health conditions. The causes
of these diseases remain poorly understood, and there is a need for better therapies. The UCSF Musculoskeletal
Training Program prepares scientists and physicians for a lifetime of scholarly pursuits that lead to in-depth
understanding and improved care for patients with MSK diseases.

application_id: 11123435
activity_code: K00
title: Investigation of tumor stem cell maintenance and cellular hierarchy in pediatric high-grade glioma
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: Project Narrative Pediatric high-grade glioma (PHGG), a deadly childhood brain tumor, likely originates from tumor stem  cells that differentiate into proliferating tumor cells. PHGG tumors are heterogeneous and include several  different types of proliferating cells, leading to the question of how tumor stem cells and proliferating tumor  cells relate to one another. I will investigate whether a single multipotent tumor stem cell population  produces the multiple proliferating cell types of PHGG, to provide a biological foundation for the  development of critically needed advances in therapeutic alternatives for this deadly childhood disease.

title: Genome Integrity Through the Cell Cycle in Homeostasis and Cancer
org_name: BRIGHAM AND WOMEN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Project Narrative Preservation of genome integrity is crucial during development, normal cellular function, and tumor suppression, and the DNA damage repair and cell division cycle pathways are at the heart of an interconnected web of molecular mechanisms. application_id: 10902353
activity_code: F31
title: Defining the Transcriptional Activity of NR2F6 in TH17 cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: NARRATIVE TH17 cells play a pivotal role in inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) pathogenesis through secretion of many pro- inflammatory cytokines and chemokines. The nuclear receptor NR2F6 is reported to act as a negative transcriptional regulator of TH17 cells making its agonism a promising target for IBD therapy. The proposed studies will address fundamental questions regarding the transcriptional role of NR2F6, insights which will be particularly valuable understanding TH17-mediated chronic inflammation and whether targeting this nuclear receptor may be therapeutically relevant.

title: The Lombard effect in muscle tension dysphonia: mechanistic and clinical characterization

org_name: UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Treatments for age-related memory decline rely on understanding the basic mechanisms of hippocampal memory formation and storage over time. application_id: 11110311
activity_code: R25
title: Native American Short-Term Research Education Program in Children's Health
org_name: UTAH STATE HIGHER EDUCATION SYSTEM--UNIVERSITY OF UTAH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This proposal seeks to continue the successful holistic University of Utah Native American Research Internship
program (NARI) to include summer research experiences in cardiovascular, pulmonary, and hematology
(NHLBI). The NARI-NHLBI program will partner with researchers at the University of Utah with AI/AN
undergraduates who aspire to help understand why AI/AN have been affected so disproportionately. The
overarching goal of the NARI program is to increase the diversity of the U.S. biomedical science workforce and

title: Elucidating the role of RNA splicing in the metastatic seeding and outgrowth of aggressive cancers.
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: Project Narrative Cancer metastasis is a deadly, elusive, and heterogeneous disease which lacks effective targeted therapeutics, and understanding the mechanisms underlying cancer metastasis remains to be fully elucidated. RNA splicing lends to cellular complexity and heterogeneity by producing distinct RNA and protein isoforms from a single gene, and the dysregulation of RNA splicing has been implicated in cancer development and progression. This research proposal seeks to identify novel mechanisms in which dysregulated alternative splicing contributes to metastatic seeding and outgrowth in breast and other aggressive cancers, with the ultimate goal of identifying potential biomarkers and therapeutic targets of cancer metastasis.

application_id: 10893369
activity_code: F31
title: Investigating the role of the membrane in particulate methane monooxygenase (pMMO) structure and function
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE  Methane is a potent greenhouse gas that drives climate change and represents an appreciable burden on human and environmental health. A promising route toward mitigating climate change exists in the particulate methane monooxygenase (pMMO), a copper-dependent transmembrane enzyme from methanotrophic bacteria that oxidizes methane to methanol, a desirable biofuel. This project involves the use of innovative membrane mimetics to render pMMO more amenable for study, addressing longstanding questions about this important enzyme and membrane proteins in general, which are underrepresented in structural studies and fundamentally important in human health.

application_id: 10847852
activity_code: T32
title: Infection and Inflammation as Drivers of Aging (IIDA) Predoctoral Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ARIZONA
phr: NARRATIVE  This multidisciplinary training program is designed to train pre-doctoral students (6 per year) to investigate the underlying the roles of Infection and Inflammation as Drivers of Aging. Increased training in biomedical sciences pertinent to the biology of aging is critical to address the needs of the growing number of older adults, that will make over a quarter of the US population and ~1.5 billion people worldwide by 2050. Specific training in the roles of infection and inflammation as drivers of aging will help understand and treat sources of inflammation that propagate and aggravate many chronic diseases of old age, including arthritis, cancers, cardiovascular, metabolic and neurodegenerative diseases, like Alzheimer’s and Parkinsons.

title: Inhibitory Control of Dendritic Spikes in CA1 Pyramidal Cells
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PITTSBURGH AT PITTSBURGH
phr: Project Narrative Neurons integrate signals from multiple sources, and dendritic spikes are fundamental to this process, serving as a key mechanism for information transfer and storage across neural circuits. This project will explore how synaptic inhibition, particularly from dendrite-targeting somatostatin interneurons, regulates dendritic spike dynamics and controls voltage and calcium dynamics in CA1 pyramidal neurons. Using optogenetics, two-photon imaging, and electrophysiology, this study will uncover the mechanisms by which inhibitory synapses modify dendritic compartments and how these processes contribute to learning and memory.

application_id: 11087885
activity_code: K23
title: SLE-AWARE: SLE-- A Window into APOL1 Regulation and Expression
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MARYLAND BALTIMORE
phr: NARRATIVE Up to 60% of African Americans, who are at heightened risk for kidney and cardiovascular disease (CVD), carry at least one Apolipoprotein L1 (APOL1) coding change variant allele. These variants associate with adverse renal and CVD outcomes—particularly in chronic inflammatory disease such as SLE. By leveraging SLE monocyte transcriptional profiles validated by primary cell culture models, this study will test the overall hypothesis that immune activation and resultant increased APOL1 expression synergizes with APOL1 genotype to precipitate human disease.

phr: Project Narrative The neural mechanisms of regulation support socioemotional competence and may operate as resilience factors in the face of risk for psychopathology. title: The effect of language experience on a bilingual's ability to process arithmetic and other memorized facts
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Many bilingual students entering US schools learned foundational concepts in a language other than English, leaving them at a potential developmental disadvantage compared to their monolingual peers when tested on this knowledge in English. activity_code: F31
title: Elucidating the Role of Neural Activity in Midbrain Dopamine Neuron Degeneration
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Parkinson’s Disease (PD) is a neurodegenerative disease characterized by the loss of substantia nigra dopamine
neurons, but our understanding of how their activity state contributes to their selective vulnerability is lacking.
Using novel chemogenetic models to manipulate dopamine neuron activity in combination with mice that have
disrupted dopamine storage, this proposal will deeply examine the role and mechanisms of neural activity in
dopamine neuron susceptibility to dopamine-induced toxicity. Results from the proposed research will aid in the
development of therapies to slow or prevent the loss of dopamine neurons and related motor symptoms in PD.

application_id: 11067406
activity_code: F31
title: Advancing Implementation of Single Session Interventions in Schools
org_name: NORTHWESTERN UNIVERSITY AT CHICAGO
phr: Project Narrative. Youth commonly seek help for their mental health concerns in schools, but many schools don’t have enough mental health providers to help everyone in need. Research studies show that digital, self-guided, single-session interventions can help reduce students’ mental health concerns, but we don’t know how to get schools to use these kinds of interventions in real life outside of research studies. In this study, we will work together with students, parents/caregivers, teachers, administrators, and school mental health providers to find out the best ways that digital, self-guided, single session interventions can be practically implemented in schools.

title: Activity dependent mechanisms of human interneuron development

phr: Project Narrative Despite the improvements in breast cancer survival, black women experience suboptimal quality of life, which is why it is important to evaluate their experiences to improve quality of life (QOL) and target healthy behaviors that foster a longer life among racial/ethnic minority breast cancer survivors. Physical activity (PA) is a modifiable behavior with the potential to help reduce racial disparities in QOL. This fellowship will help to establish the relationship between PA and QOL among black women, generate new knowledge and insights to enhance QOL among black women, cultivate community partnerships, and empower other researchers to increase the representation of racial/ethnic minorities in studies focusing on behavioral medicine to meet the needs of this community.

title: From Molecules to Behavior: Understanding How Aging Impacts Entorhinal-based Navigation
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: NARRATIVE As humans age, spatial memory gradually declines among healthy adults and those affected by neurological conditions like Alzheimer's disease, resulting in decreased individual independence and quality of life. To eventually develop therapeutics that prevent disease in and rejuvenate the aging brain, it is necessary to first understand the molecular, cellular, and circuit-level neurobiological mechanisms that drive cognitive decline. The proposed study will identify novel molecular drivers of aging in medial entorhinal cortex (MEC), a brain region critical for spatial memory, by correlating age-mediated gene expression changes in MEC neurons with differences in MEC neural activity among young and aged mice.

title: Perception of Dead Conspecifics modulates neural signaling and lifespan in Caenorhabditis elegans
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative Animals have evolved sophisticated sensory systems that allow them to sense and respond to environmental cues, optimizing their health and survival. application_id: 11377888
activity_code: K00
title: Using causal aging biomarkers and protein design to develop novel anti-aging interventions
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative In mammals, while DNA methylation levels at specific CpG sites correlate with age and have led to the development of epigenetic aging clocks, the causal relationship between methylation and aging-related phenotypes remains unclear. This proposal aims to refine these clocks by incorporating causality, using Mendelian Randomization to identify causal CpGs, and further harnessing protein language models in the K00 phase to understand and design proteins linked to longevity. The outcome could revolutionize aging research by distinguishing age-related damage from adaptation, leading to precise aging biomarkers and a new approach to treat aging through proteome redesign.

application_id: 11136550
activity_code: K00
title: Dissecting the Parabrachial Nucleus's Role in the Development and Maintenance of Neuropathic Pain
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Project Narrative Neuropathic pain is a debilitating form of chronic pain afflicting millions of patients worldwide but is poorly responsive to current analgesic therapies. It is hypothesized that increased excitatory/decreased inhibitory signaling in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord results in the initiation, maintenance, and progression of neuropathic pain. This study uses in vivo intraspinal optogenetics, conditional genetic knockout lines combined with behavioral pharmacology, and spinal cord slice electrophysiology to ultimately determine if peripheral nerve injury increases the excitability of excitatory dorsal horn interneurons (those that contain the type 1 receptor for neuropeptide Y) to contribute to the behavioral manifestations of neuropathic pain.

activity_code: K08
title: Harnessing the repetitive genome for cancer immunotherapy
org_name: DANA-FARBER CANCER INST
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Therapies that disrupt immune evasion through the PD-1 or CTLA-4 pathways have shown remarkable efficacy yet fail in many patients, likely due to additional mechanisms that are yet to be defined. This proposal will investigate how epigenetic silencing of the repetitive genome drives immune escape in cancer cells, and will specifically explore the role of the epigenetic repressor, SETDB 1, in silencing transposable elements and immune genes in melanoma. A deeper understanding of these mechanisms could ultimately yield new biomarkers and epigenetic therapies capable of selectively enhancing the efficacy and scope of immunotherapy for melanoma and other cancer types.

activity_code: R00
title: Coordination of Structure and Function in Corticospinal and Corticostriatal Pathways
org_name: NEW YORK UNIVERSITY
phr: The voluntary control of movement emerges from neuronal activity distributed across many motor controls structures – notably motor cortex, the basal ganglia, and spinal cord. We understand little of how these structures are coordinated, undermining our ability to treat associated movement disorders like Parkinson’s disease and dystonia. In this proposal I will test the idea that the logic of output of cortical projections to basal ganglia and spinal motor centers is coordinated through innervation of defined neuron types that provide cohesion in motor action.

activity_code: K01
title: Mechanisms of social identity, social networks, and recovery capital: Implicationsfor recovery
org_name: MASSACHUSETTS GENERAL HOSPITAL
phr: The career development plan includes training in alcohol-specific recovery processes and trajectories of change, social identity and social network change processes for recovery during adolescence, social network and mixed methods study design, and lab management and grantsmanship. The K01 study is a longitudinal observational study that will utilize a social identity mapping (SIM) approach to examine social network, social identity, and recovery capital change among adolescents with AUDs in outpatient treatment. The SIM will be adapted from existing adult implementation models to specifically address adolescent needs and a comprehensive longitudinal model of the effects of adolescents' social network, social identity, and recovery capital on substance use outcomes will be developed.

application_id: 10880660
activity_code: K23
title: Optimizing mobile behavioral economic interventions for rural risky drinkers
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Excessive alcohol use is the 3rd leading preventable cause of death in the U.S., and is associated with
significant economic burden and negative health-related consequences that are undertreated in rural regions.
This K23 involves evaluating two remotely-delivered behavioral economic intervention components using
remote text message delivery that will lead to the development of a well-specified, novel mobile health
intervention for rural risky drinkers. Given the high rates of mobile phone ownership, if the efficacy is
established, this intervention will have great promise for increasing access to alcohol intervention services for
rural individuals, thereby benefiting public health.

application_id: 10973695
activity_code: K00
title: Investigating Cognitive Control and Search in the Prefrontal-Hippocampal Network
org_name: BETH ISRAEL DEACONESS MEDICAL CENTER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
The brain has evolved the capacity to plan behavior multiple steps into the future by predicting the
consequences of alternative actions based on prior knowledge of the environment – a cognitive mechanism
known as multi-step planning. The proposed work will combine cellular-resolution calcium imaging and
chemogenetic inactivation with cutting-edge behavioral techniques to determine the role of the retrosplenial
cortex (RSC) in multi-step planning. The results from this work will advance our understanding of the neural
implementation of planning and inform future translational research on the disruption of action evaluation,
prospection, and goal-directed behavior in many psychiatric disorders.

application_id: 11380413
activity_code: K00
title: Aging-related Changes in the Neural Mechanisms Underlying Speech Motor Learning
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN FRANCISCO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Millions of adults above 65 years of age report communication challenges, including age-related declines in speech motor control and motor speech problems caused by neurodegenerative conditions such as Parkinson’s disease. Given that efforts to develop effective treatments are hindered by a lack of data on how aging affects speech motor learning and control, this project investigates in patients with deep brain stimulation implants and in typically aging individuals which neural circuits and sensorimotor processes involved in speech production are affected by the aging process. The findings will inform effective motor learning-based behavioral and neuromodulation approaches to prevent and ameliorate aging-related speech motor difficulties.

application_id: 10794906
activity_code: K00
title: Dynamic functional connectivity of the default mode network in youth with ADHD
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: Project Narrative The study proposed in this F99/K00 application investigates the relationship between Heart Rate Variability (a measure of autonomic nervous system function) and brain states in healthy, moderate to heavy daily alcohol drinkers following periods of typical consumption and abstinence. To date, the study design has been completed and all data collected, with preliminary analyses run. The F99 phase of the project will involve the remaining analyses investigating connectivity changes with and outside of the Default Mode Network across drinking states, while future directions of the project, to be conducted during a postdoctoral fellowship and the K00 phase of this fellowship, will include more advanced dynamic assessments of brain states using functional networks.

application_id: 10973711
activity_code: K00
title: Characterizing individual differences in toddlers' information seeking during naturalistic play
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: Project Narrative
 Visual attention – “gaze” – is a promising biomarker for autism: it can be passively measured from non-
and pre-verbal individuals, thus facilitating clinical diagnosis before hallmark behavioral traits (e.g., language
delay) have fully emerged in development. A key step in leveraging gaze as a clinical tool will be to understand
the mental processes underlying autistic group differences in visual attention. The project proposed here directly
relates to the NIH BRAIN priority to understand foundational mental processes using new theoretical and data
analysis tools.

activity_code: R25
title: Summer Research in Environmental Health Sciences
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF KENTUCKY
phr: NARRATIVE
The overall objective of the Summer Undergraduate Research Experience in Environmental
Health Sciences (SURES) program is to use a research-intensive approach for introducing
students from the impoverished region of Appalachia to the multidisciplinary nature of
environmental sciences and opportunities for pursuing relevant careers. Exceptional training in
a wide variety of research areas working with interdisciplinary collaborative research groups
who are housed in multiple departments and colleges is provided. In addition, seminars,
outreach opportunities and interactive workshops are designed to prepare students for
successful, environmental health related careers.

---
application_id: 11237084
activity_code: R25
title: Molecular and Environmental Toxicology Summer Research Opportunities Program (MET-SROP)
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: Project Narrative
This application is to support a summer research training experience for undergraduate students who are
underrepresented in the environmental health sciences. Underrepresented includes students from diverse
racial or ethnic backgrounds, economically disadvantaged communities, that are disabled, or are first in their
family to attend college. Goals of this program are to increase the diversity of the Environmental Health
Sciences workforce through hands on laboratory and training in foundations of toxicology, research and career
development.

application_id: 10848384
activity_code: T32
title: Redox Biology and Medicine Training Program
org_name: WAKE FOREST UNIVERSITY HEALTH SCIENCES
phr: Narrative Redox biology and medicine, the theme of this predoctoral training program at Wake Forest University for which funding is requested, denotes the specialized chemistry of oxidation and reduction occurring in biological systems which orchestrates metabolism and signaling to elicit specific cellular responses. Redox mechanisms support well-being and are also involved in the development and progression of many diseases. The goal of this training program is to bring scientists from diverse backgrounds and diverse programs such as chemistry, physics and molecular medicine together to train in the performance of interdisciplinary redox research to improve human health.
terms: Biology;Medicine;Oxidation-Reduction;Training Programs

org_name: STATE UNIVERSITY OF NEW YORK AT BUFFALO
phr: The proposed research will develop a non-invasive, challenge-free, widely available imaging method to map cerebral oxygen extraction fraction (OEF), a direct biomarker for tissue viability and essential for patient care strategies. application_id: 10894244
activity_code: K23
title: Support to Adopt and Retain Technology (StART) CGM: A pilot study to support continuous glucose monitoring in primary care
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) improves glycemic control, reduces hypoglycemia and facilitates personalized care in insulin-requiring type 2 diabetes (T2D) but are vastly underutilized. Multilevel interventions are increasingly recognized as a promising strategy for addressing complex healthcare problems, such as incorporating CGM devices into routine diabetes care. This proposal aims to apply Implementation Science to develop and test an innovative, multilevel intervention to support adoption and retention of CGM for insulin- requiring T2D patients in primary care.

application_id: 11067320
activity_code: F31
title: Functional characterizations of the genetic etiology of stuttering
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Developmental stuttering affects 5% of children and 1% of adults worldwide, and exhibits a sibling relative risk of 70%; however, despite this high population prevalence and remarkably high heritability, understanding of the molecular and neurological function of genes impacting stuttering risk is lacking. The present application proposes to enhance our understanding of how common and rare genetic factors contribute to stuttering by using information about how genes are transcribed and how gene transcription affects brain features. This study represents the largest and most comprehensive functional genetic analysis of stuttering ever performed and the results obtained in this study will be critical to improving basic understanding of the mechanisms that contribute to risk...

application_id: 10848399
activity_code: F31
title: Ischemic Stroke in Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy: Microvascular Injury and Recovery
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI SCHOOL OF MEDICINE
phr: Project Narrative The proposed research will study the mutual effect of amyloid-related brain injury and ischemic stroke on cerebrovascular recovery. Since dementia outcomes are a national health crisis, understanding the effects of elevated Aβ on vascular and neuronal dysfunction will provide insight into their synergistic effects on post-stroke recovery and cognitive decline. Elucidating the mechanism that drives dysfunction of the blood-brain barrier and neural progenitor cells may help us develop therapeutic strategies to reduce the burden of neurological disease in CAA, stroke, and even clinical Alzheimer’s Disease.

application_id: 10702052
activity_code: K00
title: Tracking the dynamics of how schemas scaffold recall
org_name: JOHNS HOPKINS UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative In my dissertation, I will use newly-developed machine learning techniques to study how the brain uses prior knowledge (about the spatial structure of an environment, or how certain types of events unfold in time) to scaffold new learning. By precisely characterizing how this scaffolding process works, my research will help to identify ways in which prior knowledge can be more optimally leveraged to support learning. This will lead to the development of tools to help memory-impaired individuals make better use of intact prior knowledge to support new learning, as well as remedies for groups where deficiencies in prior knowledge prevent them from learning properly.

The University of Texas Medical Branch Summer Institute in Biostatistics and Data Science will

activity_code: R25
title: University of Minnesota Summer Research in Neuroscience
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MINNESOTA
phr: PUBLIC HEALTH STATEMENT
A major health challenge facing our society is a pervasive range of neurological disorders,
which becomes more acute as our population ages. The goal of this project is to continue
support of a summer residential research program at the University of Minnesota with the goal
of inspiring new generations of bright, talented undergraduate students to enter neuroscience
research as a career. The hope is that these future scientists will contribute to the health
infrastructure by finding cures for these common, but devastating diseases of the nervous
system.

application_id: 10894282
activity_code: K08
title: Structural Intervention to Promote HIV Care in Black Sexual Minority Men
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The proposed study focuses on using the ADAPT-ITT framework to adapt material from existing career readiness interventions (Work2Prevent, tailored to young SMM and trans women of color at-risk for HIV infection, and iFOUR, tailored to older people living with HIV, regardless of race) to the needs of young Black SMM (YBSMM), ages 18-29, living with HIV. activity_code: DP2
title: Dissecting the impact of immune environment on Candida albicans pathogenic potential in the gut
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF COLORADO DENVER
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Candida albicans is a commensal fungus that colonizes the intestine of most healthy people but has the capacity to cause infections and exacerbate inflammation in susceptible people. Immune status determines risk for C. albicans-associated disease, but the impact of immune environment on C. albicans inherent pathogenic potential is not well understood. This proposal aims to comprehensively define the impact both homeostatic and pathogenic immune environments on C. albicans pathogenic potential.

phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Alcohol use disorder (AUD) is a debilitating condition with poor long-term treatment outcomes, despite effective treatments for initial recovery. application_id: 10894369
activity_code: F31
title: The impact of Wnt signaling on hematopoietic stem cell aging and its influence on fracture repair
org_name: DUKE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative Precise regulation of the Wnt signaling pathway is necessary to maintain hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) maintenance and quiescence. After a fracture event, the bone marrow environment, where the HSC niche resides, is then disordered and recruits various inflammatory cytokines as part of the healing process. Injury and age can lead to a dysregulation of Wnt signaling which this proposal hypothesizes alters HSC localization and differentiation, affecting the quality of fracture repair, and thus can be an unexplored target to treat age-related fracture complications.

application_id: 10906061
activity_code: K00
title: The scope, cause and consequences of age-related decline in neural distinctiveness and brain signal variability in healthy and pathological aging
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project Narrative Some people age significantly more gracefully than others, and understanding the neural factors that underlie those individual differences could lead to interventions to slow or even stop age-related behavioral impairments. The objective of this project is to investigate two neural factors that may play a role, namely age-related changes in the distinctiveness of neural representations and age-related changes in the variability of neural signals. This research could lead to the development of better preclinical markers of Alzheimer’s disease and open avenues for pharmaceutical interventions to slow cognitive declines in aging.

application_id: 11070644
activity_code: F31
title: Acoustic Measures in Diagnosis and Subtyping of Childhood Apraxia of Speech
org_name: TEMPLE UNIV OF THE COMMONWEALTH
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The differential diagnosis of childhood apraxia of speech (CAS), a pediatric speech disorder that affects between 60,000 and 780,000 children under 14 in the US, remains difficult in clinical and research settings. This difficulty is due in large part to the reliance on diagnostic techniques that can be subjective and prone to bias, resulting in inconsistent diagnostic and treatment outcomes for children in this population. This project systematically investigates the validity and potential differential diagnostic accuracy of a well-motivated set of objective acoustic measures in a large retrospective sample of children with and without CAS to help improve accurate diagnosis and thereby support more effective treatment decisions and outcomes for children with CAS.

application_id: 11010116
activity_code: K00
title: Elucidating the role of endocrine aging as a risk factor for Alzheimer's Disease
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative Women are disproportionately affected by Alzheimer’s disease (AD), yet the degree to which female reproductive aging shapes the structural and functional architecture of the human brain is severely understudied. During the menopausal transition, ovarian production of estradiol and progesterone declines by up to 90%, and these sex steroid hormones play neuroprotective roles in the brain to support the structure and function of regions vulnerable to neurodegeneration. The proposed studies will use state-of-the-art brain imaging techniques to establish the endocrine basis of neural aging in midlife women and provide critical insight into whether the brain demonstrates vulnerability to AD pathology as a function of the menopausal transition.

title: Community-engaged research partnerships for building capacity and training in inclusive data science
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This project builds capacity in inclusive data science for K-12 students and programs that support STEM
(Science, Technology, Education, and Mathematics). Middle and high school students engage in research and
interprofessional training that aligns qualitative research, data science, and applied ethics for enhancing
accurate data collection and authentic representation of the biomedical workforce. This project applies iterative
data feedback loops with collaborative partners to characterize responsible practices for collection and use of
inclusive demographic data, which has the power to identify key demographic groups facing barriers to
participation and retention in STEM programs needed to broaden and diversify the biomedical workforce.

title: Early Childhood Deprivation and Psychopathology: Identifying Neurodevelopmental Pathways of Risk
org_name: UNIV OF NORTH CAROLINA CHAPEL HILL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE This project aims to characterize neurodevelopmental mechanisms underlying the association of early childhood adverse experiences involving deprivation with pediatric psychopathology. The proposed research will improve understanding of neurodevelopmental risk for adverse mental health outcomes by identifying pathways of frontoparietal network development linking deprivation with psychopathology first in early childhood and then longitudinally from early childhood through adolescence. Findings will have implications for population health by identifying pathways of transdiagnostic psychopathology risk that can inform future early intervention efforts for childhood adversity, which impacts nearly 50% of children and adolescents in the United States.

title: Neuroscience Training Program
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WISCONSIN-MADISON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The Neuroscience Training Program (NTP) at the University of Wisconsin–Madison provides integrated predoctoral neuroscience training, equipping students to carry out research aimed at understanding fundamental neurobiological processes and mechanisms underlying diseases and disorders of the nervous system. Our goal is to prepare our students to lead the next generation of scientists with independent careers in academia, healthcare, teaching, industry, and public service.

application_id: 11038488
activity_code: R36
title: After the Dust Settles: Changes in Local Healthcare Access and Utilization after Civil Unrest
org_name: BROWN UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative  This proposal is relevant to long-term services and supports because civil unrest could have a substantial impact on the way Medicare beneficiaries utilize healthcare in the long term. Examining a relationship between incidents of civil unrest and healthcare access and utilization could impact the overall health of marginalized communities because understanding changes to utilization and access, such as civil unrest, could inform programming and craft policies that improve the health and well-being of vulnerable populations. The proposal aligns with the National Institute of Aging’s mission of conducting research to extend healthy, active years of living by assessing changes in healthcare access and utilization.

application_id: 10848549
activity_code: T32
title: Training Program in Brain Tumor Biology
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA AT BIRMINGHAM
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE The multidisciplinary University of Alabama at Birmingham Training Program in Brain Tumor Biology is focused on preparing highly motivated pre-doctoral and MD/PhD students for a career in fundamental and translational brain tumor research. Trainees are therefore selected for their commitment to neuro-oncology research and excellent performance in the first two years of the UAB Graduate Biomedical Sciences Program. Our dedicated and collaborative training environment provides the ideal setting for implementation of an educational program in brain tumor research.

application_id: 11038110
activity_code: R36
title: The role of networks and serious illness in Medicare Advantage disenrollment
org_name: VANDERBILT UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative  We use novel data on Medicare Advantage (MA) networks to investigate skilled nursing facility network breadth and to study whether oncology network breadth is associated with plan disenrollment. We also examine whether measures that allow beneficiaries more time to opt out of their plans led to greater Medicare Advantage disenrollment among patients with cancer. Taken together this work will investigate whether the MA program fully meets the needs of patients with serious illnesses, especially in terms of access to key services.

application_id: 10848553
activity_code: T32
title: Comprehensive Musculoskeletal Training Program
org_name: INDIANA UNIVERSITY INDIANAPOLIS
phr: We propose to renew our Comprehensive Musculoskelelal Training Program , which has been highly successful in training the next generation of musculoskelelal biologists, physicians, and engineers for research careers in MSK. We will offer trainee positons from three to four for each group: predoctoral students, postdoctoral fellows, and summer medical student interns. The training program will integrate current musculoskeletal research and teaching programs in the basic, clinical, and engineering sciences through the Indiana Center for Musculoskeletal Health, and train young scientists for research careers as basic scientists, bioengineers, clinical and translational investigators pursuing the causes of, and solutions to, musculoskelelal disease.

application_id: 11187584
activity_code: R36
title: Nothing Micro About It: Investigating the Role of miRNAs and Cannabis Use in Cardiac Dysfunction among People Living with HIV 
org_name: FLORIDA INTERNATIONAL UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Substance use is one of the most modifiable risk factors for cardiac dysfunction in people with HIV (PWH).
However, among PWH, the relationship between objective measures of cannabis and quantification of
cannabinoids (e.g., CBD and THC) with cardiac dysfunction is limited. This dissertation research proposal will
address this gap by examining the potentially divergent associations of CBD and THC metabolite levels with
miRNA profiles and cardiac dysfunction in PWH.

application_id: 11178644
activity_code: R36
title: Determinants of transitioning from injecting to smoking fentanyl and methamphetamine and implications for harm reduction implementation
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, SAN DIEGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
This R36 dissertation study examines transitions from injecting to smoking drugs (predominantly
fentanyl and methamphetamine) among people who inject drugs (PWID) in the San Diego-
Tijuana border region. Such transitions may help reduce infectious disease (e.g., HIV, HCV) and
overdose morbidity and mortality if sufficient quantities of safer smoking supplies are accessible
(e.g., through syringe service programs; SSPs). By examining the multilevel (e.g., individual,
organizational, structural) factors driving these transitions in drug consumption patterns and
related implementation considerations for distributing safer smoking supplies through SSPs, this
project will inform efforts to improve the delivery of prevention services and reduce the high
burden of infectious dis...

application_id: 11182343
activity_code: R36
title: Identification of opioid-induced innate immune dysregulation among people living with HIV
org_name: OREGON HEALTH & SCIENCE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
As the rate of opioid use disorders continues to rise in the United States, it has become important to understand
how chronic opioid exposure alters the immune system, particularly among people-living-with-HIV who already
experience compromised immunity and diseases driven by chronic inflammation. This research will determine if
chronic opioid exposure disrupts monocyte immunometabolism and function among people-living-with-HIV who
also suffer from opioid-use-disorder. Findings from this proposal will reveal the off-target effects of long-term
opioid-exposure on innate immunity and open the door for therapeutics that ameliorate opioid-induced monocyte
dysregulation.

application_id: 10895407
activity_code: K01
title: Characterization of the Role of Nemo-like Kinase in Normal and Diamond Blackfan Anemia Models of Erythropoiesis.
org_name: STANFORD UNIVERSITY
phr: Narrative Diamond Blackfan Anemia (DBA) is characterized by defective erythropoiesis. Erythroid progenitors from murine and human models of DBA have chronic hyper-activation of Nemo-Like Kinase (NLK), while suppression of NLK improves erythroid expansion in vitro. Understanding the mechanisms by which NLK contributes to disease pathogenesis will potentially lead to novel approaches to treat patients with DBA and other bone marrow failure syndromes.

title: Patient Perspectives, Follow-up Patterns, and Cost-Effectiveness of a Low-Threshold Mobile Medical Unit Providing Opioid Use Disorder Care in an Urban Setting
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS AT CHICAGO
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE
Significant and deeply rooted barriers to access prevent thousands of individuals from obtaining chronic
treatment for opioid use disorder. A low-threshold mobile medical care model that dispenses medications to
treat opioid use disorder addresses these access barriers by engaging individuals in treatment outside of a
standard clinic setting. While the low-threshold mobile medical unit model has successfully engaged
traditionally hard-to-reach individuals, this research examines the patients’ opinion, trends of engagement, and
economic value of addressing disparate access to opioid use disorder treatment with the low-threshold mobile
medical care model, to justify continued funding and inform implementation and expansion decision making.

application_id: 11234260
activity_code: F32
title: Examining Iconicity with EEG: The role of Motoric Iconicity and Embodied Cognition in American Sign Language
org_name: GALLAUDET UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
Caregivers of deaf/hard-of-hearing (DHH) may want to provide sign exposure to protect their
child from linguistic deprivation but have to learn how to sign in order to do so. Adults have
developed their embodied cognition skills – the ability to use stored sensory-motoric
experiences during tasks – and may be able to capitalize on these skills when learning to sign,
particularly for signs that directly access these stored representations. This proposal aims to
assess the impact of motoric-iconicity–the structured mapping between a sign’s form
(shape/movement) and its meaning–on embodied cognition in native signers and adult sign
learners to determine if these signs can serve as a starting point for caregiver-centric
interventions.

application_id: 10686470
activity_code: DP2
title: Molecular determinants of host-feeding manipulation and microbial colonization
org_name: YALE UNIVERSITY
phr: Project Narrative
The gut microbiome influences many aspects of human and animal health, including metabolism, physiology,
hunger and in some cases even feeding behavior, but studying this process in humans or other mammals
presents substantial challenges. Here we propose to develop tools and technology to study feeding
manipulation by gut microbes in the highly tractable invertebrate system of C. elegans, in order to gain a
comprehensive molecular understanding of this process for the first time in an animal system. We expect these
aims will yield generalizable molecular insights into microbial-nervous system communication in diverse
animals.

application_id: 10686498
activity_code: DP2
title: Enzymatic and genetic strategies for targeting disease-associated microbial metabolites
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN AT ANN ARBOR
phr: Project Narrative
The majority of studies in establishing causal associations between microbial metabolites and host processes
center around manipulating individual bacterial strains' metabolite production and investigating the subsequent
impact on the host physiology. This strategy has limitations in that certain metabolites can be derived from
multiple microbial species resulting from conserved metabolic pathways. Here, we will develop two
complementary and highly integrated methodologies to achieve direct in situ perturbation of microbial
metabolites, which will pave the way for both fundamental and applied studies relevant to metabolite-mediated
microbe-host interactions.

This proposal aims to provide resources that train young investigators at the MD or PhD level who will play a key role in extending our understanding of basic metabolic processes at the subcellular, cellular, organ and system level. Moreover, translating these new insights into both mechanistic and translational advances for disorders related to metabolic dysfunction.

application_id: 11178909
activity_code: R36
title: Development of N-acyl fluspirilene mediated proteasome enhancement for the treatment of neurodegenerative diseases
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF VIRGINIA
phr: Neurodegenerative diseases, including Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and Alzheimer’s disease related dementias (ADRD) are characterized by accumulation of intrinsically disordered proteins (IDPs) and impairment of proteasome-mediated protein degradation. This R36 proposal aims to investigate small molecule 20S proteasome activation as a novel strategy to resolve both pathogenic events. Successful completion of this work will enable the rapid design, development, and identification of 20S proteasome activators, and elucidate the role of 20S proteasome modulation in neurodegenerative diseases as a novel therapeutic strategy for disease treatment.

title: The role of antigen-independent T cell activation in controlling M. tuberculosis infection
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF WASHINGTON
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Our lack of understanding of the mechanisms of T cell mediated protection against Tuberculosis, a disease responsible for 1.6 million deaths in 2021, has hampered our ability to develop new vaccine strategies. activity_code: F31
title: Differentiation-focused CRISPR Screen identifies LSD1 and Menin as combination therapy targets that induce terminal differentiation in AML
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF PENNSYLVANIA
phr: Project Narrative  Differentiation therapy is curative in one subtype of Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) and underexplored in the rest. We conducted gain-of-differentiation screens in AML models and identified that combined inhibition of LSD1 and MEN1 synergizes to induce terminal, potentially curative, differentiation in many AMLs. Validating the therapeutic efficacy of these pre-clinical differentiation therapy targets and determining the mechanisms of inhibitor-induced differentiation will change the principles of AML treatment strategies.

application_id: 11055546
activity_code: K00
title: The neural foundations of regulation in infancy and the role of individual and environmental factors over time
org_name: WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE Very preterm (VPT) children are 2-4 times more likely than term control (TC) children to be diagnosed with a psychiatric disorder, including anxiety, ADHD, and socioemotional communicative problems. Childhood internalizing symptoms are noted developmental precursors to later psychiatric risk, yet the literature is missing a developmental account of how VPT compared to TC children progress along internalizing trajectories. Our proposed research incorporates a developmental systems approach to examine how internalizing symptoms change over time from birth to age 10, and how neural and environmental factors contribute to these trajectories.

application_id: 10895964
activity_code: F31
title: Determination of structure-function relationships and role in virulence of a MerR-type regulator that mediates zinc tolerance in Streptococcus mutans
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA
phr: Narrative Dental caries is one of the most prevalent chronic diseases in the world and the oral bacterium Streptococcus mutans is the major etiological agent of this multifactorial disease. We recently discovered that S. mutans tolerates unusually high concentrations of zinc by utilizing the ZccE exporter and ZccR regulator pair. Because zinc can be safely delivered in high concentrations via oral healthcare products, a better understanding of the importance of ZccR to dental caries and determination of ZccR structure can help us devise new anti-caries therapies by specifically targeting S. mutans without damaging either host cells or disturbing the bacteria associated with oral health.

application_id: 10848428
activity_code: K23
title: Epinephrine in the Pediatric Intensive Care Unit: A Dose-Effect Trial
org_name: BOSTON CHILDREN'S HOSPITAL
phr: Pediatric in-hospital cardiac arrest is associated with extremely high morbidity and mortality and is estimated to affect at least 6,000 children per year. In an effort to prevent cardiac arrest in the pediatric intensive care unit, physicians have adopted the practice of administering small doses of epinephrine to reverse acute hypotension; however, little is known about the efficacy, safety or optimal dosing associated with this practice. To better characterize the use of peri-arrest epinephrine, we propose a prospective, randomized, dose-effect trial in patients with acute profound hypotension in the pediatric intensive care unit which may ultimately lead to saving lives and decreasing disability in pediatric patients.

application_id: 11052608
activity_code: R36
title: Functional and molecular organization of lateral septum in threat processing
org_name: HARVARD MEDICAL SCHOOL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE To avoid potential harm, we are equipped with brain circuits that allow detecting, learning, and responding to perceived threats in our environment. Dysregulation in these processes often denotes psychopathological conditions such as post-traumatic stress and anxiety disorders. This study examines how specific neuronal populations process perceived threats to guide behavioral defenses, thus providing novel insights into the neural mechanisms underlying threat processing in both health and disease.

title: Understanding the role of schools in supporting HIV treatment outcomes among HIV infected adolescents
org_name: KENYATTA  NATIONAL  HOSPITAL
phr: PROJECT NARRATIVE In 2017, an estimated 101,000 Kenyan adolescents aged 10-19 were living with HIV, the majority of whom are enrolled in school. title: Integrative approaches to dissection of endocrine communication
org_name: UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA-IRVINE
phr: Project Narrative  Endocrine interactions have been demonstrated as causal and contributory to most common, most notably obesity, type 2 diabetes and resulting cardiovascular complications.
